title: Getting started with actiRhythm
text: circadian.rhythm() describes rest-activity pattern without assuming waveform. returns interdaily stability () intradaily variability (IV), introduced Witting et al. (1990), together relative amplitude (RA) least- -active windows L5 M10 Van Someren et al. (1999), onset time. Read three headline numbers together. ranges 0 1 rises daily pattern repeats exactly day day; IV runs 0 (smooth sine) 2 (noisy split ultradian bouts); RA ranges 0 1 grows contrast active day restful night. high RA (0.98) sits low (0.23): strong day-night contrast carried irregular timing. header also notes six eight calendar days qualified, circadian.rhythm() sets aside days little valid recording computing metrics.
code: rhythm <- circadian.rhythm(agd$axis1, agd$timestamp) rhythm #>  #> Circadian Rhythm Analysis #>  #> Data Summary #>   Days analyzed:            8 #>   Valid circadian days:     6 #>   Epoch length:             60 seconds #>  #> Non-Parametric Metrics (IS/IV: Witting et al. 1990; RA/L5/M10: van Someren et al. 1999) #>   L5 (least active 5h):     6.10 counts/min, onset 01:28 #>   M10 (most active 10h):    602.93 counts/min, onset 12:02 #>   L1 (least active 1h):     3.10 counts/min, onset 02:35 #>   M1 (most active 1h):      1175.51 counts/min, onset 17:43 #>   Relative Amplitude (RA):  0.9800 (range 0-1, higher=stronger rhythm) #>   Interdaily Stability (IS): 0.2279 (range 0-1, higher=more consistent) #>   Intradaily Variability (IV): 1.0008 (near 0 = sine, near 2 = noise) #>   Phi (autocorrelation):    0.4151 (higher=more predictable) #>  #> Sleep-Based & Variability Metrics #>   Sleep Regularity Index:   Not calculated (requires sleep_state input) #>   Onset timing variability: 2.23 hours #>   L5 timing variability:    0.77 hours (circular SD) #>   M10 timing variability:   3.70 hours (circular SD) #>  #> References: Witting (1990), van Someren (1999)

title: Getting started with actiRhythm
text: cosinor.analysis() fits 24-hour cosine returns MESOR (rhythm-adjusted mean), amplitude, acrophase, clock time peak (Cornelissen, 2014). acrophase circular: clock time, 23.5 0.5 hour apart, 23 hours apart, interpretable amplitude distinguishable zero. rhythmicity.test() check, zero-amplitude F-test Nelson et al. (1979), reported percent variance rhythm explains. small p-value rejects flat-line null. test clearly significant (p well 0.05) single cosine explains little forty percent variance, acrophase worth reading. percent-rhythm also reminds rest variance, half, mix noise structure single cosine capture. Overlay fit see catching. Hourly activity fitted 24-hour cosine overlaid; peak curve acrophase height MESOR line amplitude. Systematic departures curve single cosine smooth real rest-activity data. Look fitted curve hourly points part company: single cosine runs smooth early hours, gap structure percent-rhythm says one cosine leaves table. need joint uncertainty amplitude acrophase together, cosinor.confidence.ellipse(cos) returns Bingham et al. (1982) ellipse; ellipse encloses origin geometric form “detectable rhythm”.
code: cos <- cosinor.analysis(agd$axis1, agd$timestamp, period = 24) c(mesor = unname(cos$mesor), amplitude = unname(cos$amplitude),   acrophase = unname(cos$acrophase)) #>     mesor amplitude acrophase  #>    327.25    295.63     16.92 rhythmicity.test(agd$axis1, agd$timestamp, cosinor_result = cos) #> Cosinor Rhythmicity Test (Halberg zero-amplitude F-test) #>  #> H0: amplitude = 0 (no rhythm) #>   Period:         24 h #>   F(2, 21):        8.15 #>   P-value:        0.0024 #>   Percent rhythm: 43.7% (R-squared = 0.4371) #>   Rhythmic:       YES (alpha = 0.05) plot_extended_cosinor(agd$axis1, agd$timestamp)

title: Getting started with actiRhythm
text: cosinor assumes exactly 24 hours. circadian.period() : runs Lomb-Scargle periodogram 18 30 hour band returns dominant period tau, false-alarm probability $p_value (Baluev, 2008; Lomb, 1976; Scargle, 1982). strongest cycle sits near 25.4 hours, meaningfully longer solar day, false-alarm probability essentially zero, cycle doubt. exact length less certain. period.ci() refines peak bootstraps circular block bootstrap (Kunsch, 1989; Politis & Romano, 1992), respects autocorrelation activity data. interval returns wide enough span 24 hours. single recording period clearly present pinned hour. Passing seed = 1 makes bootstrap reproducible; 50 replicates keep vignette fast, real interval use . Lomb-Scargle spectral power across candidate periods; tallest peak dominant rhythm, peak rising significance line rejects -rhythm null. chi-square periodogram Sokolove & Bushell (1978) asks question different way, scoring trial period much variance folds onto . second estimator clears threshold period reassurance peak real rather artifact one method. Chi-square (Sokolove-Bushell) periodogram: Qp statistic trial period, significance threshold. peak fall period Lomb-Scargle periodogram found.
code: per <- circadian.period(agd$axis1, agd$timestamp) c(tau = per$tau, p_value = per$p_value) #>          tau      p_value  #> 2.543077e+01 9.388299e-97 period.ci(agd$axis1, agd$timestamp, n_boot = 50, seed = 1) #> Circadian Period with Bootstrap Confidence Interval #>  #>   tau:      24.980 h #>   95% CI:   [23.484, 26.342] h #>   SE:       1.220 h #>   Method:   circular block residual bootstrap (50/50 valid reps) plot_periodogram(agd$axis1, agd$timestamp) plot_chisq(agd$axis1, agd$timestamp)

title: Getting started with actiRhythm
text: single periodogram averages whole recording. circadian.spectrogram() slides window across recomputes periodogram , can see dominant period move, chronobiology equivalent music spectrogram. Period (vertical) time (horizontal), coloured spectral power. horizontal band colour stable period; band bends upward downward rhythm lengthening shortening across recording. Follow band colour left right: drifts upward, dominant period lengthening across recording rather holding 24 hours. Read later windows caution, since ride upper edge search band, weakening rhythm genuinely longer period can look alike.
code: circadian.spectrogram(agd$axis1, agd$timestamp, step_hours = 24)$plot

title: Getting started with actiRhythm
text: Two recordings can share amplitude yet differ moment--moment dynamics. fractal.dfa() estimates detrended-fluctuation exponent alpha, long-range temporal correlation series (Hu et al., 2001; Peng et al., 1994). alpha near 1 “1/f” scaling typical healthy physiological output; near 0.5 uncorrelated noise; well 1 smoother, random-walk-like signal. alpha comes near 0.93, close 1/f end, series carries genuine long-range correlation rather looking like noise. log-log fit shows whether one exponent holds across time scales. Detrended fluctuation log-log axes; slope DFA exponent alpha. One straight line monofractal scaling, visible kink crossover short- long-range dynamics. Two questions use series single call . multiscale.entropy() asks complexity signal changes coarse-grain across time scales (Costa et al., 2002), mfdfa() asks whether one scaling exponent enough series genuinely multifractal (Kantelhardt et al., 2002). help pages carry math, Beyond basics article runs recording; enough know refine, replace, single alpha .
code: fractal.dfa(agd$axis1)$alpha #> [1] 0.9263546 plot_dfa(agd$axis1)

title: Getting started with actiRhythm
text: state.transitions() summarises readily subject switches rest activity, kRA (rest--active) kAR (active--rest) rates capture fragmentation single amplitude (Lim et al., 2011). subject kAR runs well kRA: active settle back rest quickly, rest stay . asymmetry fragmentation single amplitude see.
code: st <- state.transitions(agd$axis1) c(kRA = st$kRA, kAR = st$kAR) #>        kRA        kAR  #> 0.03920806 0.10681627

title: Getting started with actiRhythm
text: state.transitions() gives one pair rates whole recording. sleep.changepoints() instead locates sleep-onset wake-onset night directly counts, scored sleep required: 24-hour cosinor bounds rest active span roughly, change point inside bound places precise transition (Chen & Sun, 2024). detected sleep onset (blue) wake onset (orange) date clock time. detector reads per-night sleep wake timing directly counts. recording detector finds seven nights, sleep onset late evening wake onset around seven morning, nights near eight nine hours rest. per-night sleep wake timing single transition rate localise.
code: cp <- sleep.changepoints(agd$axis1, agd$timestamp) cp #> Change-Point Sleep/Wake Detection (CircaCP) #>  #>   Span:           6.9 days (9919 epochs) #>   Cosinor acrophase: 20.5 h #>   Change points:  14 (7 sleep episodes) #>   Mean sleep duration: 11.1 h #>  #>   First sleep episodes: #>     sleep 10-07 22:36  ->  wake 10-08 07:09  (8.6 h) #>     sleep 10-08 23:30  ->  wake 10-09 07:50  (8.3 h) #>     sleep 10-09 21:30  ->  wake 10-10 12:11  (14.7 h) #>  #>   Reference: Chen and Sun (2024) cps <- cp$changepoints cps$clock <- as.numeric(format(cps$time, "%H")) + as.numeric(format(cps$time, "%M")) / 60 ggplot(cps, aes(time, clock, colour = type)) +   geom_point(size = 3) +   scale_colour_manual(values = c("sleep onset" = "#236192", "wake onset" = "#E69F00")) +   labs(x = "Date", y = "Clock hour", colour = NULL) +   theme_actiRhythm()

title: Getting started with actiRhythm
text: sleep.changepoints() finds one main rest bout cycle. rest.periods() takes complementary view, consolidating every spell low activity rest bout, however many day holds, daytime naps fragmented rest included (Roenneberg et al., 2015). epoch compared fraction 24-hour activity level, runs stay grow consolidated bouts. consolidated rest bout bar onset offset. Detecting one bout day, including naps, separates one-per-cycle change-point detector. recording consolidates nine rest bouts across six days, one day, days carry second rest beyond main night. rest.crespo() reaches comparable result different route, rank-order filtering binary morphology rather seed--grow consolidation (Crespo et al., 2012); running gives independent cross-check recording’s rest bouts fall. Neither applies wear-time filter, gate counts valid wear first recording device-stretches.
code: rp <- rest.periods(agd$axis1, agd$timestamp) rp #> Consolidated Rest Periods (Roenneberg/MASDA) #>  #>   Bouts detected:   9  (1.5 per day over 6 days) #>   Total rest:       61.3 h #>   Main bouts / naps: 9 / 0 #>  #>   First bouts: #>     main 10-08 00:49 -> 10-08 07:09  (381 min) #>     main 10-08 23:31 -> 10-09 07:50  (500 min) #>     main 10-10 00:12 -> 10-10 06:57  (406 min) #>  #>   Reference: Roenneberg et al. (2015); Loock et al. (2021) ggplot(rp$rest_periods, aes(onset, bout, xend = offset, yend = bout, colour = type)) +   geom_segment(linewidth = 3) +   scale_colour_manual(values = c(main = "#236192", nap = "#E69F00")) +   labs(x = "Time", y = "Rest bout", colour = NULL) +   theme_actiRhythm()

title: Getting started with actiRhythm
text: metrics far describe rhythm; next need per-epoch sleep wake label. sleep.cole.kripke() produces label directly counts, standard count-based classifier adults (Cole et al., 1992), sleep.sadeh() children--adolescents alternative. Count-based scoring labels every low-activity epoch sleep, mostly sedentary recording scores high sleep fraction; matters downstream regular label day day. sleep.regularity.index() measures exactly , probability two epochs 24 hours apart share state (Phillips et al., 2017). also explicit sleep periods, social.jet.lag() returns work-day free-day mid-sleep difference (Wittmann et al., 2006) lids() locomotor inactivity sleep tracks ultradian sleep structure (Winnebeck et al., 2018).
code: state <- sleep.cole.kripke(agd$axis1) table(state) #> state #>    S    W  #> 7646 2273 sleep.regularity.index(state, agd$timestamp) #> [1] 58.46

title: Getting started with actiRhythm
text: test answers “rhythm?” language. consensus.rhythmicity() runs four , cosinor F-test, Bingham ellipse, Lomb-Scargle false-alarm probability, chi-square periodogram, reports majority vote Fisher-combined p-value (Fisher, 1925). recording three four tests agree rhythm present Fisher-combined p-value effectively zero, evidence leans firmly toward real rhythm even one method might hesitate. Read combined p-value indicative rather exact, though: four tests run series, independent, assumption Fisher’s method rests .
code: consensus.rhythmicity(agd$axis1, agd$timestamp) #> Consensus Rhythmicity (multi-method) #>  #>   cosinor F-test         p=0.0024  rhythmic #>   Bingham ellipse                  rhythmic #>   Lomb-Scargle FAP       p=<2e-16  rhythmic #>   chi-square periodogram p=1       no #>  #>   Votes:        3 / 4 methods #>   Fisher p:     <2e-16 #>   Consensus:    RHYTHMIC (alpha = 0.05)

what: The idea, and a rule to remember
title: Batch processing and reporting
text: Every article runs one method one recording. study runs every method every recording, put numbers somewhere collaborator can open. family : circadian.batch() runs whole analysis folder .agd files returns one tidy row per file; circadian.workbook() writes complete analysis single recording multi-sheet Excel file; circadian.daily() breaks recording per-day rows within-recording drift shows instead averaged away. single subject, population.cosinor() cosinor.compare() pool per-subject fits group rhythm test whether two groups differ. rule carry article: one row per file, bad file costs cell, run. circadian.batch() wraps file error handler, recording fails read analyse lands error column NA metrics every file completes. batch aborts file 37 200 worse useless; batch flags file 37 finishes study can act .

title: Batch processing and reporting
text: equation (family plumbing, model), output fixed shape worth stating front. circadian.batch() returns data frame: file column, error column, full single-row summary circadian.workbook() builds internally (nonparametric /IV/RA/L5/M10, cosinor MESOR/amplitude/acrophase rhythmicity F-test, Lomb-Scargle chi-square periods, DFA multifractal width, multiscale entropy, rest-activity transition rates). Every file one row; every metric one column. circadian.workbook() writes summary first sheet .xlsx, adds detail sheets (hourly profile, periodograms, fluctuation multifractal spectra, transition curves) self-documenting Data Dictionary sheet, single source output codebook renders . circadian.daily() returns daily data frame one row per calendar day (L5, M10, onset hours, RA, IV, day’s total counts). Knowing shape knowing script .

title: Batch processing and reporting
text: Readable, regularly-epoched files. batch assumes path .agd ActiGraph file read.agd() can open. Anything else becomes error row, design, failure. consistent epoch across files. default epoch inferred per file timestamps; mixing 30 s 60 s recordings allowed, epoch-sensitive metrics (IV especially) comparable within epoch. Pass explicit epoch_length force one. Enough valid days per file. per-day -day metrics need whole days; circadian.daily() drops day fewer min_hours valid recording reporting . Subjects, epochs, unit group tools. population.cosinor() cosinor.compare() fit one cosinor per subject pool; assume subject contributes averaged 24-hour profile least min_valid_hours data. substitute mixed model. See Choosing method reach one.

title: Batch processing and reporting
text: trusting group tools real cohorts, watch recover difference plant. build two groups six subjects : morning group whose rhythm peaks 08:00 amplitude 45, evening group peaking 13:00 amplitude 38. share MESOR 100. planted -group acrophase difference exactly five hours. population.cosinor() pools group’s per-subject fits Bingham group-mean rhythm confidence intervals (Bingham et al., 1982). return planted MESOR, amplitude, acrophase group. population cosinor recovers group’s planted acrophase amplitude. cosinor.compare() tests whether two groups differ. omnibus Bingham / Hotelling T2T^2 joint (MESOR, cosine, sine) vector (Bingham et al., 1982); per-parameter rows break , acrophase test made circular-aware. omnibus overwhelmingly significant, per-parameter breakdown lands planted : MESOR difference near zero, amplitude differs planted seven units, acrophase difference recovers planted five hours. tools report exactly contrast put .
code: set.seed(42) hrs <- 0:23 act <- ts <- subj <- grp <- NULL for (g in c("morning", "evening")) for (i in 1:6) {   acro <- if (g == "morning") 8 else 13   amp  <- if (g == "morning") 45 else 38   y <- 100 + amp * cos(2 * pi * (hrs - acro) / 24) + rnorm(24, 0, 5)   act  <- c(act, y)   subj <- c(subj, rep(paste0(substr(g, 1, 1), i), 24))   grp  <- c(grp, rep(g, 24))   ts   <- c(ts, as.POSIXct("2024-01-01", tz = "UTC") + hrs * 3600) } ts <- as.POSIXct(ts, tz = "UTC", origin = "1970-01-01") pc <- population.cosinor(act, ts, subject = subj, group = grp) knitr::kable(   data.frame(     group     = c("morning", "evening"),     planted_acrophase = c(8, 13),     recovered_acrophase = c(pc$morning$acrophase, pc$evening$acrophase),     planted_amplitude = c(45, 38),     recovered_amplitude = c(pc$morning$amplitude, pc$evening$amplitude)   ),   digits = 2,   caption = "The population cosinor recovers each group's planted acrophase and amplitude." ) cmp <- cosinor.compare(act, ts, subject = subj, group = grp) cmp #> Cosinor Comparison Between Two Groups #>  #>   Groups:  evening (n=6)  vs  morning (n=6) #>   Period:  24 h #>  #>   Joint (Bingham/Hotelling T2):  F(3,8) = 594.68   p = 9.76e-10 #>  #>   mesor     diff = +0.11   t(7.5) = 0.23   p = 0.821 #>   amplitude diff = -8.04   t(7.3) = -9.27   p = 2.62e-05 #>   acrophase diff = +5.00   t(9.1) = 61.49   p = 3.3e-13

title: Batch processing and reporting
text: bundled extdata folder holds package’s example .agd files. Pointing circadian.batch() directory analyses every file returns one row . Two files , two rows , error NA (read analysed cleanly), full metric set sitting alongside columns sampled . row carries everything output codebook documents. see within recording rather across files, circadian.daily() reports calendar day . -active-window onset day. Reporting per day, rather pooled batch row, shows steady active phase across recording.
code: folder <- system.file("extdata", package = "actiRhythm") batch  <- circadian.batch(folder, verbose = FALSE) nrow(batch) #> [1] 2 batch[, c("file", "error", "IS", "IV", "RA",           "cosinor_amplitude", "rhythm_p_value", "period_tau")] #>                      file error     IS     IV     RA cosinor_amplitude #> 1 MOS2E39230594_60sec.agd  <NA> 0.2279 1.0008 0.9800            295.63 #> 2  MOS2E3923063660sec.agd  <NA> 0.4230 1.2687 0.7706            351.14 #>   rhythm_p_value period_tau #> 1    0.002396228   25.43077 #> 2    0.050151893   24.09425 agd   <- agd.counts(read.agd(example_agd(1), verbose = FALSE)) daily <- circadian.daily(agd$axis1, agd$timestamp) daily #> Per-Day Nonparametric Metrics (7 days) #>  #>        date     L5 L5_onset_h      M10 M10_onset_h    RA    IV   total #>  2025-10-08  8.590      0.200 1190.400      10.533 0.986 1.342  961265 #>  2025-10-09 12.883      1.900 1038.933      13.667 0.976 1.032  804120 #>  2025-10-10 15.660      1.433 1657.895      12.050 0.981 1.628 1247818 #>  2025-10-11  0.000      1.833    0.445      15.833 1.000 2.291     445 #>  2025-10-12  0.000      0.000    0.075      13.783 1.000 1.533      78 #>  2025-10-13  0.000      0.567    0.405      22.550 1.000 0.988     243 #>  2025-10-14  0.000      0.000   14.171      14.000 1.000 1.121    6313 ggplot(daily$daily, aes(date, M10_onset_h)) +   geom_line(colour = "grey60") +   geom_point(colour = "#236192", size = 2) +   labs(x = NULL, y = "M10 onset (h)") +   theme_actiRhythm()

Sort filter trusting metric; non-NA value means row’s numbers missing, subject flat rhythm. rhythm_p_value 0.05 says day-night cosine real, read percent_rhythm (much day single cosine captures) /IV (stable fragmented), exactly cosinor nonparametric articles. batch just lines columns side side. period_tau near 24 entrained rhythm; value drifting 24 across cohort cue look free-running, rounding error. group comparison, omnibus T2T^2 p-value answers “rhythms differ ?” read single parameter; per-parameter rows say parameter carries difference, acrophase row already unwrapped around clock.

title: Batch processing and reporting
text: machinery feeds two output formats collaborator can open directly. full single-subject workbook. circadian.workbook() runs every method one recording writes multi-sheet .xlsx: one-row summary, detail sheet per analysis, Data Dictionary. write temporary file list sheets. (chunk guarded runs openxlsx installed.) circadian.batch() accepts file = argument write single workbook whose Summary sheet one row per file, study-level companion per-subject workbook . Saving figure. save.circadian.plot() thin wrapper ggplot2::ggsave() takes package’s plot_* figures writes format implied file extension, report script can emit actograms without restating size DPI time.
code: xlsx <- tempfile(fileext = ".xlsx") circadian.workbook(agd$axis1, agd$timestamp, file = xlsx, include_period_ci = FALSE) openxlsx::getSheetNames(xlsx) #>  [1] "Summary"                  "Hourly Profile"           #>  [3] "Lomb-Scargle Periodogram" "Chi-square Periodogram"   #>  [5] "Fractal DFA"              "MF-DFA"                   #>  [7] "Multiscale Entropy"       "Rest-Active Transitions"  #>  [9] "Active-Rest Transitions"  "Data Dictionary" p   <- plot_actogram(agd$axis1, agd$timestamp) png <- tempfile(fileext = ".png") save.circadian.plot(p, png, width = 8, height = 5) file.exists(png) #> [1] TRUE

what: A fuller profile, and a decomposition
title: Beyond the basics: profile, decomposition, and finer metrics
text: cosinor fits one cosine day. circadian.flm() fits functional model 24-hour profile several Fourier harmonics, follows real, asymmetric shape day instead forcing symmetric curve (Wang et al., 2011); single cosine one-harmonic special case. functional fit (line) averaged hourly profile (points). extra Fourier harmonics let curve follow asymmetric morning rise evening decline single cosine smooths . extra harmonics buy back variance single cosine left table, curve now bends morning rise one cosine ran straight past. circadian.ssa() takes opposite view. Instead average day decomposes whole recording singular spectrum analysis additive components: slow trend, circadian pair, noise (Golyandina & Zhigljavsky, 2013). trajectory matrix grows series, bin long minute recording coarser epoch first. circadian component singular spectrum analysis pulls whole recording: daily rhythm separated slow trend noise. recording circadian pair carries 14 percent variance fundamental period near 24.5 hours, slightly-long period periodogram found earlier, now isolated clean component raw series.
code: flm <- circadian.flm(agd$axis1, agd$timestamp) flm #> Functional Linear Model (24-hour activity profile) #>  #>   Basis:        fourier (order 4, 9 terms) #>   Period:       24 hours #>   Days / profile points: 8 / 24 #>  #>   Model fit: #>     R-squared:   0.7352 #>     AIC:         406.84 #>     F-statistic: 5.21 (p = 0.003) #>  #>   Dominant harmonic: H1, amplitude 303.40, acrophase 16.99 h #>  #>   Peak 956.3 at 19.2 h; trough -117.7 at 3.8 h #>  #>   Reference: Wang et al. (2011) ggplot(flm$smooth_curve, aes(t, activity)) +   geom_point(data = flm$fitted_profile, aes(t, observed), colour = "grey55") +   geom_line(colour = "#236192", linewidth = 1) +   labs(x = "Hour of day", y = "Activity") +   theme_actiRhythm() bin      <- as.integer(as.numeric(agd$timestamp) %/% 600)   # 10-minute bins counts10 <- tapply(agd$axis1, bin, sum) t10      <- as.POSIXct(as.numeric(names(counts10)) * 600, origin = "1970-01-01", tz = "UTC") ssa <- circadian.ssa(as.numeric(counts10), t10) ssa #> Singular Spectrum Analysis (Basic SSA) #>  #>   Window length L:    144 epochs (24.0 h) #>   Series length n:    993 (K = 850, span 6.9 days) #>   Components kept:     10 of rank 144 #>  #>   Variance explained (leading components): #>     ET1   lambda = 0.2929  (cumulative 0.2929) #>     ET2   lambda = 0.0814  (cumulative 0.3743) #>     ET3   lambda = 0.0578  (cumulative 0.4321) #>     ET4   lambda = 0.0295  (cumulative 0.4616) #>     ET5   lambda = 0.0293  (cumulative 0.4909) #>  #>   Grouping: #>     Trend:      components 1 #>     Circadian:  components 2, 3 (13.9% of variance) #>     Fundamental period: 24.49 h #>  #>   Reference: Golyandina and Zhigljavsky (2013) ggplot(data.frame(time = t10, circadian = ssa$circadian), aes(time, circadian)) +   geom_line(colour = "#236192") +   labs(x = "Time", y = "Circadian component") +   theme_actiRhythm()

title: Choosing a method
text: Plot recording first plot_actogram(), run circadian.rhythm(), cosinor.analysis(), rhythmicity.test() (consensus.rhythmicity() ). active band stacks straight vertical column numbers sit comfortably, done. Reach band bends, scatters, fragments, specific question .

what: Is there a rhythm, and how strong?
title: Choosing a method
text: Two complementary answers: Without assuming shape: circadian.rhythm() returns interdaily stability (, tightly pattern repeats), intradaily variability (IV, fragmented ), relative amplitude (RA, day-night contrast) (Van Someren et al., 1999; Witting et al., 1990). fitted cosine: cosinor.analysis() gives interpretable MESOR, amplitude, acrophase, rhythmicity.test() zero-amplitude F-test whether rhythm real (Cornelissen, 2014; Nelson et al., 1979). Use nonparametric route want assume sinusoid, cosinor route want interpretable parameters. complement rather compete: high RA low , instance, strong rhythm carried irregular timing, something neither number says alone. pool every test one verdict, consensus.rhythmicity() runs cosinor F-test, Bingham ellipse, Lomb-Scargle false-alarm probability, chi-square periodogram, reports majority vote Fisher-combined p-value (Fisher, 1925).

what: What is the period, and is it really 24 hours?
title: Choosing a method
text: cosinor assumes fixed period (24 h default). rhythm might run long short (free-running drifting period), estimate instead assuming : circadian.period(): Lomb-Scargle periodogram; tolerates missing epochs uneven sampling (Lomb, 1976; Ruf, 1999). chi.sq.periodogram(): classic Sokolove-Bushell periodogram; expects near-regular sampling (Sokolove & Bushell, 1978). period.ci(): circular block-bootstrap confidence interval period (Politis & Romano, 1992). Lomb-Scargle vs chi-square: prefer Lomb-Scargle recording gaps irregular epochs; chi-square periodogram traditional choice regularly sampled actigraphy. Running seeing peak land place reassurance real artifact one method. consensus.rhythmicity() uses exactly reason.

title: Choosing a method
text: order increasing waveform flexibility, choose simplest percent-rhythm says enough: cosinor.analysis(): one symmetric cosine; simplest interpretable. cosinor.extended() / cosinor.antilogistic(): adds nonlinear shape asymmetric, squared-real day (Marler et al., 2006). cosinor.multicomponent(): several harmonics, number chosen AIC BIC. circadian.flm(): functional model whole daily profile follows true morning rise evening decline (Wang et al., 2011). last two demonstrated Beyond basics article.

what: Does the rhythm drift, shift, or fragment?
title: Choosing a method
text: actogram band bends breaks , single summary averages change away. Switch time-resolved tools: circadian.spectrogram(): period strength surface time. circadian.ssa(): separates series trend, circadian, noise (Golyandina & Zhigljavsky, 2013). circadian.emd() hilbert.huang(): instantaneous, cycle--cycle period (N. E. Huang et al., 1998). circadian.wavelet(): full time-frequency power surface. sleep.changepoints(): dates regime change directly counts. curve.registration(): chronotype phase blur days disagree (Krafty et al., 2019). walkthrough Nonstationary complex rhythms article.

what: How complex or fractal is the signal?
title: Choosing a method
text: Two recordings can share amplitude yet differ moment--moment dynamics: fractal.dfa(): detrended-fluctuation exponent (long-range correlation) (Peng et al., 1994). mfdfa(): multifractality, summarised spectrum width (Kantelhardt et al., 2002). multiscale.entropy(): complexity changes across coarse-graining scales (Costa et al., 2002). Demonstrated Beyond basics.

what: When does the person sleep, and how regular?
title: Choosing a method
text: per-epoch sleep/wake label counts: sleep.cole.kripke() adults (Cole et al., 1992), sleep.sadeh() children adolescents (Sadeh et al., 1994). Regularity label day day: sleep.regularity.index() (Phillips et al., 2017), sri.matrix() day--day heatmap. night’s onset wake, straight counts: sleep.changepoints() (Chen & Sun, 2024). Every rest bout, naps included: rest.periods() (Roenneberg) (Roenneberg et al., 2015) rest.crespo() (Crespo) (Crespo et al., 2012), two independent algorithms; rest.hmm() probabilistic state-space alternative (Q. Huang et al., 2018). Timing social clock: social.jet.lag() (Wittmann et al., 2006); ultradian structure within sleep, lids() (Winnebeck et al., 2018). rest detector? sleep.changepoints() finds one main rest bout night (sleep wake timing). rest.periods() rest.crespo() consolidate every rest bout across recording, daytime naps included. Run independent cross-check. rest.hmm() models rest probabilistically rather hard bouts.

title: Choosing a method
text: count files (.agd) pre-extracted counts: analyse directly; everything takes count series. raw files (.gt3x, .cwa, .bin): read.raw() raw.metrics() give ENMO, MAD, z-angle, gravity/posture signal counts carry. z-angle drives diary-free, posture-based sleep detection (rest.spt(), sib.vanhees()) needs scored sleep . See raw acceleration article. Cross-brand counts (converting Axivity GENEActiv ActiGraph-equivalent counts) approximation (Brond et al., 2017): fine relative normalised metrics (, IV, RA, periodograms, SRI), absolute-count comparisons ActiGraph cut-points.

title: Choosing a method
text: Every function describes one recording. study: Group mean rhythm: population.cosinor() pools per-subject cosinor fits Bingham population-mean rhythm confidence intervals (Bingham et al., 1982). Two-group comparison: cosinor.compare() tests whether rhythm differs groups (omnibus Hotelling T^2 plus per-parameter tests) (Bingham et al., 1982). Covariates, repeated measures, nesting: run circadian.batch() folder get one row metrics per recording. feed per-subject metrics (, IV, RA, MESOR, amplitude, acrophase, period) mixed model lme4 nlme alongside design. package supplies metrics; supply multilevel model.

title: Choosing a method
text: hold across package decide whether trustworthy: Evenly spaced epochs enough valid days. circadian.rhythm() sets aside days little valid recording computing; short records give unstable /IV unreliable periods. Wear time. Gate counts valid wear, supply mask; none, detect.nonwear.choi() / detect.nonwear.troiano() (counts) detect.nonwear.raw() (raw) build one. rest.periods() rest.crespo() apply wear filter . Epoch length. affects IV epoch sleep scorers, validated 60-second epochs.

what: The idea, and a rule to remember
title: Cosinor models
text: cosinor fits cosine fixed period rest-activity rhythm reads three interpretable numbers : MESOR (rhythm-adjusted mean), amplitude (half peak--trough swing), acrophase (clock time peak). nonparametric metrics make shape assumption, cosinor makes strong one (single, symmetric sinusoid) return gives parameters can model compare across people. rule carry article: significant rhythmicity test tells rhythm flat, single cosine right shape. Always read percent-rhythm alongside p-value; much daily structure one cosine actually captures, low value data telling shape wrong.

title: Cosinor models
text: Write averaged daily activity Y(t)Y(t) clock time tt, period τ\tau (24 hours default) ω=2π/τ\omega = 2\pi/\tau. cosinor model Y(t)=M+Acos⁡(ωt+ϕ)+e(t), Y(t) = M + \cos(\omega t + \phi) + e(t), fitted linear form Y(t)=M+β1cos⁡(ωt)+β2sin⁡(ωt)+e(t), Y(t) = M + \beta_1\cos(\omega t) + \beta_2\sin(\omega t) + e(t), three parameters come straight regression coefficients: MESOR=M,=β12+β22,ϕ=atan2⁡(−β2,β1). \mathrm{MESOR} = M, \qquad = \sqrt{\beta_1^2 + \beta_2^2}, \qquad \phi = \operatorname{atan2}(-\beta_2,\, \beta_1). Whether rhythm real zero-amplitude F-test H0:=0H_0\!: = 0(Nelson et al., 1979), share variance cosine explains percent-rhythm, 100R2100\,R^2.

title: Cosinor models
text: One symmetric sinusoid. model single peak trough equal, smooth shape. Asymmetric days (fast morning rise, slow evening decline), squared-days, naps, bimodal patterns violate . section “single cosine wrong shape” makes concrete. fixed, known period. cosinor assumes τ\tau; estimate . rhythm may free-running, estimate period first (see Choosing method). acrophase meaningful amplitude . clock time peak uninterpretable amplitude distinguishable zero.

title: Cosinor models
text: First, see fit recover parameters plant. build seven-day recording cosine MESOR 200, amplitude 100, 16:00 acrophase, add noise. cosinor recovers planted MESOR, amplitude, acrophase. rhythmicity test confirms rhythm real reports much variance cosine carries.
code: ts <- seq(as.POSIXct("2024-01-01", tz = "UTC"), by = 60, length.out = 7 * 1440) th <- as.numeric(difftime(ts, ts[1], units = "hours")) set.seed(1) known <- pmax(0, 200 + 100 * cos(2 * pi * (th - 16) / 24) + rnorm(length(ts), 0, 15))  ca <- cosinor.analysis(known, ts) knitr::kable(   data.frame(parameter = c("MESOR", "amplitude", "acrophase (h)"),              planted   = c(200, 100, 16),              recovered = c(ca$mesor, ca$amplitude, ca$acrophase)),   digits = 2, caption = "The cosinor recovers the planted MESOR, amplitude, and acrophase." ) rt <- rhythmicity.test(known, ts, cosinor_result = ca) c(F = rt$F, p_value = rt$p_value, percent_rhythm = rt$percent_rhythm, rhythmic = rt$rhythmic) #>              F        p_value percent_rhythm       rhythmic  #>   1.049895e+05   1.000000e-42   9.999000e+01   1.000000e+00

title: Cosinor models
text: bundled recording runs way. cosinor.analysis() returns typed object; rhythmicity.test() checks whether rhythm statistically real. Hourly activity fitted 24-hour cosine overlaid. peak curve acrophase; height MESOR line amplitude. points leave curve structure single cosine follow. joint uncertainty amplitude acrophase together, cosinor.confidence.ellipse() returns Bingham et al. (1982) ellipse; ellipse excludes origin geometric form “detectable rhythm”.
code: agd <- agd.counts(read.agd(example_agd(1), verbose = FALSE)) cos <- cosinor.analysis(agd$axis1, agd$timestamp, period = 24) c(mesor = unname(cos$mesor), amplitude = unname(cos$amplitude),   acrophase = unname(cos$acrophase), r_squared = unname(cos$r_squared)) #>     mesor amplitude acrophase r_squared  #>  327.2500  295.6300   16.9200    0.4371 rhythmicity.test(agd$axis1, agd$timestamp, cosinor_result = cos) #> Cosinor Rhythmicity Test (Halberg zero-amplitude F-test) #>  #> H0: amplitude = 0 (no rhythm) #>   Period:         24 h #>   F(2, 21):        8.15 #>   P-value:        0.0024 #>   Percent rhythm: 43.7% (R-squared = 0.4371) #>   Rhythmic:       YES (alpha = 0.05) plot_extended_cosinor(agd$axis1, agd$timestamp) el <- cosinor.confidence.ellipse(cos) c(excludes_origin = el$excludes_origin, rhythm_detected = el$rhythm_detected) #> excludes_origin rhythm_detected  #>            TRUE            TRUE

title: Cosinor models
text: MESOR rhythm-adjusted mean, midline fitted cosine rather raw average. Amplitude half peak--trough swing; larger stronger sinusoidal component. Acrophase clock time peak circular: 23.5 0.5 hour apart, 23, interpretable amplitude clearly non-zero. Percent-rhythm share variance cosine explains; low value next small p-value warning rule flags.

what: When a single cosine is the wrong shape
title: Cosinor models
text: rule action. build siesta day (active morning evening midday dip), clear 24-hour rhythm single symmetric cosine. test significant (rhythm flat), yet single cosine explains 62 percent day. missing third midday dip, symmetric cosine represent. multi-harmonic fit recovers : selects two components explains essentially (chunk reports R-squared). practice, let low percent-rhythm, significant p-value, send richer model rather misleading single acrophase.
code: hod <- as.numeric(format(ts, "%H")) + as.numeric(format(ts, "%M")) / 60 set.seed(2) siesta <- pmax(0, 120 + 70 * cos(2 * pi * (hod - 14) / 24) +     # day-night fundamental                     55 * cos(2 * pi * 2 * (hod - 14) / 24) +     # the midday dip                     rnorm(length(ts), 0, 12))  cs  <- cosinor.analysis(siesta, ts) rts <- rhythmicity.test(siesta, ts, cosinor_result = cs) c(rhythmic = rts$rhythmic, p_value = rts$p_value, percent_rhythm = rts$percent_rhythm) #>       rhythmic        p_value percent_rhythm  #>   1.000000e+00   3.743731e-05   6.212000e+01 mc <- cosinor.multicomponent(siesta, ts) c(n_harmonics = mc$n_harmonics, r_squared = mc$r_squared) #> n_harmonics   r_squared  #>   2.0000000   0.9999345 mc$harmonics #>    harmonic amplitude acrophase_h #> x2        1  69.67840   14.012182 #> x4        2  54.41006    2.011788

title: Cosinor models
text: Asymmetric squared-days: cosinor.extended() cosinor.antilogistic() fit sigmoidally transformed cosine Marler et al. (2006), adding shape parameters (steepness, - -mesor times) single cosinor lacks. Several harmonics, chosen objectively: cosinor.multicomponent() adds components picks many AIC BIC, siesta example . parametric amplitude ratio: circadian.quotient() turns cosinor amplitude MESOR circadian quotient (amplitude / MESOR). Pooling evidence: consensus.rhythmicity() runs cosinor F-test, Bingham ellipse, Lomb-Scargle false-alarm probability, chi-square periodogram together, reports majority vote combined p-value. one subject group: population.cosinor() pools per-subject fits Bingham population-mean rhythm confidence intervals, cosinor.compare() tests whether rhythm differs two groups omnibus Hotelling T2T^2 per-parameter tests (Bingham et al., 1982). See Choosing method reach .
code: fit <- cosinor.antilogistic(agd$axis1, agd$timestamp) c(MESOR = fit$MESOR, amplitude = fit$amplitude, acrophase = fit$acrophase,   UpMesor = fit$UpMesor, DownMesor = fit$DownMesor) #>     MESOR amplitude acrophase   UpMesor DownMesor  #> 372.95566 488.79342  16.59496  11.60451  21.58541

title: Cosinor models
text: period assumed, estimated. Set correctly, estimate first periodograms. single component symmetric. Real days rarely ; read percent-rhythm escalate extended multicomponent fit low. acrophase needs real amplitude. Report amplitude clearly distinguishable zero (rhythmicity test confidence ellipse checks).

title: Cosinor models
text: cosinor follows Cornelissen (2014) zero-amplitude test Nelson et al. (1979), anti-logistic extension Marler et al. (2006), population framework Bingham et al. (1982). actiRhythm’s single extended cosinor cross-checked cosinor package ActCR::ActExtendCosinor (printed precision) Validation article package’s test suite.

what: The idea, and a rule to remember
title: Fractal and nonlinear dynamics
text: fractal nonlinear metrics ask different question cosinor nonparametric battery. describe shape depth average day; fractal tools describe temporal texture minute--minute fluctuations: minute’s activity remembers last, memory stretches across scales minutes hours. Healthy activity neither smooth purely random: carries self-similar, “1/f” structure, structure breaks aging disease ways amplitude metrics see (Hu et al., 2009). rule carry article: scaling exponent measures correlation, size. signal can large regular yet α\alpha tiny one; α\alpha reports fluctuations organised across time, independent amplitude. Read rhythm’s memory, strength.

title: Fractal and nonlinear dynamics
text: Detrended fluctuation analysis (DFA). Start activity x1,…,xNx_1,\dots,x_N mean x‾\bar x, integrate mean-centred signal profile (Hu et al., 2001; Peng et al., 1994): Y(k)=∑=1k(xi−x‾). Y(k) = \sum_{=1}^{k} \bigl(x_i - \bar x\bigr).  Split YY non-overlapping windows length nn, fit remove least-squares line inside window, pool residuals fluctuation F(n)=1N∑k(Y(k)−Ŷn(k))2. F(n) = \sqrt{\tfrac{1}{N}\sum_{k}\bigl(Y(k) - \widehat{Y}_n(k)\bigr)^2}.  signal self-similar, F(n)F(n) grows power law, F(n)∝nαF(n)\propto n^{\alpha}, scaling exponent α\alpha slope log⁡F(n)\log F(n) log⁡n\log n. benchmarks exact: α≈0.5\alpha\approx 0.5 uncorrelated white noise, α≈1.0\alpha\approx 1.0 1/f1/f (pink) noise, α≈1.5\alpha\approx 1.5 Brownian random walk. Multifractal DFA (MF-DFA). single α\alpha assumes one scaling law governs whole signal. MF-DFA relaxes weighting windowed variances F2(v,n)F^2(v,n) moment order qq(Kantelhardt et al., 2002): Fq(n)=[1Ns∑v(F2(v,n))q/2]1/q∝nh(q). F_q(n) = \Bigl[\tfrac{1}{N_s}\sum_{v}\bigl(F^2(v,n)\bigr)^{q/2}\Bigr]^{1/q}         \propto n^{\,h(q)} . h(2)h(2) ordinary DFA exponent. h(q)h(q) flat signal monofractal; h(q)h(q) decreases qq signal multifractal, spectrum width Δα\Delta\alpha (spread Holder exponents) measures much. Multiscale entropy (MSE). Complexity scaling. Sample entropy counts unpredictable series : negative log chance two sub-sequences matching mm points still match m+1m+1 points, within tolerance rr(Richman & Moorman, 2000): SampEn(m,r)=−ln⁡AB. \mathrm{SampEn}(m, r) = -\ln \frac{}{B}.  MSE computes SampEn\mathrm{SampEn} coarse-grained copies series across scales τ\tau(Costa et al., 2002). Noise loses entropy averaged; genuinely complex, correlated signals hold entropy across scales.

title: Fractal and nonlinear dynamics
text: long, gap-free segment. Scaling laws need many octaves window sizes; three functions analyse longest continuous non-NA run return NA (never error) series short fit slope. days minute-level data practical minimum. Stationary fluctuations, trends. DFA removes linear trend inside window, slow drift tolerated, strong embedded rhythm device-step can masquerade long-range correlation. Gate wear time first. Amplitude-blind, design. α\alpha invariant scaling signal, complements, never replaces, amplitude metrics. Report alongside, instead , cosinor nonparametric numbers. Heavy zeros distort negative moments. Activity counts long runs exact zeros, give near-zero windowed variances. Raised negative power MF-DFA blow , inflating spectrum width; q>0q>0 side h(2)h(2) stay trustworthy.

title: Fractal and nonlinear dynamics
text: trusting α\alpha real recording, watch recover dynamics whose answer fixed construction. build three series length variance different memory: white noise (correlation, planted α=0.5\alpha=0.5), 1/f1/f pink noise synthesised power spectrum falls 1/f1/f (planted α=1.0\alpha=1.0), Brownian random walk, running sum white noise (planted α=1.5\alpha=1.5). DFA recovers planted scaling exponent: 0.5 white noise, 1.0 1/f, 1.5 random walk. exponent tracks planted process almost exactly. fluctuation plot random-walk (brown) series shows scaling directly: single straight line log-log axes, whose slope exponent. DFA fluctuation curve random-walk (brown) series log-log axes. slope scaling exponent (~1.5 ); one straight line monofractal scaling.
code: set.seed(42) n <- 8192  white <- rnorm(n)                                   # planted alpha = 0.5 brown <- cumsum(rnorm(n))                            # planted alpha = 1.5 pink  <- {                                           # planted alpha = 1.0   amp  <- 1 / sqrt(1:(n / 2))                        # 1/f amplitude spectrum   ph   <- runif(n / 2, 0, 2 * pi)   half <- complex(modulus = c(0, amp), argument = c(0, ph))   Re(fft(c(half, Conj(rev(half[2:(n / 2)]))), inverse = TRUE))[1:n] }  planted <- c(white = 0.5, pink = 1.0, brown = 1.5) recovered <- c(white = fractal.dfa(white)$alpha,                pink  = fractal.dfa(pink)$alpha,                brown = fractal.dfa(brown)$alpha) knitr::kable(   data.frame(process = names(planted), planted = planted, recovered_alpha = recovered),   row.names = FALSE, digits = 3,   caption = "DFA recovers the planted scaling exponent: 0.5 for white noise, 1.0 for 1/f, 1.5 for a random walk." ) plot_dfa(brown)

title: Fractal and nonlinear dynamics
text: bundled recording runs way. fractal.dfa() returns typed object prints exponents interpretive guide; alpha1 alpha2 split short long timescales breakpoint_min window (90 minutes default). overall α\alpha sits 0.9-1.0 healthy band (1/f1/f signature intact activity regulation) rather near 0.5 noise 1.5 random walk.
code: agd <- agd.counts(read.agd(example_agd(1), verbose = FALSE)) dfa <- fractal.dfa(agd$axis1) dfa #>  #> Detrended Fluctuation Analysis (Peng et al., 1994) #>  #>   Samples analyzed:   9919 #>   Window sizes:       29 (range 4-2479) #>   alpha  (overall):   0.9264 #>   alpha1 (n < 90):     0.9581 #>   alpha2 (n >= 90):    0.8658 #>  #>   Guide: near 0.5 = uncorrelated, near 1.0 = 1/f, near 1.5 = Brownian

title: Fractal and nonlinear dynamics
text: State exponent human terms: α\alpha runs 0.5 (uncorrelated) 1.0 (1/f1/f, healthy target) 1.5 (random-walk smoothness). Reductions toward 0.5 reported aging Alzheimer’s disease (Hu et al., 2009). α1\alpha_1 vs α2\alpha_2 compare memory breakpoint; drop short long timescales crossover, common activity circadian band changes scaling. Spectrum width Δα\Delta\alpha (MF-DFA) near zero monofractal grows multifractality; read q>0q>0 side raw counts. Complexity / area (MSE) entropy summed across scales: higher means signal stays unpredictable even smoothed, hallmark physiological complexity rather noise. useful pairing α\alpha MSE slope: 1/f1/f scaling sustained entropy across scales together say “complex healthy”, neither number asserts alone.

what: The wider fractal and nonlinear family
title: Fractal and nonlinear dynamics
text: one exponent spectrum. mfdfa() asks whether single scaling law fits, whether different-sized fluctuations scale differently. contrast monofractal signal (white noise, one law) multifractal one (binomial multiplicative cascade, textbook multifractal): spectrum width separates cleanly h(2)h(2) stays interpretable DFA exponent. narrow spectrum width flags monofractal; wide one flags multifractality. real recording, MF-DFA confirms DFA exponent h(2)h(2); raw width inflated zero-variance windows noted , report h(2)h(2) reliable summary. Complexity across scales. multiscale.entropy() applies sample entropy coarse-grained copies recording, separating signals stay complex smoothed noise loses entropy scale. Sample entropy across coarse-graining scales. flat rising curve signature physiological complexity; curve falls toward zero noise-like. complexity index (area, entropy summed across scales) condenses curve single number comparison recordings.
code: set.seed(7) mono <- rnorm(8192) cascade <- Reduce(function(v, .) as.numeric(rbind(v * 0.3, v * 0.7)),                   1:13, accumulate = FALSE)        # binomial multiplicative cascade multi <- cascade / sd(cascade)  knitr::kable(   data.frame(     signal     = c("monofractal (white noise)", "multifractal (cascade)"),     h2         = c(mfdfa(mono)$alpha_dfa, mfdfa(multi)$alpha_dfa),     width      = c(mfdfa(mono)$width,     mfdfa(multi)$width)   ),   col.names = c("signal", "h(2)", "spectrum width"),   row.names = FALSE, digits = 3,   caption = "A narrow spectrum width flags a monofractal; a wide one flags multifractality." ) mf <- mfdfa(agd$axis1) c(h2 = unname(mf$alpha_dfa)) #>        h2  #> 0.9175375 mse <- multiscale.entropy(agd$axis1, scales = 1:20) mse #>  #> Multiscale Sample Entropy (Costa et al., 2002) #>  #>   Samples analyzed:   9919 #>   Scales:             20 (1-20) #>   SampEn @ scale 1:   0.0729 #>   Complexity (area):  1.4762 #>   Slope (mse on scale):0.0006 #>  #>   Negative slope => noise-like; flat/positive => complex ggplot(data.frame(scale = mse$scales, sampen = mse$mse),        aes(scale, sampen)) +   geom_line(colour = "grey60") +   geom_point(colour = "#236192", size = 2) +   labs(x = "scale (tau)", y = "sample entropy") +   theme_actiRhythm()

what: Recovering known truth: an injected device-off block
title: Reading data and wear-time detection
text: trusting non-wear detection real data, plant gap whose location know confirm algorithms recover exactly block. build clean three-day count series day-night cosine, switch device “” (run zeros) known 180-minute window. detectors return logical mask (TRUE = wear, FALSE = non-wear), one value per epoch. ask epochs flagged non-wear compare planted block. algorithms recover planted device-block exactly: epochs 1000-1179. algorithm flags 180 epochs silenced, epoch. figure makes visual: shaded band recovered non-wear mask, sitting exactly flat-line gap. window planted series. flat zero block injected device-period; shaded band Choi non-wear mask, recovered exactly . two algorithms identical, though. agree clean block diverge design, differences matter real data. Choi requires long window guards tolerated spike flanking -zero windows; Troiano uses shorter default window count ceiling spikes (Choi et al., 2011; Troiano et al., 2008). Two short experiments expose gap. Non-wear epochs flagged. two algorithms disagree defaults differ. 75-minute gap clears Troiano’s 60-minute frame Choi’s 90-minute one, Troiano flags Choi . lone 250-count spike sits nothing Choi cares (flanked zeros, Choi absorbs keeps full 180-epoch block), exceeds Troiano’s stoplevel 100, ending bout minute leaving 179. Neither “right”; encode different definitions non-wear, choose match literature comparing . recovered mask exactly analysis functions want. Pass wear_time rhythm metrics computed worn epochs:
code: set.seed(1) n   <- 3 * 1440 ts  <- seq(as.POSIXct("2024-01-01", tz = "UTC"), by = 60, length.out = n) hod <- as.numeric(format(ts, "%H")) + as.numeric(format(ts, "%M")) / 60 worn <- pmax(0, round(100 + 80 * cos(2 * pi * (hod - 14) / 24) + rnorm(n, 0, 15)))  gap <- 1000:1179            # a 180-minute device-off block at a known location counts <- worn; counts[gap] <- 0 choi <- detect.nonwear.choi(counts) tro  <- detect.nonwear.troiano(counts)  recovered <- function(mask) { idx <- which(!mask); c(first = min(idx), last = max(idx), n = length(idx)) } knitr::kable(   rbind(planted  = c(first = min(gap), last = max(gap), n = length(gap)),         choi     = recovered(choi),         troiano  = recovered(tro)),   caption = "Both algorithms recover the planted device-off block exactly: epochs 1000-1179." ) w  <- 900:1280 df <- data.frame(epoch = w, counts = counts[w], nonwear = !choi[w]) ggplot(df, aes(epoch, counts)) +   geom_rect(data = df[df$nonwear, ],             aes(xmin = epoch - 0.5, xmax = epoch + 0.5, ymin = -Inf, ymax = Inf),             fill = "#E8A33D", alpha = 0.4, inherit.aes = FALSE) +   geom_line(colour = "#236192") +   labs(x = "epoch (minute)", y = "counts") +   theme_actiRhythm() # (1) a borderline 75-minute gap: longer than Troiano's 60-min frame, shorter than Choi's 90 short <- worn; short[1000:1074] <- 0 c75 <- c(choi = sum(!detect.nonwear.choi(short)), troiano = sum(!detect.nonwear.troiano(short)))  # (2) the same 180-min gap, broken by a single high-count spike of 250 spike <- worn; spike[gap] <- 0; spike[1090] <- 250 csp <- c(choi = sum(!detect.nonwear.choi(spike)), troiano = sum(!detect.nonwear.troiano(spike)))  knitr::kable(rbind(`75-min gap` = c75, `gap with 250-count spike` = csp),              caption = "Non-wear epochs flagged. The two algorithms disagree where their defaults differ.") r <- circadian.rhythm(counts, ts, wear_time = choi) c(IS = round(r$IS, 3), valid = isTRUE(!is.null(r$IS))) #>    IS valid  #> 0.986 1.000

code: tf  <- tempfile(fileext = ".csv") hdr <- c(   "------------ Data File Created By ActiGraph wGT3X-BT ActiLife v6.13.4 date format M/d/yyyy at 60 sec ------------",   "Serial Number: MOS2A12345678",   "Start Time 00:00:00",   "Start Date 1/1/2024",   "Epoch Period (hh:mm:ss) 00:01:00",   "--------------------------------------------------",   "Axis1,Axis2,Axis3,Steps") set.seed(2) body <- sprintf("%d,%d,%d,%d", sample(0:500, 6), sample(0:300, 6),                 sample(0:300, 6), sample(0:20, 6)) writeLines(c(hdr, body), tf)  csv <- read.actigraph.csv(tf) names(csv) #> [1] "timestamp" "axis1"     "axis2"     "axis3"     "steps"     "vm" head(csv, 3) #>             timestamp axis1 axis2 axis3 steps  vm #> 1 2024-01-01 00:00:00   340   272    62     3 440 #> 2 2024-01-01 00:01:00   462   203   135    10 522 #> 3 2024-01-01 00:02:00   197   296   230     5 423 df <- data.frame(activity = c(0, 50, 300),                  clock    = c("2024-01-01 00:00:00", "2024-01-01 00:01:00",                               "2024-01-01 00:02:00")) counts.from.data.frame(df, count_col = "activity", time_col = "clock") #>             timestamp counts #> 1 2024-01-01 00:00:00      0 #> 2 2024-01-01 00:01:00     50 #> 3 2024-01-01 00:02:00    300 out  <- tempfile(fileext = ".agd") write.agd(agd, out) back <- agd.counts(read.agd(out, verbose = FALSE)) c(rows_in = nrow(agd), rows_out = nrow(back),   axis1_identical = isTRUE(all.equal(round(agd$axis1), back$axis1))) #>         rows_in        rows_out axis1_identical  #>            9919            9919               1 agd_path <- gt3x.to.agd("subject01.gt3x", epoch = 60) agd      <- agd.counts(read.agd(agd_path))

what: The idea, and a rule to remember
title: Nonparametric rhythm metrics
text: nonparametric metrics describe rest-activity rhythm without assuming particular shape. cosinor asks “well cosine fit?”, interdaily stability, intradaily variability, relative amplitude ask “regular, fragmented, deep rhythm?”, questions can answer even daily profile asymmetric, bimodal, squared-, real rest-activity data usually . rule carry article: nonparametric metrics make shape assumption, sensitive epoch length missing data. Two recordings binned different epochs directly comparable, device-days can move numbers much real change rhythm.

title: Nonparametric rhythm metrics
text: Write recording activity values x1,…,xnx_1, \dots, x_n fixed epoch, overall mean x‾\bar x. Bin hourly means let x‾h\bar x_h mean across days hour--day hh, p=24p = 24 hours. Interdaily stability (), tightly pattern repeats one day next (Witting et al., 1990): =n∑h=1p(x‾h−x‾)2p∑=1n(xi−x‾)2. \mathrm{} = \frac{n \sum_{h=1}^{p}(\bar x_h - \bar x)^2}{p \sum_{=1}^{n}(x_i - \bar x)^2}. Intradaily variability (IV), fragmented rhythm , squared hour--hour differences (Witting et al., 1990): IV=n∑=2n(xi−xi−1)2(n−1)∑=1n(xi−x‾)2. \mathrm{IV} = \frac{n \sum_{=2}^{n}(x_i - x_{-1})^2}{(n-1)\sum_{=1}^{n}(x_i - \bar x)^2}. Relative amplitude (RA), day-night contrast, -active 10-hour window M10 least-active 5-hour window L5 average day (Van Someren et al., 1999): RA=M10−L5M10+L5,M10=maxsx¯[s,s+10h],L5=minsx¯[s,s+5h]. \mathrm{RA} = \frac{\mathrm{M10} - \mathrm{L5}}{\mathrm{M10} + \mathrm{L5}}, \qquad \mathrm{M10} = \max_s \overline{x}_{[s,\,s+10\text{h}]}, \quad \mathrm{L5} = \min_s \overline{x}_{[s,\,s+5\text{h}]} . circadian.rhythm() returns , plus 1-hour extremes L1 M1, autocorrelation predictability ϕ\phi, onset times window.

title: Nonparametric rhythm metrics
text: trusting numbers real data, worth seeing behave data whose answer know. build three seven-day recordings: one whose day repeats exactly, one whose peak wanders hours day day, one pure noise. fall near 1 toward 0 across three, IV rise toward noise ceiling 2. falls IV rises planted rhythm made irregular, noise. rhythm repeats exactly returns ceiling 1 near-zero IV; day--day phase jitter pulls third RA shrinks; pure noise leaves near 0 IV ceiling near 2. metrics report exactly regularity put .
code: ts  <- seq(as.POSIXct("2024-01-01", tz = "UTC"), by = 60, length.out = 7 * 1440) hod <- as.numeric(format(ts, "%H")) + as.numeric(format(ts, "%M")) / 60 day <- (seq_along(ts) - 1) %/% 1440 set.seed(1)  regular  <- pmax(0, 100 + 80 * cos(2 * pi * (hod - 14) / 24)) + rnorm(length(ts), 0, 5) acro_jit <- (14 + rnorm(7, 0, 4))[day + 1]                      # acrophase wanders by day jittered <- pmax(0, 100 + 80 * cos(2 * pi * (hod - acro_jit) / 24)) + rnorm(length(ts), 0, 5) noise    <- pmax(0, rnorm(length(ts), 100, 80))  recover <- function(x) {   r <- circadian.rhythm(x, ts)   c(IS = r$IS, IV = r$IV, RA = r$RA) } knitr::kable(   rbind(regular = recover(regular), jittered = recover(jittered), noise = recover(noise)),   digits = 3,   caption = "IS falls and IV rises as a planted rhythm is made irregular, then noise." )

title: Nonstationary and complex rhythms
text: nonstationary file bundle, synthesise one: fourteen days one-minute epochs, active block day quiet nights, whose peak advances two half hours earlier across day 7, whose nights fragment recording goes . methods locate per-day landmarks read minute series directly; heavier decompositions read ten-minute binned copy, split getting-started vignette uses. double-plotted actogram tells story glance. active band sits clock time first week, steps left around day 7 peak moves earlier, later rows scatter nights fragment. Double-plotted actogram synthetic recording. activity band advances two half hours earlier around day 7, later days fragment. single cosine fit fourteen days represent either change.
code: set.seed(42) days <- 14 t    <- as.POSIXct("2024-03-04", tz = "UTC") + (seq_len(days * 1440) - 1) * 60 hour <- as.numeric(format(t, "%H")) + as.numeric(format(t, "%M")) / 60 day  <- (seq_along(t) - 1) %/% 1440 + 1  acro  <- 15 - 2.5 * pmin(pmax(day - 7, 0), 1)            # peak advances over day 7 block <- plogis(4 * cos(2 * pi * (hour - acro) / 24))    # active by day, quiet at night frag  <- (day - 1) / (days - 1)                          # 0 to 1 across the recording counts <- rpois(length(t), 5 + 300 * block) night_break <- runif(length(t)) < 0.10 * frag & block < 0.2 counts[night_break] <- rpois(sum(night_break), 150)      # nights fragment late on  agg      <- tapply(counts, as.integer(as.numeric(t) %/% 600), sum)   # 10-minute bins counts10 <- as.numeric(agg) t10      <- as.POSIXct(as.numeric(names(agg)) * 600, origin = "1970-01-01", tz = "UTC") plot_actogram(counts, t, scale = "sqrt")

title: Nonstationary and complex rhythms
text: usual descriptors still run, still return one answer whole recording. cosinor averages fourteen days onto single 24-hour profile fits one cosine , two phase regimes blur one smeared peak. nonparametric metrics give one interdaily-stability one intradaily-variability number: fragmentation pushes toward less stable, variable rhythm, single value compromise clean early days broken late ones, neither number says phase moved.
code: cosinor.analysis(counts, t, period = 24) #> Cosinor Analysis Results #>  #> Period:     24 hours #> N obs:      20160 (14.0 days) #>  #> Parameters: #>   MESOR:      157.59 (rhythm-adjusted mean) #>   Amplitude:  154.72 (half peak-to-trough) #>   Acrophase:  13:47 (13.78 h, time of peak) #>  #> Model Fit: #>   R-squared:  0.9901 #>   F-statistic: 1053.60 #>   P-value:    0e+00 rhythm <- circadian.rhythm(counts, t) c(IS = rhythm$IS, IV = rhythm$IV, RA = rhythm$RA) #>     IS     IV     RA  #> 0.8778 0.0858 0.8533

code: ssa <- circadian.ssa(counts10, t10) parts <- rbind(   data.frame(t = t10, value = ssa$circadian, part = "circadian"),   data.frame(t = t10, value = ssa$trend,     part = "trend"),   data.frame(t = t10, value = ssa$residual,  part = "residual")) parts$part <- factor(parts$part, c("circadian", "trend", "residual")) ggplot(parts, aes(t, value)) +   geom_line(colour = "#236192", linewidth = 0.3) +   facet_wrap(~part, ncol = 1, scales = "free_y") +   labs(x = NULL, y = NULL) +   theme_actiRhythm() emd <- circadian.emd(counts, t) hht <- hilbert.huang(emd) ggplot(data.frame(t = emd$times, period = hht$period), aes(t, period)) +   geom_line(colour = "#236192") +   geom_hline(yintercept = 24, linetype = 2, colour = "grey50") +   coord_cartesian(ylim = c(18, 30)) +   labs(x = NULL, y = "Instantaneous period (h)") +   theme_actiRhythm() #> Warning: Removed 1 row containing missing values or values outside the scale range #> (`geom_line()`).

text: trusting tools real data, watch recover period plant. build ten-day minute-level recording cosine whose period 25 hours, 24 (deliberately free-running tau), add noise. periodograms recover planted 25-hour period. land 25, conventional 24: method reports period put , one expected. confidence interval bracket planted value. period.ci() attaches circular moving-block bootstrap interval respects autocorrelation activity (Kunsch, 1989; Politis & Romano, 1992).
code: ts <- seq(as.POSIXct("2024-01-01", tz = "UTC"), by = 60, length.out = 10 * 1440) th <- as.numeric(difftime(ts, ts[1], units = "hours")) set.seed(1) tau_true <- 25 counts <- pmax(0, 100 + 80 * cos(2 * pi * (th - 8) / tau_true) + rnorm(length(th), 0, 15))  ls0  <- circadian.period(counts, ts) chi0 <- chi.sq.periodogram(counts, ts) knitr::kable(   data.frame(method   = c("Lomb-Scargle", "chi-square"),              planted  = c(tau_true, tau_true),              tau_h    = c(ls0$tau, chi0$period)),   digits = 2, caption = "Both periodograms recover the planted 25-hour period." ) ci <- period.ci(counts, ts, n_boot = 200, seed = 1) ci #> Circadian Period with Bootstrap Confidence Interval #>  #>   tau:      25.038 h #>   95% CI:   [24.981, 25.015] h #>   SE:       0.009 h #>   Method:   circular block residual bootstrap (200/200 valid reps) tau_true >= ci$ci_lower && tau_true <= ci$ci_upper   # the interval brackets the truth #> [1] TRUE

title: Phase, regularity, and social jet lag
text: Write recording binary sleep/wake matrix si,js_{,j}, ii indexes MM epochs day (clock-time bins) jj indexes NN calendar days. Sleep Regularity Index chance two consecutive days hold state clock time, rescaled [−100,100][-100, 100](Phillips et al., 2017): SRI=−100+200M(N−1)∑=1M∑j=1N−1𝟏{si,j=si,j+1}. \mathrm{SRI} = -100 + \frac{200}{M(N-1)}   \sum_{=1}^{M}\sum_{j=1}^{N-1} \mathbf{1}\!\left\{ s_{,j} = s_{,j+1} \right\}. Perfect repetition scores +100+100, independent (coin-flip) days score 00, perfectly inverted pattern scores −100-100. Social jet lag compares mid-point sleep free days (MSF) work days (MSW) (Wittmann et al., 2006): SJL=MSF−MSW. \mathrm{SJL} = \mathrm{MSF} - \mathrm{MSW}. free-day sleep often longer (catch-sleep), chronotype read sleep-debt-corrected mid-sleep MSFsc, shifts free-day mid-point back half excess free-day sleep weekly average (Roenneberg et al., 2003; Roenneberg et al., 2012): MSFsc=MSF−12(SDf−SDweek)SDf>SDw. \mathrm{MSF_{sc}} = \mathrm{MSF} - \tfrac{1}{2}\!\left(\mathrm{SD_f} - \mathrm{SD_{week}}\right) \quad\text{} \mathrm{SD_f} > \mathrm{SD_w}. phase-concentration tests, write day’s marker angle θj=2πtj/24\theta_j = 2\pi t_j / 24. mean resultant length R=‖N−1∑jeiθj‖R = \lVert N^{-1}\sum_j e^{\theta_j} \rVert measures clustering, Rayleigh statistic Z=NR2Z = N R^2 tests uniformity (Fisher, 1993). composite phase deviation combines day’s precision (deviation person’s mean phase) accuracy (deviation reference) CPD=precision2+accuracy2¯\mathrm{CPD} = \overline{\sqrt{\text{precision}^2 + \text{accuracy}^2}}(Fischer et al., 2016).

code: ts  <- seq(as.POSIXct("2024-01-01 00:00:00", tz = "UTC"), by = 60, length.out = 7 * 1440) tod <- as.POSIXlt(ts)$hour + as.POSIXlt(ts)$min / 60 day <- as.integer(as.Date(ts) - as.Date(ts[1])) set.seed(1)  # regular: the same nightly window; jittered: onset wanders; random: coin-flip epochs regular_counts  <- ifelse(tod >= 23 | tod < 7, rpois(length(ts), 2), rpois(length(ts), 250)) onset           <- (23 + round(rnorm(7, 0, 3)))[day + 1] jittered_counts <- ifelse((tod - onset) %% 24 < 8, rpois(length(ts), 2), rpois(length(ts), 250)) random_counts   <- ifelse(rbinom(length(ts), 1, 0.5) == 1, rpois(length(ts), 2), rpois(length(ts), 250))  sri_of <- function(counts) {   state <- sleep.cole.kripke(counts)   sleep.regularity.index(state, ts) } knitr::kable(   data.frame(pattern = c("regular", "jittered", "random"),              SRI = c(sri_of(regular_counts), sri_of(jittered_counts), sri_of(random_counts))),   digits = 2,   caption = "The SRI falls from its ceiling as a planted sleep pattern is made irregular, then random." )

Uncertainty mean phase. circadian.onset.ci() gives bootstrap confidence interval mean onset time, resampling per-day onsets circle interval correct even near midnight.
code: dates <- as.Date("2024-01-01") + 0:6 free  <- weekdays(dates) %in% c("Saturday", "Sunday") sleep_periods <- data.frame(   in_bed_time  = as.POSIXct(ifelse(free, paste(dates + 1, "01:00:00"),                                          paste(dates,     "23:00:00")), tz = "UTC"),   out_bed_time = as.POSIXct(ifelse(free, paste(dates + 1, "10:00:00"),                                          paste(dates + 1, "07:00:00")), tz = "UTC") ) sjl <- social.jet.lag(sleep_periods, work_days = !free) c(MSW = sjl$MSW_time, MSF = sjl$MSF_time, MSFsc = sjl$MSFsc_time,   SJL_hours = sjl$social_jet_lag_hours, SJLsc_hours = sjl$social_jet_lag_sc_hours) #>         MSW         MSF       MSFsc   SJL_hours SJLsc_hours  #>     "03:00"     "05:30"     "05:09"       "2.5"      "2.14" d <- as.Date(agd$timestamp) onsets <- vapply(split(seq_along(d), d), function(ix) {   if (length(ix) < 600) return(NA_real_)         # skip part-days   activity.onset.offset(agd$axis1[ix], agd$timestamp[ix])$onset_h }, numeric(1)) onsets <- onsets[!is.na(onsets)] round(onsets, 2) #> 2025-10-08 2025-10-09 2025-10-10 2025-10-11 2025-10-12 2025-10-13 2025-10-14  #>       6.67       6.40       7.07       7.83       5.78       6.57       6.00 phase.concentration(onsets) #> Phase Concentration Tests #>  #>   n days:          7 #>   Mean direction:  06.61 h    R: 0.986 #>   Rayleigh:        Z = 6.81, p = 0.0000 #>   Hermans-Rasson:  T = 34.4, p = 0.0005 df <- data.frame(date = as.Date(names(onsets)), onset = onsets) ggplot(df, aes(date, onset)) +   geom_hline(yintercept = mean(onsets), colour = "grey70", linetype = 2) +   geom_line(colour = "grey60") + geom_point(colour = "#236192", size = 2) +   labs(x = NULL, y = "Activity onset (h)") +   theme_actiRhythm() composite.phase.deviation(onsets) #> $CPD #> [1] 0.693 #>  #> $precision #> [1] 0.49 #>  #> $accuracy #> [1] 0.49 #>  #> $reference_phase #> [1] 6.615 #>  #> $n_days #> [1] 7 circadian.onset.ci(onsets) #> $mean_onset #> [1] 6.615 #>  #> $ci_lower #> [1] 6.177 #>  #> $ci_upper #> [1] 7.133 #>  #> $n_days #> [1] 7
