title: Phase, regularity, and social jet lag
text: Sleep Regularity Index follows Phillips et al. (2017) full epoch--day concordance form; social jet lag sleep-debt correction follow Wittmann et al. (2006) (2003; Roenneberg et al.; 2012). phase markers use relative-difference onset Roenneberg et al. (2003), composite phase deviation Fischer et al. (2016), circular tests Fisher (1993) Landler et al. (2019). actiRhythm’s SRI phase metrics cross-checked reference definitions Validation article package’s test suite.

title: From raw acceleration
text: Everything far runs activity counts. Raw accelerometer files also record gravity component, body posture, counts carry. actiRhythm reads three common formats: ActiGraph .gt3x, Axivity .cwa, GENEActiv .bin. raw.metrics() takes file path. Raw acceleration far large bundle, reproducible illustration example_raw() synthesises recording; real file read way, raw.metrics("recording.cwa"). ENMO activity signal, every method takes directly: pass circadian.rhythm(), let circadian.raw() run whole analysis file. Calibration applied first inside raw.metrics(). auto.calibrate() finds per-axis gain offset return still periods 1 g sphere (Hees et al., 2014); data known distortion returns original gain offset within fraction percent. z-angle supports sleep detector needs diary works posture, counts . rest.spt() finds nightly sleep-period-time window distribution angle change [HDCZA; Hees et al. (2018)], sib.vanhees() scores sustained-inactivity bouts (Hees et al., 2015), sleep..spt() combines onset, wake efficiency. detect.nonwear.raw() flags stationary, taken-device [low standard deviation range hour; Hees et al. (2011)], passing mask wear keeps device-stretch read one long night. Computed synthetic recording 5-second epoch, detected windows shaded. z-angle synthetic recording 5-second epochs, two nightly sleep-period-time windows detected angle alone (HDCZA), gated raw non-wear, shaded. Two estimators measure fragmentation, run counts already loaded: activity.balance.index() maps detrended-fluctuation exponent 0 1 score peaks healthy 1/f balance (Danilevicz et al., 2024), transition.probability() gives closed-form rest--active active--rest transition probabilities.
code: m <- raw.metrics(raw, epoch = 60)   # per-epoch ENMO (mg), MAD, and the z-angle head(m) #>                  time     ENMO      MAD    anglez #> 1 2024-01-01 12:00:00 43.95883 24.78791  8.280445 #> 2 2024-01-01 12:01:00 43.13860 24.89003 14.666095 #> 3 2024-01-01 12:02:00 44.56626 25.55921  8.287697 #> 4 2024-01-01 12:03:00 44.25995 24.90778 14.705513 #> 5 2024-01-01 12:04:00 44.79032 25.36485  8.292428 #> 6 2024-01-01 12:05:00 44.17071 24.72920 14.755796 cr <- circadian.rhythm(m$ENMO, m$time) c(IS = cr$IS, IV = cr$IV, RA = cr$RA) #>     IS     IV     RA  #> 1.0000 0.3823 0.9873 set.seed(1) u <- matrix(rnorm(40 * 3), 40, 3); u <- u / sqrt(rowSums(u^2)) cal <- do.call(rbind, lapply(seq_len(40), function(i)   matrix(rep(u[i, ] / c(1.03, 0.97, 1.01) + c(0.04, -0.03, 0.02), each = 300),          300, 3) + rnorm(900, 0, 0.004))) auto.calibrate(data.frame(x = cal[, 1], y = cal[, 2], z = cal[, 3]),                fs = 30)[c("scale", "offset")] #> $scale #> [1] 1.0301368 0.9701036 1.0099054 #>  #> $offset #> [1]  0.04000814 -0.02992012  0.01990532 m5    <- raw.metrics(raw, epoch = 5, metrics = "anglez") wear  <- detect.nonwear.raw(raw, epoch = 5) spt   <- rest.spt(m5$anglez, m5$time, epoch_length = 5, wear = wear) sib   <- sib.vanhees(m5$anglez, epoch_length = 5) sleep <- sleep.from.spt(spt, sib, m5$time, epoch_length = 5) thin <- seq(1, nrow(m5), by = 12) ggplot() +   geom_rect(data = spt, aes(xmin = onset, xmax = offset, ymin = -90, ymax = 90),             fill = "#236192", alpha = 0.18) +   geom_line(data = m5[thin, ], aes(time, anglez), linewidth = 0.2, colour = "grey30") +   labs(x = NULL, y = "z-angle (deg)") +   theme_actiRhythm() sleep[, c("date", "onset", "offset", "tst", "efficiency")] #>         date               onset              offset      tst efficiency #> 1 2024-01-01 2024-01-01 23:00:00 2024-01-02 07:00:00 8.001389  0.9968853 #> 2 2024-01-02 2024-01-02 23:00:00 2024-01-03 07:00:00 8.001389  0.9968853 activity.balance.index(fractal.dfa(agd$axis1)) #> $ABI_overall #> [1] 0.9900827 #>  #> $ABI_short #> [1] 0.9943423 #>  #> $ABI_long #> [1] 0.9820051 transition.probability(agd$axis1)[c("tp_ra_mle", "tp_ar_mle")] #> $tp_ra_mle #> [1] 0.04853705 #>  #> $tp_ar_mle #> [1] 0.1366603

title: From raw acceleration
text: raw metrics z-angle reimplement van Hees algorithms GGIR established (Migueles et al., 2019), reproduce GGIR’s output, merely internally consistent. check runs comparison directly: computes GGIR’s per-epoch ENMO z-angle (GGIR::g.getmeta()) actiRhythm’s (raw.metrics()) real wrist recording measures closely agree. recording lives package’s data-raw/ folder (kept source repository excluded CRAN build, since multi-day raw file far large ship), table computed whenever repository GGIR present skipped otherwise (gt3x_file resolved setup chunk). Per-epoch agreement GGIR 118,800 matched 5-second epochs. recording present, table shows two implementations tracking almost exactly whole file; small residual likely epoch-edge rounding rather difference algorithm. actiRhythm’s van Hees auto-calibration brings recording onto 1 g sphere first (calibration demo recovers planted gain offset), lets ENMO values line . agreement holds sleep-period (SPT) window. Feeding z-angle actiRhythm’s rest.spt() GGIR’s HDCZA detector (GGIR:::HASPT) isolates detection algorithm upstream metric table already validated, difference algorithm . SPT window: actiRhythm rest.spt() vs GGIR HASPT (HDCZA) z-angle. recording present, table shows two implementations placing window within single 5-second epoch every night. long-duration nights parked-device tail (watch lying still came ); wear-time screen removes , two detectors still agree raw algorithm returns , point check. sleep parameters sleep..spt() derives (total sleep time, WASO, efficiency) built window, inherit agreement. ActiGraph idle-sleep gaps imputed zeros read.gt3x, otherwise collapse z-angle constant quiescent periods. Like GGIR, actiRhythm carries last gravity vector forward gaps, angle stays correct still periods sleep detector relies .
code: P <- GGIR::load_params() P$params_general$windowsizes <- c(5, 900, 3600) I <- GGIR::g.inspectfile(gt3x_file, params_rawdata = P$params_rawdata,                          params_general = P$params_general) ggir <- GGIR::g.getmeta(gt3x_file, params_rawdata = P$params_rawdata,                         params_general = P$params_general,                         params_cleaning = P$params_cleaning,                         inspectfileobject = I)$metashort #>  #> Loading chunk: 1 2 3 acti <- raw.metrics(gt3x_file, epoch = 5)  # Align on the 5-second wall-clock label, then compare per epoch. gg <- data.frame(k = gsub("T", " ", substr(as.character(ggir$timestamp), 1, 19)),                  enmo = ggir$ENMO * 1000, anglez = ggir$anglez) aa <- data.frame(k = format(acti$time, "%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S"),                  enmo = acti$ENMO, anglez = acti$anglez) m  <- merge(gg, aa, by = "k", suffixes = c("_ggir", "_acti"))  knitr::kable(   data.frame(     metric = c("ENMO (mg)", "z-angle (deg)"),     cor    = c(cor(m$enmo_ggir, m$enmo_acti), cor(m$anglez_ggir, m$anglez_acti)),     mad    = c(mean(abs(m$enmo_ggir - m$enmo_acti)),                mean(abs(m$anglez_ggir - m$anglez_acti)))   ),   digits = c(0, 4, 2),   col.names = c("Metric", "Correlation with GGIR", "Mean abs. difference"),   caption = sprintf("Per-epoch agreement with GGIR over %s matched 5-second epochs.",                     format(nrow(m), big.mark = ",")) ) ps  <- GGIR::load_params()$params_sleep spt <- rest.spt(acti$anglez, acti$time, epoch_length = 5)   # acti is from the chunk above day <- as.Date(acti$time - 12 * 3600)                       # noon-to-noon nights  ggir_spt <- do.call(rbind, lapply(unique(day), function(dd) {   idx <- which(day == dd)   if (length(idx) < 720) return(NULL)   h <- GGIR:::HASPT(acti$anglez[idx], params_sleep = ps, ws3 = 5,                     HASPT.algo = "HDCZA", invalid = rep(0L, length(idx)))   if (length(h$SPTE_start) == 0 || is.na(h$SPTE_start)) return(NULL)   data.frame(date = as.Date(dd, origin = "1970-01-01"),              ggir_onset  = acti$time[idx[round(h$SPTE_start)]],              ggir_offset = acti$time[idx[round(h$SPTE_end)]]) }))  cmp <- merge(spt[, c("date", "onset", "offset", "duration")], ggir_spt, by = "date") cmp$onset_diff_s  <- round(as.numeric(difftime(cmp$onset,  cmp$ggir_onset,  units = "secs"))) cmp$offset_diff_s <- round(as.numeric(difftime(cmp$offset, cmp$ggir_offset, units = "secs"))) knitr::kable(   cmp[, c("date", "duration", "onset_diff_s", "offset_diff_s")],   digits = 2,   col.names = c("Night", "SPT duration (h)", "Onset diff (s)", "Offset diff (s)"),   caption = "SPT window: actiRhythm rest.spt() vs GGIR HASPT (HDCZA) on the same z-angle." )

what: The idea, and a rule to remember
title: Sleep and rest detection
text: Sleep rest detection turns stream activity counts answer two different questions. epoch scorers (Cole-Kripke Sadeh) label every single minute sleep wake, giving per-epoch state can total sleep time. bout detectors (change-point, Roenneberg, Crespo, state-space model) step back mark spans rest, onset offset clock times consolidated period. One tells much sleep, happened. rule carry article: decide first whether want one main sleep window per night every rest bout recording contains, choice, algorithm’s accuracy, separates functions. sleep.changepoints() finds single dominant rest bout circadian cycle; rest.periods() rest.crespo() return every consolidated bout, naps included. Asking one-bout method naps, many-bout method “night”, common way misread output.

title: Sleep and rest detection
text: two epoch scorers weighted windows counts. Cole-Kripke (Cole et al., 1992) forms sleep index seven-epoch window: four epochs current one (P4…P1P_4 \dots P_1), current epoch CC, two (N1,N2N_1, N_2). Counts scaled 100 capped: D=0.001(106P4+54P3+58P2+76P1+230C+74N1+67N2), D = 0.001\,(106\,P_4 + 54\,P_3 + 58\,P_2 + 76\,P_1 + 230\,C + 74\,N_1 + 67\,N_2),  scores epoch sleep D<1D < 1. Webster’s rescoring rules (Webster et al., 1982) re-label short sleep bouts bracketed sustained wake. Sadeh (Sadeh et al., 1994) uses eleven-epoch window (five side) counts capped 300: SI=7.601−0.065A¯−1.08NAT−0.056s−0.703log⁡(C+1), SI = 7.601 - 0.065\,\overline{} - 1.08\,\mathrm{NAT} - 0.056\,s - 0.703\,\log(C+1),  ¯\overline{} window mean, NAT\mathrm{NAT} count epochs [50,100)[50,100), ss standard deviation current five preceding epochs, CC current count. epoch scored sleep SI>−4SI > -4. change-point detector (Chen & Sun, 2024) geometric rather weighted: fits 24-hour cosinor bound rest active span roughly, places exact transition inside bound split kk minimising within-segment residual sum squares, $$ k^\* = \arg\min_k \;\sum_{\le k}(x_i - \bar x_{\le k})^2 + \sum_{> k}(x_i - \bar x_{>k})^2 . $$

title: Sleep and rest detection
text: One-minute epochs. Cole-Kripke Sadeh validated 60-second epochs coefficients tied resolution; warn epoch_length differs. Re-bin one minute scoring. Counts, raw acceleration. scorers expect ActiGraph-style activity counts vertical axis; feed counts (agd$axis1), raw g. Non-wear sleep. taken-device reads deep rest. scorers’ na_action argument governs : default "na" emits NA missing-count epochs device-gap silently scored sleep. clear day-night contrast bout detectors. sleep.changepoints(), rest.periods(), rest.crespo() need enough rhythm separate rest activity; flat short record return empty result rather erroring.

title: Sleep and rest detection
text: trusting real data, plant answer check comes back. build seven-day recording known nightly sleep window 23:00 07:00 (near-zero activity night, busy day) ask whether epoch scorers label window sleep whether sleep.changepoints() recovers 23:00 onset 07:00 wake. scorers label planted 23:00-07:00 window sleep day wake. scorers tag essentially night sleep essentially day wake. Now change-point detector, place onset near 23:00 wake near 07:00 every night: Mean recovered transition time across seven nights vs planted values. recovered onset wake land within minute planted 23:00 07:00, detector finds exactly seven nightly episodes, mean duration eight hours built . method recovers truth planted.
code: ts  <- seq(as.POSIXct("2024-01-01", tz = "UTC"), by = 60, length.out = 7 * 1440) hod <- as.numeric(format(ts, "%H")) + as.numeric(format(ts, "%M")) / 60 set.seed(1) asleep <- hod >= 23 | hod < 7                       # the planted sleep window counts <- ifelse(asleep, 0, 250) + pmax(0, rnorm(length(ts), 0, 8))  ck <- sleep.cole.kripke(counts) sd <- sleep.sadeh(counts) knitr::kable(   data.frame(     scorer        = c("Cole-Kripke", "Sadeh"),     sleep_recall  = c(mean(ck[asleep] == "S"),  mean(sd[asleep] == "S")),     wake_recall   = c(mean(ck[!asleep] == "W"), mean(sd[!asleep] == "W"))),   digits = 3,   caption = "Both scorers label the planted 23:00-07:00 window sleep and the day wake." ) cp <- sleep.changepoints(counts, ts) cps_h <- as.numeric(format(cp$changepoints$time, "%H")) +          as.numeric(format(cp$changepoints$time, "%M")) / 60 knitr::kable(   data.frame(     transition = c("sleep onset", "wake onset"),     planted    = c(23, 7),     recovered  = c(mean(cps_h[cp$changepoints$type == "sleep onset"]),                    mean(cps_h[cp$changepoints$type == "wake onset"]))),   digits = 2,   caption = "Mean recovered transition time across the seven nights vs the planted values." ) c(n_episodes = cp$n_episodes, mean_sleep_duration_h = cp$mean_sleep_duration) #>            n_episodes mean_sleep_duration_h  #>                     6                     8

title: Sleep and rest detection
text: bundled recording runs calls. two scorers return per-epoch state vector; sleep.changepoints() returns per-night episode table. two scorers agree closely total sleep fraction, change-point detector resolves one main sleep episode per night onset wake clock times.
code: agd <- agd.counts(read.agd(example_agd(1), verbose = FALSE)) table(cole_kripke = sleep.cole.kripke(agd$axis1)) #> cole_kripke #>    S    W  #> 7646 2273 table(sadeh       = sleep.sadeh(agd$axis1)) #> sadeh #>    S    W  #> 7190 2729 cp_real <- sleep.changepoints(agd$axis1, agd$timestamp) cp_real #> Change-Point Sleep/Wake Detection (CircaCP) #>  #>   Span:           6.9 days (9919 epochs) #>   Cosinor acrophase: 20.5 h #>   Change points:  14 (7 sleep episodes) #>   Mean sleep duration: 11.1 h #>  #>   First sleep episodes: #>     sleep 10-07 22:36  ->  wake 10-08 07:09  (8.6 h) #>     sleep 10-08 23:30  ->  wake 10-09 07:50  (8.3 h) #>     sleep 10-09 21:30  ->  wake 10-10 12:11  (14.7 h) #>  #>   Reference: Chen and Sun (2024)

title: Sleep and rest detection
text: sleep_state (“S”/“W”) scorers per-epoch label. useful summaries count sleep epochs (multiply epoch length sleep time) runs consecutive sleep (sleep periods lengths). Onset wake times sleep.changepoints() clock times; read “main rest bout begin end”, read mean_sleep_duration average nightly rest length. Cole-Kripke vs Sadeh. Cole-Kripke validated adults (Cole et al., 1992), Sadeh children adolescents (Sadeh et al., 1994); pick population. agree epoch--epoch, disagreement largest sleep-wake boundary, middle night.

what: One bout per night, or every bout? The rest detectors compared
title: Sleep and rest detection
text: rule action, stands usual “wrong-way” demo: wrong algorithm , wrong question. recording, run four detectors, gives four different bout counts, answer different questions, three wrong. Four detectors, four counts: difference question, accuracy. sleep.changepoints() (Chen & Sun, 2024) returns one episode per circadian cycle: single dominant night-time rest bout. rest.periods() (Loock et al., 2021; Roenneberg et al., 2015) rest.crespo() (Crespo et al., 2012) instead consolidate every rest bout, daytime nap fragmented night becomes row. reach result two independent routes, Roenneberg’s trend--correlation consolidation versus Crespo’s median-filter--morphology pipeline, bout counts differ even though aim catch rest. rest.hmm() (Huang et al., 2018) state-space alternative: rather thresholding epoch, fits Gaussian hidden Markov model whose latent rest state persists, reports 24-hour rest-occupancy profile. HMM’s probability rest state across day. state-space model infers rest band, centred night, without fixed count threshold. lesson rule: report sleep.changepoints() reporting night; report rest.periods() rest.crespo() reporting rest, naps . Choose one whose question matches , rather treating differing bout counts contradiction.
code: cp <- sleep.changepoints(agd$axis1, agd$timestamp)   # one main bout per night rp <- rest.periods(agd$axis1, agd$timestamp)         # every bout (Roenneberg/MASDA) rc <- rest.crespo(agd$axis1, agd$timestamp)          # every bout (Crespo morphology) hm <- rest.hmm(agd$axis1, agd$timestamp, seed = 1)   # state-space alternative  knitr::kable(   data.frame(     detector = c("sleep.changepoints", "rest.periods", "rest.crespo", "rest.hmm"),     question = c("one main bout / night", "every bout (naps incl.)",                  "every bout (naps incl.)", "latent rest state"),     bouts    = c(cp$n_episodes, rp$n_bouts, rc$n_rest_periods, NA),     family   = c("CircaCP", "Roenneberg", "Crespo", "Gaussian HMM")),   caption = "Four detectors, four counts: the difference is the question, not the accuracy." ) ggplot(hm$tod_profile, aes(hour, p_rest)) +   geom_col(fill = "#236192") +   scale_x_continuous(breaks = seq(0, 24, 6)) +   labs(x = "Hour of day", y = "P(rest state)") +   theme_actiRhythm()

title: Sleep and rest detection
text: state-space view detail. Beyond occupancy profile , rest.hmm() returns per-state emission means, transition matrix, decoded state_path sleep_state vector, plus AIC/BIC choosing two- three-state model (Huang et al., 2018). ultradian structure within sleep. sleep windows, lids() describes sleep-cycle oscillation inside . needs sleep_periods data frame in_bed_time out_bed_time columns (build directly change-point episodes), transforms activity locomotor inactivity (100/(activity+1)100/(\text{activity}+1)), fits ultradian cosine, reporting best period Munich Rhythmicity Index (Winnebeck et al., 2018). mean LIDS period lands ninety-minute--three-hour band expected human sleep cycles, recovered inactivity signal alone.
code: hm$emission[, c("state", "label", "mean_transformed")] #>   state  label mean_transformed #> 1     1   rest    6.326781e-295 #> 2     2 active     2.812874e+01 c(time_at_rest = mean(hm$state_path == 1L),   rest_persistence = hm$transition[1, 1], AIC = hm$AIC) #>     time_at_rest rest_persistence              AIC  #>     7.373727e-01     9.514564e-01    -1.604721e+05 sleep_periods <- data.frame(   in_bed_time  = cp$sleep_episodes$sleep_onset,   out_bed_time = cp$sleep_episodes$wake_onset) li <- lids(agd$axis1, agd$timestamp, sleep_periods) li #> Locomotor Inactivity During Sleep (LIDS) #>  #>   Sleep periods:    7 #>   Mean LIDS period: 167.9 min #>   Mean MRI:         2.844

code: cmp <- data.frame(   metric     = c("IS", "IV"),   actiRhythm = c(cr$IS, cr$IV),   ActCR      = c(ActCR::IS(hourly), ActCR::IV(c(t(hourly)))) ) cmp$abs_diff <- abs(cmp$actiRhythm - cmp$ActCR) knitr::kable(cmp, digits = 5, caption = "IS and IV: actiRhythm vs ActCR.")

title: Validation against reference packages
text: nparACT free reference implementation full nonparametric battery: , IV, RA, L5, M10 (Van Someren et al., 1999; Witting et al., 1990). reads time / activity data frame name global environment sampling rate Hz. chunk reproduces package’s test-circadian-nparact.R: run nparACT installed prints agreement (/RA two decimals, L5/M10 within nparACT’s rounding).
code: assign("np_validation_df", data.frame(time = ts, activity = x), envir = globalenv()) np <- nparACT::nparACT_base("np_validation_df", SR = 1 / 60, plot = FALSE) rm("np_validation_df", envir = globalenv())  cmp <- data.frame(   metric     = c("IS", "IV", "RA", "L5", "M10"),   actiRhythm = c(cr$IS, cr$IV, cr$RA, cr$L5, cr$M10),   nparACT    = c(np$IS, np$IV, np$RA, np$L5, np$M10) ) cmp$abs_diff <- abs(cmp$actiRhythm - cmp$nparACT) knitr::kable(cmp, digits = 3, caption = "IS/IV/RA/L5/M10: actiRhythm vs nparACT.")

what: Single cosinor versus the cosinor package
title: Validation against reference packages
text: cosinor.analysis() fits single-component cosinor Cornelissen (2014). cosinor package fits model formula interface; given averaged daily profile, MESOR amplitude match exactly. Single cosinor: actiRhythm vs cosinor package.
code: ca   <- cosinor.analysis(x, ts) prof <- data.frame(hour = rep(0:23, nrow(hourly)), Y = c(t(hourly))) cl   <- cosinor::cosinor.lm(Y ~ time(hour) + 1, period = 24, data = prof)  cmp <- data.frame(   parameter  = c("MESOR", "amplitude"),   actiRhythm = c(ca$mesor, ca$amplitude),   cosinor    = c(unname(cl$coefficients[1]), unname(cl$coefficients[2])) ) cmp$abs_diff <- abs(cmp$actiRhythm - cmp$cosinor) knitr::kable(cmp, digits = 5, caption = "Single cosinor: actiRhythm vs the cosinor package.")

title: Validation against reference packages
text: cosinor.antilogistic() fits sigmoidally transformed cosine Marler et al. (2006), model ActCR::ActExtendCosinor(). validate controlled profile moderate transition, every shape parameter identifiable. recording near-square days, steepness beta weakly constrained rest-activity transition sharp, likelihood nearly flat beta; parameters still agree closely beta pinned , show well-posed case . Anti-logistic cosinor: actiRhythm vs ActExtendCosinor.
code: # A 3-day synthetic recording whose average day is a moderate sigmoid. set.seed(1) n   <- 1440 * 3 tse <- seq(as.POSIXct("2024-01-01", tz = "UTC"), by = 60, length.out = n) hr  <- as.numeric(format(tse, "%H")) + as.numeric(format(tse, "%M")) / 60 ctp <- cos((hr - 15) * 2 * pi / 24) ce  <- 50 + 250 * plogis(3 * (ctp - 0.1)) + rnorm(n, 0, 12) ce[ce < 0] <- 0  # The 1440-minute averaged profile ActExtendCosinor consumes. idx  <- as.integer(format(tse, "%H")) * 60 + as.integer(format(tse, "%M")) prof1440 <- vapply(0:1439, function(i) mean(ce[idx == i], na.rm = TRUE), numeric(1))  fit <- cosinor.antilogistic(ce, tse) ref <- ActCR::ActExtendCosinor(prof1440, window = 1)$params  cmp <- data.frame(   parameter  = c("minimum", "amplitude", "alpha", "beta", "acrotime"),   actiRhythm = c(fit$minimum, fit$amplitude, fit$alpha, fit$beta, fit$acrophase),   ActCR      = c(ref$minimum, ref$amp, ref$alpha, ref$beta, ref$acrotime) ) cmp$abs_diff <- abs(cmp$actiRhythm - cmp$ActCR) knitr::kable(cmp, digits = 3, caption = "Anti-logistic cosinor: actiRhythm vs ActExtendCosinor.")

Every analysis returns typed object prints metrics plots one call. can usually see answer computing . Plot recording actogram regular sleeper forms single vertical band activity clock time day, drifting rhythm scatters across panel. methods follow put numbers picture shows, carries reference defined , Witting (1990) van Someren (1999) cosinor periodogram.

title: Circadian Rest-Activity Rhythm Analysis
text: One ActiGraph recording ships package, runs without data . bundled file single de-identified 60-second-epoch recording, included examples run. actogram usually first thing worth plotting. Double-plotted actogram: row one day shown twice across 48 hours, stable rhythm forms vertical band activity clock time day, scattered fill marks fragmented shifting rhythm. recording high relative amplitude near 0.98 sits low interdaily stability near 0.23: days strongly active, pattern land clock time one day next. Every number figure regenerates installed package bundled recording, calculations behind exercised package’s test suite.
code: library(actiRhythm)  # Read the bundled .agd and pull the count series agd <- agd.counts(read.agd(example_agd(1), verbose = FALSE))  # Describe the rest-activity rhythm without assuming a waveform rhythm <- circadian.rhythm(agd$axis1, agd$timestamp) rhythm #>  #> Circadian Rhythm Analysis #>  #> Data Summary #>   Days analyzed:            8 #>   Valid circadian days:     6 #>   Epoch length:             60 seconds #>  #> Non-Parametric Metrics (IS/IV: Witting et al. 1990; RA/L5/M10: van Someren et al. 1999) #>   L5 (least active 5h):     6.10 counts/min, onset 01:28 #>   M10 (most active 10h):    602.93 counts/min, onset 12:02 #>   L1 (least active 1h):     3.10 counts/min, onset 02:35 #>   M1 (most active 1h):      1175.51 counts/min, onset 17:43 #>   Relative Amplitude (RA):  0.9800 (range 0-1, higher=stronger rhythm) #>   Interdaily Stability (IS): 0.2279 (range 0-1, higher=more consistent) #>   Intradaily Variability (IV): 1.0008 (~0=sine, ~2=noise) #>   Phi (autocorrelation):    0.4151 (higher=more predictable) #>  #> Sleep-Based & Variability Metrics #>   Sleep Regularity Index:   Not calculated (requires sleep_state input) #>   Onset timing variability: 2.23 hours #>   L5 timing variability:    0.77 hours (circular SD) #>   M10 timing variability:   3.70 hours (circular SD) #>  #> References: Witting (1990), van Someren (1999) plot_actogram(agd$axis1, agd$timestamp)

title: Circadian Rest-Activity Rhythm Analysis
text: recording runs rest package. cosinor.analysis() fits 24-hour cosine (Cornelissen 2014) rhythmicity.test() checks whether rhythm statistically real. circadian.period() estimates free-running period Lomb-Scargle periodogram (Lomb 1976), period.ci() puts bootstrap confidence interval , circadian.spectrogram() shows period drifts across recording. fractal.dfa() measures long-range correlation series (Peng 1994), multiscale.entropy() mfdfa() describe finer nonlinear structure. circadian.flm() fits functional (multi-harmonic) model daily profile circadian.ssa() decomposes recording trend, circadian, noise components. sleep.regularity.index() (Phillips 2017), social.jet.lag(), state.transitions() summarise sleep timing rest-activity fragmentation, sleep.changepoints() locates night’s sleep wake onset directly counts (Chen Sun 2024). sleep.cole.kripke() sleep.sadeh() score epoch sleep wake (Cole et al. 1992; Sadeh et al. 1994), rest.periods() (Roenneberg et al. 2015) rest.crespo() (Crespo et al. 2012) consolidate every rest bout across recording, naps included, two independent algorithms. set methods covers harder cases. circadian.wavelet() gives time-frequency power surface, ultradian.bandpower() dyadic ultradian bands, circadian.emd() hilbert.huang() data-adaptive decomposition instantaneous phase. cosinor.multicomponent() fits multi-harmonic shape, activity.onset.offset() marks daily onset offset, phase.concentration() tests day--day phase clustering. rest.hmm() state-space rest-activity model, curve.registration() aligns daily profiles scale-invariant chronotype phase, residual.spectrum() spectra cosinor leaves behind. circadian.daily(), intradaily.variability.multiscale(), activity.extrema(), dichotomy.index() complete nonparametric set. Counts can come .agd files: read.actigraph.csv() reads ActiLife epoch CSVs counts..data.frame() takes count series data frame. read.raw() turns raw ActiGraph .gt3x, Axivity .cwa, GENEActiv .bin files counts agcounts band-pass filter, gt3x.counts(), axivity.counts(), geneactiv.counts() per brand. Cross-brand counts approximation rather native ActiGraph output. detect.nonwear.choi() detect.nonwear.troiano() flag non-wear time directly counts use wear filter (Choi et al. 2011; Troiano et al. 2008). Raw acceleration also gives gravity-based metrics counts carry. raw.metrics() returns per-epoch ENMO, MAD z-angle van Hees (2014) auto-calibration, circadian.raw() runs every method ENMO single call. z-angle also supports sleep pipeline needs diary, counts . rest.spt() finds nightly sleep-period window (HDCZA, van Hees 2018) sib.vanhees() scores sustained-inactivity bouts (van Hees 2015); sleep..spt() turns onset, wake, WASO efficiency. detect.nonwear.raw() gates device taken left still, read sleep (van Hees 2011). activity.balance.index() transition.probability() add two fragmentation estimators. study many files, circadian.batch() runs whole pipeline folder returns one row per recording, circadian.workbook() writes full analysis multi-sheet Excel file. Every plotting function returns ggplot object can theme save save.circadian.plot().

title: Generalized Least- and Most-Active Periods (MX / LX) — activity.extrema
code: ts <- seq(as.POSIXct("2024-01-01", tz = "UTC"), by = 60, length.out = 4 * 1440) h  <- as.numeric(format(ts, "%H")) activity.extrema(ifelse(h >= 23 | h < 7, 5, 300), ts, windows = c(5, 8, 10)) #> Least / Most Active Periods (MX / LX) #>  #>   L5: 5 at 00.0h   M5: 300 at 07.0h #>   L8: 5 at 23.0h   M8: 300 at 07.0h #>   L10: 64 at 21.0h   M10: 300 at 07.0h #>

what: Auto-Calibrate Raw Acceleration to the Unit Gravity Sphere — auto.calibrate
title: Auto-Calibrate Raw Acceleration to the Unit Gravity Sphere — auto.calibrate
text: Estimates per-axis gain offset corrections van Hees et al. (2014) method: non-movement windows acceleration vector lie 1 g sphere, gain/offset fit iteratively projecting non-movement window means onto closest point unit sphere. Apply (raw - offset) * scale.

title: Auto-Calibrate Raw Acceleration to the Unit Gravity Sphere — auto.calibrate
code: auto.calibrate(   xyz,   fs,   sphere_crit = 0.3,   sd_crit = 0.013,   max_iter = 1000,   tol = 1e-09 )

title: Auto-Calibrate Raw Acceleration to the Unit Gravity Sphere — auto.calibrate
text: xyz data frame matrix raw acceleration g (columns x, y, z). fs Sample rate Hz. sphere_crit Minimum coverage (g) axis must span sides zero stable fit (default 0.3). sd_crit Per-axis rolling SD (g) 10-second window counts non-movement (default 0.013). max_iter Maximum refinement iterations (default 1000). tol Convergence tolerance calibration error (default 1e-9).

title: Auto-Calibrate Raw Acceleration to the Unit Gravity Sphere — auto.calibrate
text: list scale offset (length-3), calibration error (cal_error_start, cal_error_end, mean absolute deviation 1 g), npoints, calibrated flag. little non-movement data identity correction returned calibrated = FALSE.

title: Auto-Calibrate Raw Acceleration to the Unit Gravity Sphere — auto.calibrate
text: van Hees VT, Fang Z, Langford J, Assah F, Mohammad , da Silva ICM, Trenell MI, White T, Wareham NJ, Brage S (2014). “Autocalibration accelerometer data free-living physical activity assessment using local gravity temperature: evaluation four continents.” Journal Applied Physiology, 117(7), 738–744. doi:10.1152/japplphysiol.00421.2014 .

title: Auto-Calibrate Raw Acceleration to the Unit Gravity Sphere — auto.calibrate
code: # Recover a known per-axis gain and offset from non-movement windows set.seed(1) u <- matrix(rnorm(40 * 3), 40, 3); u <- u / sqrt(rowSums(u^2))   # sphere directions raw <- do.call(rbind, lapply(seq_len(40), function(i)   matrix(rep(u[i, ] / c(1.03, 0.97, 1.01) + c(0.04, -0.03, 0.02), each = 300),          300, 3) + rnorm(900, 0, 0.004))) auto.calibrate(data.frame(x = raw[, 1], y = raw[, 2], z = raw[, 3]), fs = 30)$scale #> [1] 1.0301368 0.9701036 1.0099054

title: Chi-square (Sokolove-Bushell) Periodogram — chi.sq.periodogram
text: Estimates dominant period activity time series Sokolove-Bushell (1978) chi-square periodogram, periodogram widely used chronobiology actigraphy. pairs Lomb-Scargle estimator circadian.period: Lomb-Scargle least-squares spectral method unevenly sampled data; chi-square periodogram analysis--variance method regularly sampled data reports significance threshold.

title: Chi-square (Sokolove-Bushell) Periodogram — chi.sq.periodogram
code: chi.sq.periodogram(   counts,   timestamps,   from = 18,   to = 30,   alpha = 0.05,   epoch_length = NULL )

title: Chi-square (Sokolove-Bushell) Periodogram — chi.sq.periodogram
text: counts Numeric vector activity counts regular epoch grid. NA values (e.g. non-wear) handled removal phase grand means. timestamps POSIXct vector (numeric seconds) length counts; used infer epoch length. , Period search window hours (default 18 30). alpha Significance level chi-square threshold (default 0.05). epoch_length Epoch length seconds. NULL (default) inferred median spacing timestamps.

title: Chi-square (Sokolove-Bushell) Periodogram — chi.sq.periodogram
text: named list period (hours, \(Q_P\) peak), Qp_peak, p_value (family-wise Sidak p-value peak across scanned periods), significant (logical, p_value < alpha), scanned (trial periods hours), Qp (periodogram aligned scanned), critical (per-period chi-square threshold), epoch_length alpha. insufficient/degenerate data shape returned period/Qp_peak NA empty vectors; function never throws.

title: Chi-square (Sokolove-Bushell) Periodogram — chi.sq.periodogram
text: trial period \(P\) (integer number epochs) series folded \(P\) phase bins \(K = \lfloor N/P \rfloor\) complete cycles statistic $$Q_P = \frac{N \sum_{h=1}^{P}(\bar{}_h - \bar{})^2}{\sum_{=1}^{N}(A_i - \bar{})^2}$$ computed, \(\bar{}_h\) mean phase \(h\) \(\bar{}\) grand mean \(N = KP\) retained points. null hypothesis rhythm \(P\), \(Q_P\) follows chi-square distribution \(P-1\) degrees freedom, giving significance line \(\chi^2_{P-1,\,1-\alpha}\). estimated period \(P\) maximising \(Q_P\) within search window.

title: Chi-square (Sokolove-Bushell) Periodogram — chi.sq.periodogram
text: Sokolove PG, Bushell WN (1978). “chi square periodogram: utility analysis circadian rhythms.” Journal Theoretical Biology, 72(1), 131–160. doi:10.1016/0022-5193(78)90022-X . Refinetti R, Cornelissen G, Halberg F (2007). “Procedures numerical analysis circadian rhythms.” Biological Rhythm Research, 38(4), 275–325. doi:10.1080/09291010600903692 . Sidak Z (1967). “Rectangular confidence regions means multivariate normal distributions.” Journal American Statistical Association, 62(318), 626–633. doi:10.1080/01621459.1967.10482935 .

Van Someren EJW, Swaab DF, Colenda CC, Cohen W, McCall WV, Rosenquist PB (1999). “Bright light therapy: improved sensitivity effects rest-activity rhythms Alzheimer patients application nonparametric methods.” Chronobiology International, 16(4), 505–518. doi:10.3109/07420529908998724 . what: Fractal and Complexity Metrics for Activity Time Series — circadian-fractal
title: Fractal and Complexity Metrics for Activity Time Series — circadian-fractal
text: Two nonlinear complexity scaling metrics accelerometer activity time series: Detrended Fluctuation Analysis (DFA) Multiscale Sample Entropy (MSE). run base R (stats used) add new runtime dependency. measure temporal structure (long-range correlations information-theoretic complexity) minute-level activity counts, alongside amplitude- timing-based circadian metrics circadian.rhythm.

title: Endogenous Circadian Period Estimation (Lomb-Scargle) — circadian-period
text: Estimates dominant (endogenous) circadian PERIOD (tau) activity time series using Lomb-Scargle periodogram. Unlike classical Fast Fourier Transform (FFT), Lomb-Scargle method require evenly sampled data, correctly handles irregular gappy sampling results non-wear periods, dropped epochs, mixed epoch lengths accelerometer recordings.

title: Circadian Rhythm Visualizations — circadian-plots
text: ggplot2 visualizations circadian / chronobiology metrics produced actiRhythm. functions thin plotting wrappers around existing analytic engines add new fitting logic: plot_periodogram draws Lomb-Scargle periodogram highlights endogenous period estimated circadian.period. plot_extended_cosinor overlays Marler extended (anti-logistic) cosinor fit cosinor.antilogistic ordinary cosinor cosinor.analysis averaged 24-hour activity profile. plot_dfa draws detrended-fluctuation log-log scaling plot fractal.dfa. Every function returns ggplot object, never errors degenerate insufficient input (returns annotated empty plot instead), falls back ggplot2::theme_minimal() package theme helpers unavailable.

what: Empirical Mode Decomposition of the Activity Rhythm — circadian.emd
title: Empirical Mode Decomposition of the Activity Rhythm — circadian.emd
text: Decomposes activity series intrinsic mode functions empirical mode decomposition, data-adaptive, nonlinear alternative linear SSA decomposition (Huang et al. 1998). intrinsic mode function whose period nearest 24 hours taken circadian component. Optional ensemble EMD adds noise reduce mode mixing (Wu Huang 2009).

title: Empirical Mode Decomposition of the Activity Rhythm — circadian.emd
code: circadian.emd(   counts,   timestamps,   max_imf = 10L,   ensemble = 1L,   noise_sd = 0.2,   period_range = c(20, 28),   epoch_length = 60,   seed = NULL )

text: counts Numeric activity vector (coarse epoch recommended speed). timestamps POSIXct timestamps, one per value. max_imf Maximum number modes extract (default 10). ensemble Ensemble size EEMD (default 1 = plain EMD). noise_sd Added-noise SD fraction series SD (default 0.2). period_range Period window (hours) circadian mode (default 20 28). epoch_length Epoch length seconds (default 60). seed Optional seed EEMD noise.

title: Empirical Mode Decomposition of the Activity Rhythm — circadian.emd
text: Huang NE, Shen Z, Long SR, Wu MC, Shih HH, Zheng Q, Yen NC, Tung CC, Liu HH (1998). “empirical mode decomposition Hilbert spectrum nonlinear non-stationary time series analysis.” Proceedings Royal Society , 454(1971), 903–995. doi:10.1098/rspa.1998.0193 . Wu Z, Huang NE (2009). “Ensemble empirical mode decomposition: noise-assisted data analysis method.” Advances Adaptive Data Analysis, 1(1), 1–41. doi:10.1142/S1793536909000047 .

title: Empirical Mode Decomposition of the Activity Rhythm — circadian.emd
code: ts <- seq(as.POSIXct("2024-01-01", tz = "UTC"), by = 600, length.out = 6 * 144) th <- as.numeric(difftime(ts, ts[1], units = "hours")) circadian.emd(100 + 60 * cos(2 * pi * th / 24), ts, epoch_length = 600) #> Empirical Mode Decomposition #>  #>   IMFs: 2   reconstruction error: 7.11e-15 #>  IMF period_h var_share #>    1    22.15     0.932 #>    2    48.00     0.068 #>  #>   Circadian IMF: 1 (period 22.15 h) #>

what: Functional Linear Model of the 24-Hour Activity Profile — circadian.flm
title: Functional Linear Model of the 24-Hour Activity Profile — circadian.flm
text: Fits averaged 24-hour activity profile periodic basis expansion (Fourier default, B-spline alternative) weighted least squares, giving smooth functional form daily activity pattern. single-component cosinor one-harmonic special case; adding harmonics fits non-sinusoidal shape real rest-activity profile. follows functional form Wang et al. (2011), implemented actigraphy pyActigraphy.

title: Functional Linear Model of the 24-Hour Activity Profile — circadian.flm
code: circadian.flm(   counts,   timestamps,   basis = c("fourier", "bspline"),   n_harmonics = 4,   nbasis = 9,   spline_order = 4,   period = 24,   wear_time = NULL,   min_valid_hours = 10,   weights = c("n", "none"),   n_eval = 1440 )

title: Functional Linear Model of the 24-Hour Activity Profile — circadian.flm
text: counts Numeric activity vector. timestamps POSIXct timestamps, one per value. basis Basis type: "fourier" (default) "bspline". n_harmonics Number Fourier harmonics (default 4, giving 9-term expansion Wang et al. (2011)). nbasis Number B-spline basis functions (default 9). spline_order B-spline order (default 4, cubic). period Profile period hours (default 24). wear_time Optional logical wear-time mask. min_valid_hours Minimum valid hours per day (default 10). weights Profile weighting: "n" (square root per-hour observation count, default, matching cosinor.analysis) "none" (plain least squares, matching pyActigraphy). n_eval Length dense within-day evaluation grid (default 1440).

title: Functional Linear Model of the 24-Hour Activity Profile — circadian.flm
text: object class actiRhythm_flm: fitted coefficients, smooth daily curve (smooth_curve), fitted profile, per-harmonic amplitudes acrophases (harmonics, Fourier ), peak trough, fit statistics (r_squared, aic, f_statistic, p_value). function never errors; insufficient data returns structure r_squared NA.

title: Functional Linear Model of the 24-Hour Activity Profile — circadian.flm
text: Wang J, Xian H, Licis , Deych E, Ding J, McLeland J, Toedebusch C, Li T, Duntley S, Shannon W (2011). “Measuring impact apnea obesity circadian activity patterns using functional linear modeling actigraphy data.” Journal Circadian Rhythms, 9, 11. doi:10.1186/1740-3391-9-11 . Ramsay JO, Silverman BW (2005). Functional Data Analysis, 2nd edition. Springer. doi:10.1007/b98888 . Hammad G, Reyt M, Beliy N, Baillet M, Deantoni M, Lesoinne , Muto V, Schmidt C (2021). “pyActigraphy: open-source python package actigraphy data visualization analysis.” PLOS Computational Biology, 17(10), e1009514. doi:10.1371/journal.pcbi.1009514 .

title: Functional Linear Model of the 24-Hour Activity Profile — circadian.flm
code: ts <- seq(as.POSIXct("2024-01-01", tz = "UTC"), by = 60, length.out = 4 * 1440) h  <- as.numeric(format(ts, "%H")) + as.numeric(format(ts, "%M")) / 60 counts <- 100 + 60 * cos(2 * pi * (h - 14) / 24) + 25 * cos(2 * pi * (h - 6) / 12) circadian.flm(counts, ts) #> Functional Linear Model (24-hour activity profile) #>  #>   Basis:        fourier (order 4, 9 terms) #>   Period:       24 hours #>   Days / profile points: 4 / 24 #>  #>   Model fit: #>     R-squared:   1.0000 #>     AIC:         -1371.37 #>     F-statistic: 13059180580519173999266600266066.00 (p = <2e-16) #>  #>   Dominant harmonic: H1, amplitude 59.83, acrophase 14.01 h #>  #>   Peak 165.0 at 16.5 h; trough 20.5 at 0.8 h #>  #>   Reference: Wang et al. (2011) #>

title: Multi-resolution Interdaily Stability (IS) — circadian.is.multiscale
text: Computes interdaily stability several within-day bin resolutions. classically reported 60-min bins, finer bins (30/15 min) capture higher-frequency day--day regularity. bin width, = (N * sum_h (xbar_h - xbar)^2) / (p * sum_i (x_i - xbar)^2) p bins-per-day, xbar_h mean bin--day h, N total number bins.

title: Multi-resolution Interdaily Stability (IS) — circadian.is.multiscale
code: circadian.is.multiscale(counts, timestamps, bin_minutes = c(60, 30, 15))

title: Multi-resolution Interdaily Stability (IS) — circadian.is.multiscale
text: counts Numeric vector epoch-level activity. timestamps POSIXct timestamps (one per epoch). bin_minutes Integer vector bin widths minutes (default c(60, 30, 15)).

what: Estimate Endogenous Circadian Period via the Lomb-Scargle Periodogram — circadian.period
title: Estimate Endogenous Circadian Period via the Lomb-Scargle Periodogram — circadian.period
text: Computes Lomb-Scargle periodogram activity counts series sampled supplied timestamps returns period (tau, hours) strongest spectral peak within search window [, ]. Lomb-Scargle method (Lomb 1976; Scargle 1982) least-squares-equivalent spectral estimator unevenly sampled time series therefore appropriate actigraphy data containing gaps, FFT accommodate.

title: Estimate Endogenous Circadian Period via the Lomb-Scargle Periodogram — circadian.period
code: circadian.period(counts, timestamps, from = 18, to = 30, ofac = 4)

title: Estimate Endogenous Circadian Period via the Lomb-Scargle Periodogram — circadian.period
text: counts Numeric vector activity counts (minute-level recommended). NA values (e.g. non-wear epochs) dropped together timestamps estimation. timestamps POSIXct vector (anything coercible .numeric) epoch timestamps, length counts. Internally converted hours elapsed since first timestamp. Numeric. Lower bound period search window, hours (default 18). Numeric. Upper bound period search window, hours (default 30). ofac Integer oversampling factor controlling period-grid resolution. Higher values give finer period grid precise peak location cost computation (default 4).

title: Estimate Endogenous Circadian Period via the Lomb-Scargle Periodogram — circadian.period
text: named list elements: tau Numeric. Period (hours) strongest Lomb-Scargle peak [, ], .e. estimated endogenous circadian period. NA_real_ data insufficient. peak_power Numeric. Normalized power peak (Lomb-Scargle peak statistic). NA_real_ insufficient. p_value Numeric. P-value peak null hypothesis Gaussian noise (Baluev 2008 analytic false-alarm probability). NA_real_ insufficient. oversampling ofac oversampling factor used. n_used Integer. Number non-NA observations actually passed periodogram (NA_integer_ run). span_days Numeric. Total recording span days (max minus min timestamp), used >= 2-day guard. scanned Numeric vector trial periods (hours) full Lomb-Scargle spectrum (numeric(0) run). power Numeric vector standard-normalized Lomb-Scargle power, aligned scanned (numeric(0) run). edge case (points, short span, degenerate input, internal numerical failure) function returns structure tau, peak_power p_value set NA; never throws.

title: Estimate Endogenous Circadian Period via the Lomb-Scargle Periodogram — circadian.period
text: Processing steps: Timestamps converted hours since first sample (t_hours = (.numeric(timestamps) - min) / 3600). Pairs missing count missing/non-finite time dropped. Two guards applied estimate never based insufficient data: recording must span least 2 days (otherwise 18-30 h period resolved) least 10 non-NA observations must remain. standard-normalized Lomb-Scargle periodogram evaluated period window. strongest peak gives period (hours), Baluev (2008) analytic false-alarm probability gives p-value. Lomb-Scargle periodogram chosen specifically actigraphy series rarely gap-free: FFT assumes uniform sampling, whereas Lomb-Scargle fits sinusoids least squares trial frequency unbiased irregular sampling.

title: Estimate Endogenous Circadian Period via the Lomb-Scargle Periodogram — circadian.period
text: Lomb NR (1976). “Least-squares frequency analysis unequally spaced data.” Astrophysics Space Science, 39(2), 447–462. doi:10.1007/BF00648343 . Scargle JD (1982). “Studies astronomical time series analysis. II. Statistical aspects spectral analysis unevenly spaced data.” Astrophysical Journal, 263, 835–853. doi:10.1086/160554 . Ruf T (1999). “Lomb-Scargle periodogram biological rhythm research: analysis incomplete unequally spaced time-series.” Biological Rhythm Research, 30(2), 178–201. doi:10.1076/brhm.30.2.178.1422 . Refinetti R, Cornelissen G, Halberg F (2007). “Procedures numerical analysis circadian rhythms.” Biological Rhythm Research, 38(4), 275–325. doi:10.1080/09291010600903692 .

title: Estimate Endogenous Circadian Period via the Lomb-Scargle Periodogram — circadian.period
code: # \donttest{ # Seven days of minute-level data with a 24 h rhythm t_hours <- seq(0, 7 * 24 - 1/60, by = 1/60) ts <- as.POSIXct("2024-01-01 00:00:00") + t_hours * 3600 counts <- 100 + 80 * cos(2 * pi * (t_hours - 8) / 24) + rnorm(length(t_hours), 0, 5) circadian.period(counts, ts)$tau   # about 24.0 #> [1] 23.99762 # }

title: Circadian Rhythm Analysis — circadian.rhythm
text: counts Numeric vector activity counts (minute-level recommended accuracy) timestamps POSIXct vector epoch timestamps sleep_state Optional character vector sleep states ("S" "W") SRI calculation wear_time Optional logical vector indicating wear time (TRUE = worn) min_valid_hours Numeric. Valid-day criterion (GGIR includedaycrit): minimum wear hours day count. Applied wear_time given; default 10. Set 0/NULL disable. epoch_length Numeric. Epoch length seconds (default: 60) calculate_sri Logical. Calculate Sleep Regularity Index? (default: TRUE sleep_state provided) use_cpp Logical. Use C++ backend faster computation? (default: TRUE)

title: Circadian Rhythm Analysis — circadian.rhythm
text: List class 'actiRhythm_circadian' containing: L5, L5_start, L5_start_hour Least active 5-hour average timing (sliding window) M10, M10_start, M10_start_hour active 10-hour average timing (sliding window) L1, M1 Least/active 1-hour additional granularity RA Relative amplitude: (M10-L5)/(M10+L5), range 0-1 Interdaily stability: day--day consistency (0-1, higher=stable) IV Intradaily variability: within-day fragmentation (0 sine wave, near 2 noise) phi First-order autocorrelation 1-hour lag SRI Sleep Regularity Index (-100 100, higher=regular) onset_timing_variability Mean circular SD daily L5/M10 onset times (day--day phase variability). published Fischer/Roenneberg CPD. hourly_profile Mean activity hour day daily_metrics Per-day L5, M10, RA values

title: Circadian Rhythm Analysis — circadian.rhythm
text: Non-Parametric Metrics (van Someren et al., 1999): L5/M10 use minute-level sliding window, hourly aggregation, timing resolved minute rather hour. L5 = Average activity least active 5 consecutive hours. Uses sliding window across minute-level data precise timing. M10 = Average activity active 10 consecutive hours. RA = Relative amplitude = (M10 - L5) / (M10 + L5). Ranges 0 1; higher values mean stronger day-night amplitude. = Interdaily stability. Measures coupling rhythm stable zeitgebers. Range 0 (Gaussian noise) 1 (perfect stability). IV = Intradaily variability. Measures rhythm fragmentation. 0 perfect sine wave, 2 Gaussian noise, can exceed 2 ultradian rhythms. Sleep Regularity Index (Phillips et al., 2017): SRI = probability sleep/wake state two time points 24 hours apart. Range -100 +100, 100 indicating perfect regularity.

title: Circadian Rhythm Analysis — circadian.rhythm
code: # \donttest{ counts <- agd.counts(read.agd(example_agd())) #> AGD file tables: awakenings, capsense, crouterEpoch, crouterMinute, data, filterCategory, filters, logDiaryTimes, logEventHistory, logEventType, proximity, settings, sleep, sqlite_sequence, wtvBouts  #> Epochs loaded: 9919  #> Sleep periods found: 4  #> Awakenings found: 41  #> Wear time bouts: 5  #> Capsense samples: 9912  result <- circadian.rhythm(counts$axis1, counts$timestamp) print(result) #>  #> Circadian Rhythm Analysis #>  #> Data Summary #>   Days analyzed:            8 #>   Valid circadian days:     6 #>   Epoch length:             60 seconds #>  #> Non-Parametric Metrics (IS/IV: Witting et al. 1990; RA/L5/M10: van Someren et al. 1999) #>   L5 (least active 5h):     6.10 counts/min, onset 01:28 #>   M10 (most active 10h):    602.93 counts/min, onset 12:02 #>   L1 (least active 1h):     3.10 counts/min, onset 02:35 #>   M1 (most active 1h):      1175.51 counts/min, onset 17:43 #>   Relative Amplitude (RA):  0.9800 (range 0-1, higher=stronger rhythm) #>   Interdaily Stability (IS): 0.2279 (range 0-1, higher=more consistent) #>   Intradaily Variability (IV): 1.0008 (near 0 = sine, near 2 = noise) #>   Phi (autocorrelation):    0.4151 (higher=more predictable) #>  #> Sleep-Based & Variability Metrics #>   Sleep Regularity Index:   Not calculated (requires sleep_state input) #>   Onset timing variability: 2.23 hours #>   L5 timing variability:    0.77 hours (circular SD) #>   M10 timing variability:   3.70 hours (circular SD) #>  #> References: Witting (1990), van Someren (1999) #>  # }

what: Singular Spectrum Analysis of an Activity Series — circadian.ssa
title: Singular Spectrum Analysis of an Activity Series — circadian.ssa
text: Decomposes activity-count series additive components (trend, circadian component, ultradian components, noise) Basic Singular Spectrum Analysis: embed series Hankel trajectory matrix, take singular value decomposition, group resulting elementary series, reconstruct group diagonal averaging. pyActigraphy implements model-free decomposition actigraphy.

title: Singular Spectrum Analysis of an Activity Series — circadian.ssa
code: circadian.ssa(   counts,   timestamps,   window_hours = 24,   n_components = 10,   groups = NULL,   w_components = NULL,   period_range = c(20, 28),   detrend = FALSE )

title: Singular Spectrum Analysis of an Activity Series — circadian.ssa
text: counts Numeric activity vector. timestamps POSIXct timestamps, one per value. window_hours Embedding window length hours (default 24). window epochs L = round(window_hours * 3600 / epoch_seconds). n_components Number leading elementary components reconstruct keep (default 10). groups Optional named list 1-based component indices, e.g. list(trend = 1, circadian = 2:3). NULL grouping chosen automatically (component 1 trend; circadian pair largest component whose period falls period_range). w_components Number leading components used w-correlation matrix (default min(n_components, 10)). period_range Two-element period window hours used identify circadian component (default c(20, 28)). detrend TRUE, trend component removed reconstructed.

title: Singular Spectrum Analysis of an Activity Series — circadian.ssa
text: object class actiRhythm_ssa: singular values partial variances, reconstructed component series (trend, circadian, ultradian), w-correlation matrix, circadian fundamental_period, share variance circadian component carries. function never errors; insufficient data returns structure insufficient = TRUE.

title: Singular Spectrum Analysis of an Activity Series — circadian.ssa
text: Singular Spectrum Analysis long minute-level series builds large trajectory matrix; multi-day recordings, resampling coarser epoch (example 10 30 minutes) calling keeps decomposition fast.

title: Singular Spectrum Analysis of an Activity Series — circadian.ssa
text: Golyandina N, Zhigljavsky (2013). “Singular Spectrum Analysis Time Series.” SpringerBriefs Statistics. doi:10.1007/978-3-642-34913-3 . Vautard R, Yiou P, Ghil M (1992). “Singular-spectrum analysis: toolkit short, noisy chaotic signals.” Physica D: Nonlinear Phenomena, 58(1-4), 95–126. doi:10.1016/0167-2789(92)90103-T . Hammad G, Reyt M, Beliy N, Baillet M, Deantoni M, Lesoinne , Muto V, Schmidt C (2021). “pyActigraphy: open-source python package actigraphy data visualization analysis.” PLOS Computational Biology, 17(10), e1009514. doi:10.1371/journal.pcbi.1009514 .

title: Singular Spectrum Analysis of an Activity Series — circadian.ssa
code: ts <- seq(as.POSIXct("2024-01-01", tz = "UTC"), by = 1800, length.out = 7 * 48) h  <- as.numeric(format(ts, "%H")) + as.numeric(format(ts, "%M")) / 60 counts <- 50 + 0.5 * seq_along(ts) + 60 * cos(2 * pi * (h - 14) / 24) ssa <- circadian.ssa(counts, ts) ssa #> Singular Spectrum Analysis (Basic SSA) #>  #>   Window length L:    48 epochs (24.0 h) #>   Series length n:    336 (K = 289, span 7.0 days) #>   Components kept:     4 of rank 4 #>  #>   Variance explained (leading components): #>     ET1   lambda = 0.9171  (cumulative 0.9171) #>     ET2   lambda = 0.0418  (cumulative 0.9589) #>     ET3   lambda = 0.0410  (cumulative 0.9999) #>     ET4   lambda = 0.0001  (cumulative 1.0000) #>     ET5   lambda = 0.0000  (cumulative 1.0000) #>  #>   Grouping: #>     Trend:      components 1 #>     Circadian:  components 2, 3 (8.3% of variance) #>     Fundamental period: 23.93 h #>  #>   Reference: Golyandina and Zhigljavsky (2013) #>

what: Continuous Wavelet Transform of the Activity Rhythm — circadian.wavelet
title: Continuous Wavelet Transform of the Activity Rhythm — circadian.wavelet
text: Runs Morlet continuous wavelet transform activity series returns time-frequency power surface across circadian ultradian periods dominant-period--time track (Torrence Compo 1998; Leise 2013). Unlike sliding chi-square spectrogram, localizes period drift every time point, lengthening fragmenting rhythm shows directly.

title: Continuous Wavelet Transform of the Activity Rhythm — circadian.wavelet
code: circadian.wavelet(counts, timestamps, dj = 1/12, omega0 = 6, epoch_length = 60)

title: Continuous Wavelet Transform of the Activity Rhythm — circadian.wavelet
text: counts Numeric activity vector (coarse epoch, e.g. 10-minute bins, recommended speed; see example). timestamps POSIXct timestamps, one per value. dj Scale resolution voices per octave step (default 1/12). omega0 Morlet central frequency (default 6). epoch_length Epoch length seconds (default 60).

title: Continuous Wavelet Transform of the Activity Rhythm — circadian.wavelet
text: object class actiRhythm_wavelet: period grid (hours), time-averaged global power spectrum, per-time dominant period, overall peak period, power matrix, cone influence (coi_period_h, largest reliable period time, in_coi mask edge-affected cells). global spectrum dominant period computed outside cone edge effects bias . significant logical matrix flags cells whose power exceeds 95\ confidence level AR(1) red-noise background (per-scale threshold sig_power lag-1 autocorrelation phi), separates real rhythm background. Never errors.

title: Continuous Wavelet Transform of the Activity Rhythm — circadian.wavelet
text: Torrence C, Compo GP (1998). “practical guide wavelet analysis.” Bulletin American Meteorological Society, 79(1), 61–78. doi:10.1175/1520-0477(1998)079<0061:APGTWA>2.0.CO;2 . Leise TL (2013). “Wavelet analysis circadian ultradian behavioral rhythms.” Journal Circadian Rhythms, 11, 5. doi:10.1186/1740-3391-11-5 .

title: Continuous Wavelet Transform of the Activity Rhythm — circadian.wavelet
code: ts <- seq(as.POSIXct("2024-01-01", tz = "UTC"), by = 600, length.out = 6 * 144) th <- as.numeric(difftime(ts, ts[1], units = "hours")) circadian.wavelet(100 + 80 * cos(2 * pi * th / 24), ts, epoch_length = 600) #> Continuous Wavelet Transform (Morlet) #>  #>   Periods scanned: 0.3 to 47.9 h (87 scales) #>   Peak global period: 23.95 h #>   Dominant period: median 23.95 h (IQR 12.68-23.95) #>   Cone of influence: 81% of cells edge-reliable #>   Significant power: 7.2% of reliable cells (95% vs AR(1) red noise, phi=0.99) #>

what: Export a Full Circadian Analysis to an Excel Workbook — circadian.workbook
title: Export a Full Circadian Analysis to an Excel Workbook — circadian.workbook
text: Runs complete actiRhythm circadian analysis one activity time series writes multi-sheet .xlsx workbook: one-row Summary every metric (nonparametric /IV/RA/L5/M10, cosinor, rhythmicity F-test, Lomb-Scargle period bootstrap confidence interval, chi-square periodogram, DFA, multifractal DFA, multiscale entropy, rest-activity state transitions, , sleep supplied, Sleep Regularity Index, social jet lag LIDS), plus detail sheets hourly profile, periodograms, fluctuation multifractal spectra, transition curves, LIDS fits, data dictionary.

title: Export a Full Circadian Analysis to an Excel Workbook — circadian.workbook
code: circadian.workbook(   activity,   timestamps,   file = NULL,   sleep_state = NULL,   sleep_periods = NULL,   wear_time = NULL,   epoch_length = 60,   include_period_ci = TRUE,   n_boot = 200 )

title: Export a Full Circadian Analysis to an Excel Workbook — circadian.workbook
text: activity Numeric activity vector. timestamps POSIXct timestamps, one per value. file Output .xlsx path; NULL workbook object returned without writing. sleep_state Optional per-epoch sleep/wake vector (turns SRI). sleep_periods Optional sleep-period data frame in_bed_time/out_bed_time (turns social jet lag LIDS). wear_time Optional logical wear-time mask. epoch_length Epoch length seconds (default 60). include_period_ci Whether bootstrap period CI (default TRUE). n_boot Bootstrap replicates period CI (default 200).

title: Export a Full Circadian Analysis to an Excel Workbook — circadian.workbook
text: openxlsx workbook object, invisibly.

title: Export a Full Circadian Analysis to an Excel Workbook — circadian.workbook
code: # \donttest{ ts <- seq(as.POSIXct("2024-01-01", tz = "UTC"), by = 60, length.out = 2 * 1440) h  <- as.numeric(format(ts, "%H")) act <- pmax(0, 100 + 80 * cos(2 * pi * (h - 14) / 24) + rnorm(length(ts), 0, 20)) circadian.workbook(act, ts, file = tempfile(fileext = ".xlsx"), include_period_ci = FALSE) # }

title: Composite Phase Deviation (Fischer & Roenneberg, 2016) — composite.phase.deviation
text: Combines, day, deviation phase marker individual's mean phase (precision) reference phase (accuracy) CPD = mean(sqrt(precision^2 + accuracy^2)). external reference phase supplied, accuracy taken relative individual's mean phase (accuracy = 0), CPD reduces mean absolute phase deviation - still valid, published measure phase instability, distinct circular SD reported onset_timing_variability.

title: Composite Phase Deviation (Fischer & Roenneberg, 2016) — composite.phase.deviation
code: composite.phase.deviation(onset_hours, reference_phase = NULL)

title: Composite Phase Deviation (Fischer & Roenneberg, 2016) — composite.phase.deviation
text: onset_hours Numeric vector daily phase-marker onset times decimal hours (e.g. daily L5 onsets). reference_phase Optional reference phase decimal hours (e.g. scheduled/expected time, group mean). Default NULL uses individual's mean phase (accuracy term = 0).

title: Composite Phase Deviation (Fischer & Roenneberg, 2016) — composite.phase.deviation
text: List CPD, precision (mean absolute deviation mean phase, hours), accuracy (mean absolute deviation reference, hours), reference_phase, n_days.

title: Composite Phase Deviation (Fischer & Roenneberg, 2016) — composite.phase.deviation
text: Fischer D, Vetter C, Roenneberg T (2016). “novel method visualise quantify circadian misalignment.” Scientific Reports, 6, 38601. doi:10.1038/srep38601 .

title: Consensus Rhythmicity Across Methods — consensus.rhythmicity
text: Combines rhythmicity verdicts several independent tests one call: cosinor zero-amplitude F-test (rhythmicity.test), Bingham confidence ellipse (cosinor.confidence.ellipse), Lomb-Scargle Baluev false-alarm probability (circadian.period), chi-square (Sokolove-Bushell) periodogram (chi.sq.periodogram). available p-values pooled Fisher's method, majority vote across methods also reported.

title: Consensus Rhythmicity Across Methods — consensus.rhythmicity
code: consensus.rhythmicity(   counts,   timestamps,   period = 24,   alpha = 0.05,   wear_time = NULL )

title: Consensus Rhythmicity Across Methods — consensus.rhythmicity
text: counts Numeric activity vector. timestamps POSIXct timestamps, one per value. period Rhythm period hours cosinor tests (default 24). alpha Significance level (default 0.05). wear_time Optional logical wear-time mask cosinor fit.

title: Consensus Rhythmicity Across Methods — consensus.rhythmicity
text: object class actiRhythm_consensus: Fisher-combined p-value consensus call, vote count, per-method tests data frame, agreement fraction.

title: Consensus Rhythmicity Across Methods — consensus.rhythmicity
text: Fisher RA (1925). Statistical Methods Research Workers. Oliver Boyd, Edinburgh.

title: Consensus Rhythmicity Across Methods — consensus.rhythmicity
code: ts <- seq(as.POSIXct("2024-01-01", tz = "UTC"), by = 60, length.out = 3 * 1440) h <- as.numeric(format(ts, "%H")) counts <- pmax(0, 100 + 80 * cos(2 * pi * (h - 14) / 24)) consensus.rhythmicity(counts, ts) #> Consensus Rhythmicity (multi-method) #>  #>   cosinor F-test         p=<2e-16  rhythmic #>   Bingham ellipse                  no #>   Lomb-Scargle FAP       p=<2e-16  rhythmic #>   chi-square periodogram p=<2e-16  rhythmic #>  #>   Votes:        3 / 4 methods #>   Fisher p:     <2e-16 #>   Consensus:    RHYTHMIC (alpha = 0.05)

what: Cosinor Analysis for Circadian Rhythm — cosinor.analysis
title: Cosinor Analysis for Circadian Rhythm — cosinor.analysis
text: Fits single-component cosinor model activity data returns parametric circadian parameters.

title: Cosinor Analysis for Circadian Rhythm — cosinor.analysis
code: cosinor.analysis(   counts,   timestamps,   period = 24,   wear_time = NULL,   min_valid_hours = 10,   transform = c("none", "log1p") )

title: Cosinor Analysis for Circadian Rhythm — cosinor.analysis
text: counts Numeric vector activity counts timestamps POSIXct vector timestamps period Period hours (default: 24 circadian rhythm) wear_time Optional logical vector indicating wear time min_valid_hours Numeric. Valid-day criterion (GGIR includedaycrit): minimum wear hours day count; default 10, 0/NULL disable. transform Input transform: "none" (default) "log1p", GGIR's log(ENMO_mg + 1) pre-transform raw acceleration metrics (g-unit input scaled mg first).

title: Cosinor Analysis for Circadian Rhythm — cosinor.analysis
text: List class 'actiRhythm_cosinor' containing: mesor Rhythm-adjusted mean (MESOR) amplitude Half peak--trough difference acrophase Time peak activity (hours, 0-24) acrophase_time Acrophase HH:MM string r_squared Goodness fit (coefficient determination) f_statistic F-statistic model significance p_value P-value model significance

title: Cosinor Analysis for Circadian Rhythm — cosinor.analysis
text: single-component cosinor model fits: $$Y(t) = M + \cdot cos(2\pi t / T + \phi)$$ : M = mesor (rhythm-adjusted mean) = amplitude (half peak--trough difference) T = period (typically 24 hours) φ = acrophase (phase angle, converted time) model fit using linear least squares linearized form: Y(t) = M + β₁·cos(2πt/T) + β₂·sin(2πt/T)

title: Cosinor Analysis for Circadian Rhythm — cosinor.analysis
text: Nelson W, Tong YL, Lee JK, Halberg F (1979). “Methods cosinor-rhythmometry.” Chronobiologia, 6(4), 305–323. Cornelissen G (2014). “Cosinor-based rhythmometry.” Theoretical Biology Medical Modelling, 11, 16. doi:10.1186/1742-4682-11-16 .

title: Cosinor Analysis for Circadian Rhythm — cosinor.analysis
code: # \donttest{ counts <- agd.counts(read.agd(example_agd())) #> AGD file tables: awakenings, capsense, crouterEpoch, crouterMinute, data, filterCategory, filters, logDiaryTimes, logEventHistory, logEventType, proximity, settings, sleep, sqlite_sequence, wtvBouts  #> Epochs loaded: 9919  #> Sleep periods found: 4  #> Awakenings found: 41  #> Wear time bouts: 5  #> Capsense samples: 9912  result <- cosinor.analysis(counts$axis1, counts$timestamp) print(result) #> Cosinor Analysis Results #>  #> Period:     24 hours #> N obs:      9919 (8.0 days) #>  #> Parameters: #>   MESOR:      327.25 (rhythm-adjusted mean) #>   Amplitude:  295.63 (half peak-to-trough) #>   Acrophase:  16:55 (16.92 h, time of peak) #>  #> Model Fit: #>   R-squared:  0.4371 #>   F-statistic: 8.15 #>   P-value:    2.4e-03 # }

what: Anti-Logistic (Extended) Cosinor Analysis (Marler et al. 2006) — cosinor.antilogistic
title: Anti-Logistic (Extended) Cosinor Analysis (Marler et al. 2006) — cosinor.antilogistic
text: Fits sigmoidally transformed cosine ("anti-logistic" extended cosinor) model Marler et al. (2006) averaged 24-hour activity profile. extended model relaxes symmetric shape ordinary cosinor adding two shape parameters: alpha sets relative width active versus rest phase, beta sets steepness rest--active transitions. model ActCR::ActExtendCosinor reproduces parameter estimates numerically.

title: Anti-Logistic (Extended) Cosinor Analysis (Marler et al. 2006) — cosinor.antilogistic
code: cosinor.antilogistic(counts, timestamps, period = 24)

title: Anti-Logistic (Extended) Cosinor Analysis (Marler et al. 2006) — cosinor.antilogistic
text: counts Numeric vector activity counts (one value per epoch). timestamps POSIXct vector timestamps, length counts. period Numeric period rhythm hours (default 24).

title: Anti-Logistic (Extended) Cosinor Analysis (Marler et al. 2006) — cosinor.antilogistic
text: list class "actiRhythm_cosinor_ext" containing: minimum Lower asymptote fitted curve (rest-phase level). amplitude Vertical span sigmoidal transition (amp); peak level minimum + amplitude. alpha Width-asymmetry parameter \([-1, 1]\). beta Steepness parameter (\(\ge 0\)). acrophase Time peak activity clock hours (acrotime). acrotime Alias acrophase (ActCR naming). UpMesor Clock time rest--active (rising) transition, \(-\arccos(\alpha) / (2\pi/T) + acrotime\). DownMesor Clock time active--rest (falling) transition, \(\arccos(\alpha) / (2\pi/T) + acrotime\). MESOR Midline statistic minimum + amplitude / 2. F_pseudo Pseudo-F improvement extended fit ordinary cosinor: \(((RSS_{cos} - RSS_{ext})/2)/(RSS_{ext}/(n-5))\). rss_cosinor Residual sum squares ordinary cosinor. rss_extended Residual sum squares extended cosinor. converged Logical; whether nonlinear fit converged. period Period used (hours). n_profile_hours Number profile hours used fit. non-convergence insufficient data numeric parameters returned NA converged = FALSE.

title: Anti-Logistic (Extended) Cosinor Analysis (Marler et al. 2006) — cosinor.antilogistic
text: model fitted averaged profile $$f(t) = minimum + amplitude \cdot expit(\beta (cos(2\pi (t - acrotime)/T) - \alpha))$$ \(expit(z) = 1/(1+e^{-z})\). ActCR/Marler parameterization, amplitude raw multiplier logistic transform (renormalized \(expit(\beta(1-\alpha))\)). closely related "normalized" Marler form, \(f(t) = min + amp \cdot expit(\beta(cos(\cdot) - \alpha)) / expit(\beta(1-\alpha))\), rescales amplitude peak equals exactly minimum + amplitude; two forms describe curve shape (alpha, beta, acrotime) parameters identical. normalized peak level reported peak. Starting values taken ordinary cosinor fit (minimum = max(mesor - amp, 0), amplitude = 2 * amp, alpha = 0, beta = 2, acrotime = ordinary acrophase). nonlinear least-squares problem solved stats::optim() using box-constrained L-BFGS-B method (bounds lower = c(0, 0, -1, 0, -3), upper = c(Inf, Inf, 1, Inf, 27), matching ActCR), extra package dependency required.

title: Anti-Logistic (Extended) Cosinor Analysis (Marler et al. 2006) — cosinor.antilogistic
text: Marler MR, Gehrman P, Martin JL, Ancoli-Israel S (2006). “sigmoidally transformed cosine curve: mathematical model circadian rhythms symmetric non-sinusoidal shapes.” Statistics Medicine, 25(22), 3893–3904. doi:10.1002/sim.2466 . Wang J, Xian H, Di J, Zipunnikov V (2021). ActCR: Extract Circadian Rhythms Metrics Actigraphy Data. R package.

title: Anti-Logistic (Extended) Cosinor Analysis (Marler et al. 2006) — cosinor.antilogistic
code: # \donttest{ ts <- seq(as.POSIXct("2024-01-01 00:00:00"), by = 60, length.out = 1440 * 3) hour <- as.numeric(format(ts, "%H")) + as.numeric(format(ts, "%M")) / 60 counts <- 50 + 300 * plogis(4 * (cos((hour - 14) * 2 * pi / 24) - 0.2)) fit <- cosinor.antilogistic(counts, ts) print(fit) #> $minimum #> [1] 49.62602 #>  #> $amplitude #> [1] 301.3645 #>  #> $alpha #> [1] 0.2014903 #>  #> $beta #> [1] 3.903463 #>  #> $acrophase #> [1] 14.00833 #>  #> $acrotime #> [1] 14.00833 #>  #> $peak #> [1] 338.2087 #>  #> $UpMesor #> [1] 8.783275 #>  #> $DownMesor #> [1] 19.23339 #>  #> $MESOR #> [1] 200.3083 #>  #> $F_pseudo #> [1] 632333.8 #>  #> $rss_cosinor #> [1] 13776.46 #>  #> $rss_extended #> [1] 0.2069704 #>  #> $converged #> [1] TRUE #>  #> $period #> [1] 24 #>  #> $n_profile_hours #> [1] 24 #>  #> attr(,"class") #> [1] "actiRhythm_cosinor_ext" # }

what: Compare Cosinor Rhythms Between Two Groups — cosinor.compare
title: Compare Cosinor Rhythms Between Two Groups — cosinor.compare
text: Tests whether rest-activity rhythm differs two groups. Fits subject's cosinor engine cosinor.analysis. overall multivariate test (Hotelling's \(T^2\) joint MESOR, cosine, sine parameters, Bingham et al. (1982) population-cosinor comparison) gives one omnibus p-value whether rhythms differ , accounting correlation cosine sine components. per-parameter two-sample (Welch) t-tests break result MESOR, amplitude, acrophase. acrophase test circular-aware, unwrapping per-subject acrophases common circular mean.

title: Compare Cosinor Rhythms Between Two Groups — cosinor.compare
code: cosinor.compare(   activity,   timestamps,   subject,   group,   period = 24,   level = 0.95,   min_valid_hours = 12 )

title: Compare Cosinor Rhythms Between Two Groups — cosinor.compare
text: activity Numeric activity vector subjects stacked. timestamps POSIXct timestamps, one per value. subject Subject identifier, one per value. group Group identifier (exactly two levels), one per value. period Rhythm period hours (default 24). level Confidence level difference CIs (default 0.95). min_valid_hours Minimum profile hours subject included.

title: Compare Cosinor Rhythms Between Two Groups — cosinor.compare
text: object class actiRhythm_cosinor_compare: joint list (omnibus Hotelling \(T^2\), F statistic, degrees freedom p-value), tests data frame (one row per parameter, group's estimate, difference, t statistic, degrees freedom, p-value CI), per-subject coefficients.

title: Compare Cosinor Rhythms Between Two Groups — cosinor.compare
text: Bingham C, Arbogast B, Cornelissen Guillaume G, Lee JK, Halberg F (1982). “Inferential statistical methods estimating comparing cosinor parameters.” Chronobiologia, 9(4), 397–439.

title: Compare Cosinor Rhythms Between Two Groups — cosinor.compare
code: set.seed(1); hrs <- 0:23 act <- ts <- subj <- grp <- NULL for (g in c("A", "B")) for (i in 1:5) {   acro <- if (g == "A") 8 else 11   y <- 100 + 40 * cos(2 * pi * (hrs - acro) / 24) + rnorm(24, 0, 4)   act <- c(act, y); subj <- c(subj, rep(paste0(g, i), 24)); grp <- c(grp, rep(g, 24))   ts <- c(ts, as.POSIXct("2024-01-01", tz = "UTC") + hrs * 3600) } cosinor.compare(act, as.POSIXct(ts, tz = "UTC", origin = "1970-01-01"), subj, grp) #> Cosinor Comparison Between Two Groups #>  #>   Groups:  A (n=5)  vs  B (n=5) #>   Period:  24 h #>  #>   Joint (Bingham/Hotelling T2):  F(3,6) = 572.83   p = 9.2e-08 #>  #>   mesor     diff = +0.70   t(5.6) = 1.31   p = 0.243 #>   amplitude diff = -0.22   t(7.8) = -0.34   p = 0.743 #>   acrophase diff = -2.92   t(6.8) = -39.92   p = 2.77e-09

what: Joint Amplitude-Acrophase Confidence Ellipse (Bingham et al. 1982) — cosinor.confidence.ellipse
title: Joint Amplitude-Acrophase Confidence Ellipse (Bingham et al. 1982) — cosinor.confidence.ellipse
text: Computes joint confidence region cosinor amplitude acrophase, following Bingham et al. (1982). region ellipse \((\beta_1, \beta_2)\) (cosine/sine coefficient) plane. ellipse excludes origin, population amplitude differs significantly zero rhythm detected.

title: Joint Amplitude-Acrophase Confidence Ellipse (Bingham et al. 1982) — cosinor.confidence.ellipse
code: cosinor.confidence.ellipse(cosinor_result, level = 0.95, n_points = 200)

title: Joint Amplitude-Acrophase Confidence Ellipse (Bingham et al. 1982) — cosinor.confidence.ellipse
text: cosinor_result list returned cosinor.analysis() (class "actiRhythm_cosinor"). must contain amplitude, acrophase (clock hours), se_amplitude, period n_profile_hours. object additionally carries explicit vcov_beta (2x2 covariance \((\beta_1, \beta_2)\)) /beta1/beta2, used directly. level Confidence level region (default 0.95). n_points Number points used trace ellipse boundary (default 200).

title: Joint Amplitude-Acrophase Confidence Ellipse (Bingham et al. 1982) — cosinor.confidence.ellipse
text: list class "actiRhythm_cosinor_ellipse" containing: ellipse Data frame columns x (\(\beta_1\)) y (\(\beta_2\)) giving ellipse boundary vertices. center Numeric vector c(beta1, beta2) point estimate. excludes_origin Logical; TRUE ellipse excludes (0, 0) (.e. rhythm detected requested level). rhythm_detected Alias excludes_origin. distance_stat Mahalanobis-type statistic origin relative fitted ellipse. critical_value Threshold \(2 F_{2, df, level}\) statistic compared . level Confidence level used. input missing required fields degenerate, boundary returned NA excludes_origin = NA.

title: Joint Amplitude-Acrophase Confidence Ellipse (Bingham et al. 1982) — cosinor.confidence.ellipse
text: cosinor model \(Y(t) = M + \beta_1 cos(\omega t) + \beta_2 sin(\omega t)\), \(100(1-\alpha)\%\) joint confidence region \((\beta_1, \beta_2)\) set points \(b\) satisfying $$(b - \hat b)^\top \Sigma^{-1} (b - \hat b) \le 2 F_{2, df, 1-\alpha}$$ \(\Sigma\) covariance estimated coefficients \(df\) residual degrees freedom. averaged-profile cosinor design used cosinor.analysis() orthogonal cosine sine columns, \(\Sigma\) (nearly) diagonal equal variances \(\sigma^2\); case se_amplitude (delta method) equals \(\sigma\), \(\Sigma\) reconstructed supplied explicitly. point estimate recovered amplitude acrophase \(\beta_1 = cos(\phi)\), \(\beta_2 = sin(\phi)\) \(\phi = acrophase \cdot 2\pi / T\).

title: Joint Amplitude-Acrophase Confidence Ellipse (Bingham et al. 1982) — cosinor.confidence.ellipse
text: Bingham C, Arbogast B, Cornelissen Guillaume G, Lee JK, Halberg F (1982). “Inferential statistical methods estimating comparing cosinor parameters.” Chronobiologia, 9(4), 397–439.

title: Joint Amplitude-Acrophase Confidence Ellipse (Bingham et al. 1982) — cosinor.confidence.ellipse
code: # \donttest{ ts <- seq(as.POSIXct("2024-01-01", tz = "UTC"), by = 60, length.out = 1440 * 3) counts <- 100 + 80 * cos(2 * pi * (as.numeric(format(ts, "%H")) - 14) / 24) cos <- cosinor.analysis(counts, ts) cosinor.confidence.ellipse(cos)$rhythm_detected #> [1] NA # }

what: Multi-Component Cosinor Analysis with Harmonics — cosinor.extended

title: Multi-Component Cosinor Analysis with Harmonics — cosinor.extended
code: cosinor.extended(   counts,   timestamps,   harmonics = c(24, 12),   wear_time = NULL,   min_valid_hours = 10 )

title: Calculate Interdaily Stability (IS) and Intradaily Variability (IV) — .calculate.IS.IV
text: Formula (Witting et al., 1990): = (n * sum((Xh - Xmean)^2)) / (p * sum((Xi - Xmean)^2)) : n = total number hourly data points p = number hours per day (24) Xh = mean activity hour h across days Xmean = overall mean activity IV Formula (Witting et al., 1990): IV = (n * sum((Xi - Xi-1)^2)) / ((n-1) * sum((Xi - Xmean)^2))
