title: Detrended Fluctuation Analysis (DFA) — fractal.dfa
text: Computes scaling exponent alpha activity time series using Detrended Fluctuation Analysis (Peng et al., 1994). DFA quantifies long-range temporal correlations: alpha approximately 0.5 indicates uncorrelated (white) noise, alpha approximately 1.0 indicates 1/f (pink) noise, alpha approximately 1.5 indicates Brownian (random-walk / brown) noise. Healthy human activity fluctuations typically show alpha 0.9 1.0 range, reductions reported aging Alzheimer's disease (Hu et al., 2009).

title: Detrended Fluctuation Analysis (DFA) — fractal.dfa
code: fractal.dfa(x, scale_min = 4, scale_max = NULL, breakpoint_min = 90)

title: Detrended Fluctuation Analysis (DFA) — fractal.dfa
text: x Numeric vector activity counts (minute-level recommended). Internally analyzed longest continuous non-NA segment. scale_min Integer. Smallest window size (box length) samples. Must >= 4 line can detrended residual degrees freedom. Default 4. scale_max Integer NULL. Largest window size samples. NULL (default) set floor(N / 4) N length analyzed segment, ensuring least four windows largest scale. breakpoint_min Numeric. Window-size boundary (samples / minutes minute-level data) separating short-timescale exponent alpha1 (scales < breakpoint_min) long-timescale exponent alpha2 (scales >= breakpoint_min). Default 90.

title: Detrended Fluctuation Analysis (DFA) — fractal.dfa
text: list class "actiRhythm_dfa" containing: alpha Overall scaling exponent: slope lm(log10(F) ~ log10(n)) across scales. alpha1 Short-timescale exponent (scales < breakpoint_min). NA fewer two qualifying scales. alpha2 Long-timescale exponent (scales >= breakpoint_min). NA fewer two qualifying scales. scales Integer vector window sizes n used. fluctuations Numeric vector fluctuation magnitudes F(n) corresponding scales. n_used Length analyzed (longest non-NA) segment. breakpoint_min breakpoint value used. unusable series (short, NA, constant) numeric scaling outputs returned NA structure (never error).

title: Detrended Fluctuation Analysis (DFA) — fractal.dfa
text: Algorithm (integrated, non-overlapping, linear DFA): Extract longest continuous non-NA segment x. Integrate mean-centred signal: y = cumsum(x - mean(x)). window size n (log-spaced scale_min scale_max), split y floor(N / n) non-overlapping windows length n, fit remove least-squares line within window, pool residuals. fluctuation F(n) = sqrt(mean(residuals^2)) pooled residuals. scaling exponent slope log10(F(n)) regressed log10(n). Window sizes unique integers chosen base-10 log grid, gives approximately even spacing log-log fit matches convention used reference implementations nonlinearTseries.

title: Detrended Fluctuation Analysis (DFA) — fractal.dfa
code: # \donttest{ set.seed(1) # White noise -> alpha near 0.5 fractal.dfa(rnorm(10000))$alpha #> [1] 0.4953119 # Brown noise -> alpha near 1.5 fractal.dfa(cumsum(rnorm(10000)))$alpha #> [1] 1.494906 # }

title: Hilbert-Huang Instantaneous Phase and Frequency — hilbert.huang
text: Huang NE, Shen Z, Long SR, Wu MC, Shih HH, Zheng Q, Yen NC, Tung CC, Liu HH (1998). “empirical mode decomposition Hilbert spectrum nonlinear non-stationary time series analysis.” Proceedings Royal Society , 454(1971), 903–995. doi:10.1098/rspa.1998.0193 .

title: Hilbert-Huang Instantaneous Phase and Frequency — hilbert.huang
code: ts <- seq(as.POSIXct("2024-01-01", tz = "UTC"), by = 600, length.out = 6 * 144) th <- as.numeric(difftime(ts, ts[1], units = "hours")) e <- circadian.emd(100 + 60 * cos(2 * pi * th / 24), ts, epoch_length = 600) hilbert.huang(e) #> Hilbert-Huang Instantaneous Dynamics #>  #>   Instantaneous period: 24.85 h (SD 3.60) #>   Instantaneous amplitude: 58.8 (CV 0.25) #>   Time in 20-28 h band: 76% #>

title: Multifractal Detrended Fluctuation Analysis (MF-DFA) — mfdfa
text: Generalizes detrended fluctuation analysis (fractal.dfa) spectrum moment orders q (Kantelhardt et al. 2002). flat generalized Hurst exponent h(q) indicates monofractal signal; decreasing h(q) wide multifractal spectrum indicate multifractality. h(2) equals standard DFA scaling exponent.

title: Multifractal Detrended Fluctuation Analysis (MF-DFA) — mfdfa
code: mfdfa(   x,   scale_min = 8L,   scale_max = NULL,   q_values = seq(-5, 5, by = 0.5),   both_ends = TRUE )

title: Multifractal Detrended Fluctuation Analysis (MF-DFA) — mfdfa
text: x Numeric series (e.g. activity counts); longest gap-free run analysed. scale_min Smallest window size samples (default 8). scale_max Largest window size (default floor(N/4)). q_values Moment orders evaluate (default seq(-5, 5, 0.5)). both_ends TRUE (default) windows taken ends tail profile discarded; FALSE reproduces start-convention fractal.dfa.

title: Multifractal Detrended Fluctuation Analysis (MF-DFA) — mfdfa
text: object class actiRhythm_mfdfa: list q_values, h_q (generalized Hurst exponent), tau_q (mass exponent), alpha/f_alpha (multifractal spectrum), alpha_dfa (= h(2)) spectrum width. Returns NA structure insufficient data; never errors.

title: Multifractal Detrended Fluctuation Analysis (MF-DFA) — mfdfa
text: Kantelhardt JW, Zschiegner SA, Koscielny-Bunde E, Havlin S, Bunde , Stanley (2002). “Multifractal detrended fluctuation analysis nonstationary time series.” Physica : Statistical Mechanics Applications, 316(1-4), 87–114. doi:10.1016/S0378-4371(02)01383-3 .

title: Multifractal Detrended Fluctuation Analysis (MF-DFA) — mfdfa
code: set.seed(1) mfdfa(stats::rnorm(4096))$alpha_dfa   # near 0.5 for white noise #> [1] 0.4772896

title: Multiscale Sample Entropy (MSE) — multiscale.entropy
text: Computes Multiscale Sample Entropy (Costa et al., 2002, 2005), applies Sample Entropy (Richman & Moorman, 2000) coarse-grained versions activity time series across range temporal scales. MSE distinguishes genuinely complex signals (whose entropy sustained increases across scales, e.g. 1/f-like physiological signals) uncorrelated random signals (whose entropy falls monotonically scale).

title: Multiscale Sample Entropy (MSE) — multiscale.entropy
code: multiscale.entropy(x, scales = 1:20, m = 2, r = 0.15)

title: Multiscale Sample Entropy (MSE) — multiscale.entropy
text: x Numeric vector activity counts (minute-level recommended). Internally analyzed longest continuous non-NA segment. scales Integer vector coarse-graining scale factors tau. Default 1:20. m Integer embedding dimension (template length) Sample Entropy. Default 2. r Numeric tolerance fraction standard deviation ORIGINAL (scale-1) series. Chebyshev matching radius used every scale r * sd(x_original), following standard MSE convention holding r fixed absolute units across scales. Default 0.15.

title: Multiscale Sample Entropy (MSE) — multiscale.entropy
text: list class "actiRhythm_mse" containing: mse Numeric vector SampEn values, one per requested scale (NA coarse-grained series short yields matches). scales Integer vector scale factors used. area Sum mse scales (complexity index), sum(mse, na.rm = TRUE). slope Slope lm(mse ~ scales) non-NA points (negative => entropy declines scale, typical noise). r_absolute absolute tolerance r * sd(x_original) used. n_used Length analyzed (longest non-NA) segment. unusable series entropy vector NA structure (never error).

title: Multiscale Sample Entropy (MSE) — multiscale.entropy
text: scale tau series coarse-grained non-overlapping means length tau, Sample Entropy computed coarse-grained series embedding dimension m FIXED absolute tolerance r * sd(x_original). Sample Entropy negative natural log conditional probability two sub-sequences matching m points (within tolerance, Chebyshev distance, self-matches excluded) also match m + 1 points. implementation fully self-contained (base R / stats ).

what: Confidence Interval for the Endogenous Circadian Period — period.ci
title: Confidence Interval for the Endogenous Circadian Period — period.ci
text: Attaches bootstrap confidence interval (standard error) Lomb-Scargle period estimate circadian.period. activity strongly autocorrelated, ..d. resample destroy rhythm give invalid (-narrow) interval, circular moving-block bootstrap cosinor residuals used: rhythm fitted point estimate held fixed blocks residuals resampled, periodogram peak re-located replicate (sub-grid parabolic refinement), interval taken quantiles replicate periods.

title: Confidence Interval for the Endogenous Circadian Period — period.ci
code: period.ci(   counts,   timestamps,   from = 18,   to = 30,   ofac = 4,   n_boot = 200,   block_hours = 24,   level = 0.95,   seed = NULL )

title: Confidence Interval for the Endogenous Circadian Period — period.ci
text: counts Numeric activity vector; NA dropped times. timestamps POSIXct (numeric-coercible) timestamps, length. , Period search window hours (default 18, 30). ofac Integer oversampling factor period grid (default 4). n_boot Number bootstrap replicates (default 200). block_hours Moving-block length hours (default 24). level Confidence level (default 0.95). seed Optional integer seed passed set.seed reproducible bootstrap draws.

title: Confidence Interval for the Endogenous Circadian Period — period.ci
text: object class actiRhythm_period_ci: list tau (point estimate, hours), ci_lower/ci_upper, se, level, number valid replicates.

title: Confidence Interval for the Endogenous Circadian Period — period.ci
text: Kunsch HR (1989). “jackknife bootstrap general stationary observations.” Annals Statistics, 17(3), 1217–1241. doi:10.1214/aos/1176347265 . Politis DN, Romano JP (1992). “circular block-resampling procedure stationary data.” LePage R, Billard L (eds.), Exploring Limits Bootstrap, 263–270. Wiley, New York.

title: Confidence Interval for the Endogenous Circadian Period — period.ci
code: # \donttest{ t_hours <- seq(0, 5 * 24 - 1/60, by = 1/60) ts <- as.POSIXct("2024-01-01", tz = "UTC") + t_hours * 3600 counts <- 100 + 80 * cos(2 * pi * (t_hours - 8) / 24) + rnorm(length(t_hours), 0, 20) period.ci(counts, ts, n_boot = 50, seed = 1) #> Circadian Period with Bootstrap Confidence Interval #>  #>   tau:      24.103 h #>   95% CI:   [23.980, 24.064] h #>   SE:       0.022 h #>   Method:   circular block residual bootstrap (50/50 valid reps) # }

title: Phase Concentration Tests — phase.concentration

title: Phase Concentration Tests — phase.concentration
text: Fisher NI (1993). Statistical Analysis Circular Data. Cambridge University Press. doi:10.1017/CBO9780511564345 . Landler L, Ruxton GD, Malkemper EP (2019). “Hermans-Rasson test powerful alternative Rayleigh test circular statistics biology.” BMC Ecology, 19, 30. doi:10.1186/s12898-019-0246-8 .

title: Phase Concentration Tests — phase.concentration
code: set.seed(1) onsets <- 23 + stats::rnorm(10, 0, 0.5)      # tightly clustered near 23:00 phase.concentration(onsets %% 24) #> Phase Concentration Tests #>  #>   n days:          10 #>   Mean direction:  23.07 h    R: 0.995 #>   Rayleigh:        Z = 9.91, p = 0.0000 #>   Hermans-Rasson:  T = 42.0, p = 0.0005 #>

title: Plot the Detrended Fluctuation Analysis (DFA) Scaling Relationship — plot_dfa

title: Plot the Extended (Marler) Cosinor Fit on the 24-Hour Activity Profile — plot_extended_cosinor
text: profile mean activity clock-hour bin averaged across recorded days (bin centres hour + 0.5), matching profile cosinor.analysis() cosinor.antilogistic() fit internally. fitted curves evaluated dense 0-24 h grid: Extended (Marler): \(f(t) = minimum + amplitude \cdot     expit(\beta (cos(2\pi (t - acrotime)/T) - \alpha))\), using minimum, amplitude, alpha, beta acrotime returned cosinor.antilogistic(). Ordinary: \(M + cos(2\pi (t - acrophase)/T)\), using mesor, amplitude acrophase cosinor.analysis(). subtitle reports extended-fit MESOR, amplitude, alpha width-asymmetry beta steepness fit converged.

title: Plot the Extended (Marler) Cosinor Fit on the 24-Hour Activity Profile — plot_extended_cosinor
text: Marler MR, Gehrman P, Martin JL, Ancoli-Israel S (2006). “sigmoidally transformed cosine curve: mathematical model circadian rhythms symmetric non-sinusoidal shapes.” Statistics Medicine, 25(22), 3893–3904. doi:10.1002/sim.2466 . Cornelissen G (2014). “Cosinor-based rhythmometry.” Theoretical Biology Medical Modelling, 11, 16. doi:10.1186/1742-4682-11-16 .

title: Plot the Extended (Marler) Cosinor Fit on the 24-Hour Activity Profile — plot_extended_cosinor
code: # \donttest{ ts <- seq(as.POSIXct("2024-01-01 00:00:00"), by = 60, length.out = 1440 * 7) hour <- as.numeric(format(ts, "%H")) + as.numeric(format(ts, "%M")) / 60 counts <- 50 + 300 * plogis(4 * (cos((hour - 14) * 2 * pi / 24) - 0.2)) +   rnorm(length(ts), 0, 10) plot_extended_cosinor(counts, ts)  # }

text: Computes full Lomb-Scargle periodogram activity series period search window plots spectral power period (hours). dominant endogenous period (tau) estimated circadian.period marked labelled vertical line, dashed reference line drawn 24 hours deviation biological clock exactly one solar day visible.

title: Plot the Lomb-Scargle Periodogram with the Endogenous Period — plot_periodogram
code: plot_periodogram(counts, timestamps, from = 18, to = 30, ofac = 4)

title: Plot the Lomb-Scargle Periodogram with the Endogenous Period — plot_periodogram
text: counts Numeric vector activity counts (minute-level recommended). NA values (e.g. non-wear epochs) dropped together timestamps estimation. timestamps POSIXct vector (anything coercible .numeric) epoch timestamps, length counts. Numeric. Lower bound period search window, hours (default 18). Numeric. Upper bound period search window, hours (default 30). ofac Integer oversampling factor passed lomb::lsp. Higher values give finer period grid (default 4).

title: Plot the Lomb-Scargle Periodogram with the Endogenous Period — plot_periodogram
text: ggplot object: Lomb-Scargle power (y) versus period hours (x), peak period 24 h reference annotated. insufficient data (-NA, constant, fewer 2 days span) ggplot carrying centred "Insufficient data periodogram" annotation returned instead; function never errors.

title: Plot the Lomb-Scargle Periodogram with the Endogenous Period — plot_periodogram
text: full spectrum obtained lomb::lsp(x, times, , , type = "period", ofac, plot = FALSE), whose $scanned component holds trial periods (hours) $power corresponding normalized Lomb-Scargle power. peak period tau p_value come circadian.period highlighted peak exactly value reported analytic function. Lomb-Scargle periodogram least-squares spectral estimator unevenly sampled series therefore appropriate gappy actigraphy data, FFT accommodate.

title: Plot the Lomb-Scargle Periodogram with the Endogenous Period — plot_periodogram
text: Lomb NR (1976). “Least-squares frequency analysis unequally spaced data.” Astrophysics Space Science, 39(2), 447–462. doi:10.1007/BF00648343 . Scargle JD (1982). “Studies astronomical time series analysis. II. Statistical aspects spectral analysis unevenly spaced data.” Astrophysical Journal, 263, 835–853. doi:10.1086/160554 . Ruf T (1999). “Lomb-Scargle periodogram biological rhythm research: analysis incomplete unequally spaced time-series.” Biological Rhythm Research, 30(2), 178–201. doi:10.1076/brhm.30.2.178.1422 .

title: Plot the Lomb-Scargle Periodogram with the Endogenous Period — plot_periodogram
code: # \donttest{ t_hours <- seq(0, 7 * 24 - 1 / 60, by = 1 / 60) ts <- as.POSIXct("2024-01-01 00:00:00") + t_hours * 3600 counts <- 100 + 80 * cos(2 * pi * (t_hours - 8) / 24) +   rnorm(length(t_hours), 0, 5) plot_periodogram(counts, ts)  # }

title: Population-Mean Cosinor (Bingham) — population.cosinor
text: Pools single-subject cosinor fits group-mean rhythm confidence intervals, following Bingham et al. (1982). Fits subject's averaged 24-hour profile weighted-least-squares engine cosinor.analysis, averages linearized cos/sin coefficients across subjects, returns group MESOR, amplitude, acrophase Bingham confidence intervals.

title: Population-Mean Cosinor (Bingham) — population.cosinor
code: population.cosinor(   activity,   timestamps,   subject,   group = NULL,   period = 24,   level = 0.95,   min_valid_hours = 12 )

title: Population-Mean Cosinor (Bingham) — population.cosinor
text: activity Numeric activity vector subjects stacked together. timestamps POSIXct timestamps, one per value. subject Subject identifier, one per value. group Optional group identifier, one per value; supplied population cosinor returned group. period Rhythm period hours (default 24). level Confidence level (default 0.95). min_valid_hours Minimum profile hours subject included (default 12).

title: Population-Mean Cosinor (Bingham) — population.cosinor
text: object class actiRhythm_population_cosinor (group MESOR, amplitude, acrophase Bingham CIs conf_interval_valid flag), named list (class actiRhythm_population_cosinor_list) group supplied.

title: Population-Mean Cosinor (Bingham) — population.cosinor
text: Bingham C, Arbogast B, Cornelissen Guillaume G, Lee JK, Halberg F (1982). “Inferential statistical methods estimating comparing cosinor parameters.” Chronobiologia, 9(4), 397–439.

title: Population-Mean Cosinor (Bingham) — population.cosinor
code: set.seed(1) hrs <- 0:23 act <- ts <- subj <- NULL for (i in 1:6) {   y <- 100 + 40 * cos(2 * pi * (hrs - (8 + i / 3)) / 24) + rnorm(24, 0, 4)   act <- c(act, y); subj <- c(subj, rep(paste0("S", i), 24))   ts <- c(ts, as.POSIXct("2024-01-01", tz = "UTC") + hrs * 3600) } population.cosinor(act, as.POSIXct(ts, tz = "UTC", origin = "1970-01-01"), subj) #> Population-Mean Cosinor (Bingham) #>  #>   Subjects:   6 #>   Period:     24 h #>   MESOR:      100.17  [99.32, 101.01] #>   Amplitude:  39.68  [38.54, 40.81] #>   Acrophase:  9.71 h  [9.07, 10.36]

what: Raw-Acceleration Epoch Metrics (ENMO, MAD, z-Angle) — raw.metrics
title: Raw-Acceleration Epoch Metrics (ENMO, MAD, z-Angle) — raw.metrics
text: Reads raw accelerometer file returns per-epoch raw activity posture metrics, gravity-preserving signals counts represent: ENMO (Euclidean Norm Minus One, raw activity metric), MAD (Mean Amplitude Deviation), z-angle (arm/posture angle). Auto-calibration (van Hees 2014) applied first default. Requires relevant raw reader (read.gt3x .gt3x, GGIRread .cwa/.bin).

title: Raw-Acceleration Epoch Metrics (ENMO, MAD, z-Angle) — raw.metrics
code: raw.metrics(   x,   device = "auto",   epoch = 60,   metrics = c("ENMO", "MAD", "anglez"),   calibrate = TRUE,   tz = "UTC" )

title: Raw-Acceleration Epoch Metrics (ENMO, MAD, z-Angle) — raw.metrics
text: x path raw file (.gt3x, .cwa, .bin) raw data frame x/y/z columns g fs attribute (e.g. example_raw device). device One "auto", "gt3x", "axivity", "geneactiv" (default "auto", inferred extension; used x file path). epoch Epoch length seconds (default 60). metrics metrics return; "ENMO", "MAD", "anglez" (default three). calibrate Apply van Hees auto-calibration first (default TRUE). tz Time zone timestamps (default "UTC").

title: Raw-Acceleration Epoch Metrics (ENMO, MAD, z-Angle) — raw.metrics
text: data frame time requested metrics (ENMO MAD mg, anglez degrees), one row per epoch. calibration result attached "calibration" attribute.

title: Raw-Acceleration Epoch Metrics (ENMO, MAD, z-Angle) — raw.metrics
text: van Hees VT, Gorzelniak L, Dean Leon EC, Eder M, Pias M, Taherian S, Ekelund U, Renstrom F, Franks PW, Horsch , Brage S (2013). “Separating movement gravity components acceleration signal implications assessment human daily physical activity.” PLoS ONE, 8(4), e61691. doi:10.1371/journal.pone.0061691 . Vaha-Ypya H, Vasankari T, Husu P, Suni J, Sievanen H (2015). “universal, accurate intensity-based classification different physical activities using raw data accelerometer.” Clinical Physiology Functional Imaging, 35(1), 64–70. doi:10.1111/cpf.12127 .

title: Residual Circadian Spectrum — residual.spectrum
text: Removes fitted cosinor mean estimates spectrum left, residual circadian spectrum, integrating frequency bands. measures ultradian noise structure cosinor fit (Krafty et al. 2019), two recordings 24-hour rhythm different residual fragmentation told apart.

title: Residual Circadian Spectrum — residual.spectrum
code: residual.spectrum(   counts,   timestamps,   period = 24,   bands = list(ultradian = c(2, 8), high_freq = c(0.5, 2)) )

title: Residual Circadian Spectrum — residual.spectrum
text: counts Numeric activity vector. timestamps POSIXct timestamps, one per value. period Cosinor period hours (default 24). bands Named list c(low_hours, high_hours) period bands residual power; defaults ultradian (2-8 h) high-frequency (<2 h).

title: Residual Circadian Spectrum — residual.spectrum
text: object class actiRhythm_rcs: residual variance, per-band power table, spectrum. Never errors.

title: Residual Circadian Spectrum — residual.spectrum
text: Krafty RT, Fu H, Graves JL, Bruce SA, Hall MH, Smagula SF (2019). “Measuring variability rest-activity rhythms actigraphy application characterizing symptoms depression.” Statistics Biosciences, 11, 314–333. doi:10.1007/s12561-018-09230-2 .

title: Residual Circadian Spectrum — residual.spectrum
code: ts <- seq(as.POSIXct("2024-01-01", tz = "UTC"), by = 600, length.out = 6 * 144) th <- as.numeric(difftime(ts, ts[1], units = "hours")) residual.spectrum(100 + 80 * cos(2 * pi * th / 24) + 20 * sin(2 * pi * th / 4), ts,                   period = 24) #> Residual Circadian Spectrum #>  #>   Residual variance: 200.2 #>  #>       band low_h high_h power fraction #>  ultradian   2.0      8 86400        1 #>  high_freq   0.5      2     0        0 #>

title: Rest-Activity Bout Fragmentation — rest.activity.fragmentation
text: Summarizes broken rest-activity rhythm , per-epoch rest/active state: mean median rest active bout durations, number state transitions, transitions per day. add bout-length view fragmentation transition probabilities state.transitions (kRA/kAR) (Lim et al. 2011). covers rest-activity-rhythm fragmentation ; omits sedentary-behaviour bout distribution (Gini, power law, hazard), physical-activity-epidemiology concern, circadian one.

title: Rest-Activity Bout Fragmentation — rest.activity.fragmentation
code: rest.activity.fragmentation(state, timestamps, epoch_length = 60)

title: Rest-Activity Bout Fragmentation — rest.activity.fragmentation
text: state Per-epoch state: logical vector (TRUE = active) character vector "R"/"S"/"sleep"/"rest" mark rest. timestamps POSIXct timestamps, one per value. epoch_length Epoch length seconds (default 60).

title: Rest-Activity Bout Fragmentation — rest.activity.fragmentation
text: object class actiRhythm_rafrag: mean/median rest active bout durations (minutes), bout counts, transition count, transitions per day. Never errors.

title: Rest-Activity Bout Fragmentation — rest.activity.fragmentation
text: Lim ASP, Yu L, Costa MD, Leurgans SE, Buchman , Bennett DA, Saper CB (2011). “Quantification fragmentation rest-activity patterns elderly individuals using state transition analysis.” Sleep, 34(11), 1569–1581. doi:10.5665/sleep.1400 .

title: Rest-Activity Bout Fragmentation — rest.activity.fragmentation
code: ts <- seq(as.POSIXct("2024-01-01", tz = "UTC"), by = 60, length.out = 3 * 1440) h  <- as.numeric(format(ts, "%H")) rest.activity.fragmentation(h >= 7 & h < 23, ts) #> Rest-Activity Bout Fragmentation #>  #>   Active bouts: 3, mean 960 min (median 960) #>   Rest bouts:   4, mean 360 min (median 450) #>   Transitions:  6 (2.0 per day) #>

title: Crespo Rest/Activity Period Detection — rest.crespo
text: Detects consolidated rest bouts across recording Crespo algorithm (Crespo et al. 2012): two-pass rank-order (median) filter mathematical-morphology pipeline turns activity counts binary rest/activity series reads every rest bout transitions. Like rest.periods returns number bouts, naps fragmented rest included; reaches result morphological filtering rather Roenneberg-style consolidation, two differ method.

title: Crespo Rest/Activity Period Detection — rest.crespo
code: rest.crespo(   counts,   timestamps,   epoch_length = 60,   zeta = 15,   zeta_r = 30,   zeta_a = 2,   t = 0.33,   alpha = 8 * 3600,   beta = 1 * 3600 )

title: Crespo Rest/Activity Period Detection — rest.crespo
text: counts Numeric activity vector (minute epochs recommended). timestamps POSIXct timestamps, one per value. epoch_length Epoch length seconds (default 60). zeta, zeta_r, zeta_a Maximum valid consecutive-zero run, epochs, global pre-conditioning, within rest segments, within active segments (defaults 15, 30, 2). Longer zero runs treated non-wear. t Quantile counts used replace non-wear epochs (default 0.33). alpha Expected daily rest length, seconds (default 28800, 8 hours); sets threshold percentile (alpha / 24 h) minimum data required. beta Filter morphology scale, seconds (default 3600, 1 hour); sets rank-order filter window structuring-element size.

title: Crespo Rest/Activity Period Detection — rest.crespo
text: object class actiRhythm_crespo: rest_periods data frame (one row per bout, onset, offset, duration), per-epoch rest_state ("R"/""), per-bout counts. function never errors; resolvable bout returns empty rest_periods.

title: Crespo Rest/Activity Period Detection — rest.crespo
text: Crespo C, Aboy M, Fernandez JR, Mojon (2012). “Automatic identification activity-rest periods based actigraphy.” Medical & Biological Engineering & Computing, 50(4), 329–340. doi:10.1007/s11517-012-0875-y . Hammad G, Reyt M, Beliy N, Baillet M, Deantoni M, Lesoinne , Muto V, Schmidt C (2021). “pyActigraphy: open-source python package actigraphy data visualization analysis.” PLOS Computational Biology, 17(10), e1009514. doi:10.1371/journal.pcbi.1009514 .

title: Crespo Rest/Activity Period Detection — rest.crespo
code: # Two nights of rest with a daytime nap between them ts <- seq(as.POSIXct("2024-01-01", tz = "UTC"), by = 60, length.out = 3 * 1440) h  <- as.numeric(format(ts, "%H")) + as.numeric(format(ts, "%M")) / 60 day <- as.integer(format(ts, "%j")) counts <- ifelse(h >= 23 | h < 7, 5, 300) counts[h >= 14 & h < 15 & day == min(day) + 1L] <- 5 rest.crespo(counts, ts) #> Crespo Rest/Activity Periods #>  #>   Rest bouts:   3  (1.5 per day over 2 days) #>   Total rest:   23.0 h #>  #>   First bouts: #>     01-01 00:01 -> 01-01 06:59  (419 min) #>     01-01 23:00 -> 01-02 06:59  (480 min) #>     01-02 23:00 -> 01-03 06:59  (480 min) #>  #>   Reference: Crespo et al. (2012) #>

title: State-Space (Hidden Markov) Rest-Activity Model — rest.hmm
text: Fits unsupervised Gaussian hidden Markov model activity counts, inferring latent rest active states rhythm subject moves (Huang et al. 2018). threshold scorer labels epoch independently; HMM uses persistence states. decoded path gives 24-hour state-occupancy profile, probability rest across day.

title: State-Space (Hidden Markov) Rest-Activity Model — rest.hmm
code: rest.hmm(   counts,   timestamps,   states = 2L,   transform = c("sqrt", "log", "none"),   max_iter = 200L,   tol = 1e-06,   n_starts = 5L,   seed = NULL )

title: State-Space (Hidden Markov) Rest-Activity Model — rest.hmm
text: counts Numeric activity vector (coarse epoch recommended speed). timestamps POSIXct timestamps, one per value. states Number hidden states (default 2 = rest/active; 3 adds moderate state). transform Variance-stabilizing transform counts: "sqrt" (default), "log", "none". max_iter, tol EM iteration cap log-likelihood tolerance. n_starts Random restarts; highest-likelihood fit kept (default 5). seed Optional seed restarts.

title: State-Space (Hidden Markov) Rest-Activity Model — rest.hmm
text: object class actiRhythm_hmm: per-state emission means SDs, transition matrix, decoded state_path sleep_state ("S"/"W") vector, 24-hour state-occupancy profile, log-likelihood AIC/BIC. Never errors.

title: State-Space (Hidden Markov) Rest-Activity Model — rest.hmm
text: Huang Q, Cohen D, Komarzynski S, Li X, Innominato P, Levi F, Finkenstadt B (2018). “Hidden Markov models monitoring circadian rhythmicity telemetric activity data.” Journal Royal Society Interface, 15(139), 20170885. doi:10.1098/rsif.2017.0885 .

title: State-Space (Hidden Markov) Rest-Activity Model — rest.hmm
code: ts <- seq(as.POSIXct("2024-01-01", tz = "UTC"), by = 600, length.out = 6 * 144) h  <- as.numeric(format(ts, "%H")) counts <- ifelse(h >= 23 | h < 7, 2, 250) + pmax(0, stats::rnorm(length(ts), 0, 10)) rest.hmm(counts, ts) #> State-Space Rest-Activity Model (Gaussian HMM) #>  #>   States: 2   log-likelihood: -349.0   AIC: 710.1 #>  #>  state mean_transformed sd_transformed  label #>      1             2.26           1.07   rest #>      2            15.94           0.19 active #>  #>   Time at rest: 33%   persistence (rest self-transition): 0.98 #>

title: Consolidated Rest-Period Detection (Roenneberg / MASDA) — rest.periods
text: Detects consolidated rest bouts across recording Roenneberg consolidation algorithm (Munich Actimetry Sleep Detection Algorithm). epoch compared fraction 24-hour activity trend, runs threshold seed candidate rest bouts, correlation procedure grows seed consolidated bout. Unlike sleep.changepoints (one nightly bout per cycle), returns number bouts, including daytime naps fragmented polyphasic rest.

title: Consolidated Rest-Period Detection (Roenneberg / MASDA) — rest.periods
code: rest.periods(   counts,   timestamps,   epoch_length = 60,   trend_period = 86400,   min_trend_period = 43200,   threshold = 0.15,   min_seed_period = 1800,   max_test_period = 43200,   r_consec_below = 1800,   nap_max_minutes = 180 )

title: Consolidated Rest-Period Detection (Roenneberg / MASDA) — rest.periods
text: counts Numeric activity vector (minute epochs recommended). timestamps POSIXct timestamps, one per value. epoch_length Epoch length seconds (default 60). trend_period Moving-average window activity trend, seconds (default 86400, 24 hours). min_trend_period Minimum non-missing seconds required trend window (default 43200, 12 hours). threshold Fraction local trend epoch candidate rest (default 0.15). min_seed_period Minimum -threshold run seed bout, seconds (default 1800, 30 minutes). max_test_period Maximum bout length consolidation tests, seconds (default 43200, 12 hours). r_consec_below -threshold tolerance consolidation, seconds (default 1800, 30 minutes). nap_max_minutes non-main bout shorter labelled nap (default 180).

title: Consolidated Rest-Period Detection (Roenneberg / MASDA) — rest.periods
text: object class actiRhythm_roenneberg: rest_periods data frame (one row per consolidated bout, onset, offset, duration, main/nap label), per-epoch rest vector, activity trend, per-bout counts. function never errors; resolvable bout returns empty rest_periods.

title: Consolidated Rest-Period Detection (Roenneberg / MASDA) — rest.periods
text: Roenneberg T, Keller LK, Fischer D, Matera JL, Vetter C, Winnebeck EC (2015). “Human activity rest situ.” Methods Enzymology, 552, 257–283. doi:10.1016/bs.mie.2014.11.028 . Loock , Khan Sullivan , Reis C, Paiva T, Ghotbi N, Pilz LK, Biller , Molenda C, Vuori-Brodowski MT, Roenneberg T, Winnebeck EC (2021). “Validation Munich Actimetry Sleep Detection Algorithm estimating sleep-wake patterns activity recordings.” Journal Sleep Research, 30(6), e13371. doi:10.1111/jsr.13371 . Hammad G, Reyt M, Beliy N, Baillet M, Deantoni M, Lesoinne , Muto V, Schmidt C (2021). “pyActigraphy: open-source python package actigraphy data visualization analysis.” PLOS Computational Biology, 17(10), e1009514. doi:10.1371/journal.pcbi.1009514 .

title: Consolidated Rest-Period Detection (Roenneberg / MASDA) — rest.periods
code: # Three nights of rest plus one daytime nap ts <- seq(as.POSIXct("2024-01-01", tz = "UTC"), by = 60, length.out = 3 * 1440) h  <- as.numeric(format(ts, "%H")) + as.numeric(format(ts, "%M")) / 60 day <- as.integer(format(ts, "%j")) counts <- ifelse(h >= 23 | h < 7, 5, 300) counts[h >= 14 & h < 15.5 & day == min(day) + 1L] <- 5   # a nap on day 2 rest.periods(counts, ts) #> Consolidated Rest Periods (Roenneberg/MASDA) #>  #>   Bouts detected:   4  (2.0 per day over 2 days) #>   Total rest:       24.5 h #>   Main bouts / naps: 3 / 1 #>  #>   First bouts: #>     main 01-01 00:00 -> 01-01 06:59  (420 min) #>     main 01-01 23:00 -> 01-02 06:59  (480 min) #>     nap  01-02 14:00 -> 01-02 15:29  (90 min) #>  #>   Reference: Roenneberg et al. (2015); Loock et al. (2021) #>

what: Sleep-Period-Time Window from the z-Angle (HDCZA) — rest.spt
title: Sleep-Period-Time Window from the z-Angle (HDCZA) — rest.spt
text: Detects main sleep-period-time (SPT) window per day directly z-angle, sleep diary, using van Hees et al. (2018) Heuristic algorithm based Distribution Change Z-Angle (HDCZA): absolute 5-second change arm angle smoothed 5 minutes, thresholded fraction distribution, longest sustained low-change block per noon--noon day becomes SPT. raw-native: count-based equivalent.

title: Sleep-Period-Time Window from the z-Angle (HDCZA) — rest.spt
code: rest.spt(   anglez,   timestamps,   epoch_length = 5,   pct = 10,   mult = 15,   clamp = c(0.13, 0.5),   min_block = 30,   max_gap = 60,   algo = c("HDCZA", "HorAngle"),   wear = NULL )

title: Sleep-Period-Time Window from the z-Angle (HDCZA) — rest.spt
text: anglez Numeric z-angle (degrees) per epoch, e.g. raw.metrics(..., metrics = "anglez") short epoch. timestamps POSIXct timestamps, one per epoch. epoch_length Epoch length seconds (default 5, validated value). pct Percentile change distribution threshold (default 10). mult Multiplier percentile (default 15). clamp Lower/upper clamp (degrees) threshold (default c(0.13, 0.50)). min_block Minimum SPT block length minutes (default 30). max_gap Maximum movement gap bridge within SPT, minutes (default 60). algo "HDCZA" (wrist, change--angle) "HorAngle" (hip variant thresholding absolute angle 60 degrees). wear Optional logical wear mask, one per epoch (e.g. detect.nonwear.raw); non-wear epochs excluded SPT stationary, taken-device scored sleep.

title: Sleep-Period-Time Window from the z-Angle (HDCZA) — rest.spt
text: object class actiRhythm_spt: data frame one row per day (date, onset, offset, duration hours, threshold used). Never errors; returns rows day detectable window.

title: Sleep-Period-Time Window from the z-Angle (HDCZA) — rest.spt
text: van Hees VT, Sabia S, Jones SE, Wood AR, Anderson KN, Kivimaki M, Frayling TM, Pack AI, Bucan M, Trenell MI, Mazzotti DR, Gehrman PR, Singh-Manoux BA, Weedon MN (2018). “Estimating sleep parameters using accelerometer without sleep diary.” Scientific Reports, 8, 12975. doi:10.1038/s41598-018-31266-z .

title: Sleep-Period-Time Window from the z-Angle (HDCZA) — rest.spt
code: # One still night inside an active day yields one SPT window ts <- seq(as.POSIXct("2024-01-01 12:00", tz = "UTC"), by = 5, length.out = 17280) h <- as.numeric(format(ts, "%H")); night <- h >= 23 | h < 7 set.seed(1) anglez <- ifelse(night, -60, -30) + rnorm(17280, 0, ifelse(night, 0.02, 20)) rest.spt(anglez, ts, epoch_length = 5) #>         date               onset              offset duration threshold #> 1 2024-01-01 2024-01-01 23:00:05 2024-01-02 06:59:55 7.997222 0.2670371

title: Cosinor Rhythmicity Test — rhythmicity.test
text: Tests whether rhythm given period present, using Halberg zero-amplitude F-test (H0: amplitude = 0) single-component cosinor fit, reports percent rhythm (proportion variance cosinor explains, R^2). reuses cosinor.analysis single fitting engine, MESOR/amplitude/acrophase valid-day gating identical.

title: Cosinor Rhythmicity Test — rhythmicity.test
code: rhythmicity.test(   counts,   timestamps,   period = 24,   alpha = 0.05,   wear_time = NULL,   min_valid_hours = 10,   cosinor_result = NULL )

title: Cosinor Rhythmicity Test — rhythmicity.test
text: counts Numeric vector activity counts (activity measure). timestamps POSIXct timestamps, one per count. period Rhythm period hours (default 24). alpha Significance level rhythmic flag (default 0.05). wear_time Optional logical wear-time mask passed cosinor fit. min_valid_hours Minimum valid hours per day cosinor fit. cosinor_result Optional existing cosinor.analysis result test without refitting; supplied, counts/timestamps ignored.

title: Cosinor Rhythmicity Test — rhythmicity.test
text: object class actiRhythm_rhythmicity: list F statistic, numerator/denominator degrees freedom (df1, df2), p_value, percent_rhythm (r_squared), logical rhythmic flag, cosinor parameters.

title: Cosinor Rhythmicity Test — rhythmicity.test
text: Nelson W, Tong YL, Lee JK, Halberg F (1979). “Methods cosinor-rhythmometry.” Chronobiologia, 6(4), 305–323.

title: Cosinor Rhythmicity Test — rhythmicity.test
code: ts <- seq(as.POSIXct("2024-01-01", tz = "UTC"), by = 60, length.out = 1440) h <- as.numeric(format(ts, "%H")) counts <- pmax(0, 100 + 80 * cos(2 * pi * (h - 14) / 24)) rhythmicity.test(counts, ts) #> Cosinor Rhythmicity Test (Halberg zero-amplitude F-test) #>  #> H0: amplitude = 0 (no rhythm) #>   Period:         24 h #>   F(2, 21):        Inf #>   P-value:        <1e-300 #>   Percent rhythm: 100.0% (R-squared = 1.0000) #>   Rhythmic:       YES (alpha = 0.05)

what: Sustained-Inactivity-Bout Sleep Scoring from the z-Angle — sib.vanhees
title: Sustained-Inactivity-Bout Sleep Scoring from the z-Angle — sib.vanhees
text: Scores epoch sleep ("S") wake ("W") z-angle van Hees et al. (2015) sustained-inactivity-bout rule: interval arm-posture change exceeding angle_thresh degrees least time_thresh minutes sustained inactivity (sleep). output shape sleep.cole.kripke, feeds sleep.regularity.index, lids rest consumers directly. Intersect rest.spt via sleep..spt.

title: Sustained-Inactivity-Bout Sleep Scoring from the z-Angle — sib.vanhees
code: sib.vanhees(anglez, angle_thresh = 5, time_thresh = 5, epoch_length = 5)

title: Sustained-Inactivity-Bout Sleep Scoring from the z-Angle — sib.vanhees
text: anglez Numeric z-angle (degrees) per epoch. angle_thresh Posture-change threshold degrees (default 5). time_thresh Minimum sustained-inactivity duration minutes (default 5). epoch_length Epoch length seconds (default 5).

title: Sustained-Inactivity-Bout Sleep Scoring from the z-Angle — sib.vanhees
text: Character vector "S"/"W", one per epoch. day fewer 10 posture changes scored "S" (sustained inactivity, gated SPT window wear time).

title: Sustained-Inactivity-Bout Sleep Scoring from the z-Angle — sib.vanhees
text: van Hees VT, Sabia S, Anderson KN, Denton SJ, Oliver J, Catt M, Abell JG, Kivimaki M, Trenell MI, Singh-Manoux (2015). “novel, open access method assess sleep duration using wrist-worn accelerometer.” PLoS ONE, 10(11), e0142533. doi:10.1371/journal.pone.0142533 .

title: Sustained-Inactivity-Bout Sleep Scoring from the z-Angle — sib.vanhees

what: Change-Point Detection of Sleep and Wake Onsets — sleep.changepoints
title: Change-Point Detection of Sleep and Wake Onsets — sleep.changepoints
text: Locates sleep-onset wake-onset time circadian cycle cosinor-anchored change-point method CircaCP (Chen Sun 2024). fixed 24-hour cosinor fitted bound rest active span roughly, precise transition inside bound placed single change point raw counts. result per-night sleep-onset / wake-onset table. single rest-activity transition rate (state.transitions) localise timing.

title: Change-Point Detection of Sleep and Wake Onsets — sleep.changepoints
code: sleep.changepoints(   counts,   timestamps,   period = 1440,   thr = 0.2,   window_minutes = 240 )

title: Change-Point Detection of Sleep and Wake Onsets — sleep.changepoints
text: counts Numeric activity vector (minute epochs recommended). timestamps POSIXct timestamps, one per value. period Cosinor period minutes (default 1440, one day). thr Dichotomisation threshold range-scaled cosine, \([0, 1]\) (default 0.2): fitted curve thr rough active span, rough rest span. window_minutes Half-width search window, minutes, rough boundary refined change point (default 240).

title: Change-Point Detection of Sleep and Wake Onsets — sleep.changepoints
text: object class actiRhythm_changepoints: cosinor summary, changepoints data frame (time type, "sleep onset" "wake onset"), sleep_episodes data frame (sleep onset, wake onset, duration hours), mean sleep duration. function never errors; insufficient data returns structure insufficient = TRUE.

title: Change-Point Detection of Sleep and Wake Onsets — sleep.changepoints
text: Chen S, Sun X (2024). “Validating CircaCP: generic sleep-wake cycle detection algorithm unlabelled actigraphy data.” Royal Society Open Science, 11(5), 231468. doi:10.1098/rsos.231468 . Chen J, Gupta AK, Pan J (2006). “Information criterion change point problem regular models.” Sankhya: Indian Journal Statistics, 68(2), 252–282. Killick R, Fearnhead P, Eckley IA (2012). “Optimal detection changepoints linear computational cost.” Journal American Statistical Association, 107(500), 1590–1598. doi:10.1080/01621459.2012.737745 .

title: Change-Point Detection of Sleep and Wake Onsets — sleep.changepoints
code: # Five days with a clear active day / restful night ts <- seq(as.POSIXct("2024-01-01", tz = "UTC"), by = 60, length.out = 5 * 1440) h  <- as.numeric(format(ts, "%H")) counts <- ifelse(h >= 8 & h < 23, 300, 5) + pmax(0, stats::rnorm(length(ts), 0, 5)) sleep.changepoints(counts, ts) #> Change-Point Sleep/Wake Detection (CircaCP) #>  #>   Span:           5.0 days (7200 epochs) #>   Cosinor acrophase: 15.5 h #>   Change points:  10 (4 sleep episodes) #>   Mean sleep duration: 9.0 h #>  #>   First sleep episodes: #>     sleep 01-01 22:59  ->  wake 01-02 07:59  (9.0 h) #>     sleep 01-02 22:59  ->  wake 01-03 07:59  (9.0 h) #>     sleep 01-03 22:59  ->  wake 01-04 07:59  (9.0 h) #>  #>   Reference: Chen and Sun (2024) #>

title: Cole-Kripke Sleep/Wake Scoring — sleep.cole.kripke
text: Classifies epoch sleep wake activity counts Cole-Kripke algorithm (Cole et al. 1992), validated adults one-minute epochs, optional Webster rescoring rules (Webster et al. 1982). Returns per-epoch sleep_state regularity locomotor-sleep metrics read (sleep.regularity.index, lids).

title: Cole-Kripke Sleep/Wake Scoring — sleep.cole.kripke
code: sleep.cole.kripke(   counts,   apply_rescoring = TRUE,   epoch_length = 60,   na_action = c("na", "wake", "zero") )

title: Cole-Kripke Sleep/Wake Scoring — sleep.cole.kripke
text: counts Numeric vector activity counts (vertical axis). apply_rescoring Apply Webster's rescoring rules (default TRUE). epoch_length Epoch length seconds (default 60). algorithm validated 60-second epochs; lengths raise warning. na_action NA-count epochs appear output: "na" (default) emits NA, non-wear gaps read sleep; "wake" scores wake; "zero" scores zero count.

title: Cole-Kripke Sleep/Wake Scoring — sleep.cole.kripke
text: Character vector states, "S" (sleep) "W" (wake), length counts.

title: Cole-Kripke Sleep/Wake Scoring — sleep.cole.kripke
text: sleep index uses seven-epoch window (four , current epoch, two ), counts divided 100 capped 300: $$D = 0.001 (106 P_4 + 54 P_3 + 58 P_2 + 76 P_1 + 230 C + 74 N_1 + 67 N_2)$$ epoch scored sleep \(D < 1\). Webster's rescoring re-labels short sleep bouts follow surrounded sustained wake wake.

title: Cole-Kripke Sleep/Wake Scoring — sleep.cole.kripke
text: Cole RJ, Kripke DF, Gruen W, Mullaney DJ, Gillin JC (1992). “Automatic sleep/wake identification wrist activity.” Sleep, 15(5), 461–469. doi:10.1093/sleep/15.5.461 . Webster JB, Kripke DF, Messin S, Mullaney DJ, Wyborney G (1982). “activity based sleep monitor system ambulatory use.” Sleep, 5(4), 389–399. doi:10.1093/sleep/5.4.389 .

text: van Hees VT, Sabia S, Jones SE, Wood AR, Anderson KN, Kivimaki M, Frayling TM, Pack AI, Bucan M, Trenell MI, Mazzotti DR, Gehrman PR, Singh-Manoux BA, Weedon MN (2018). “Estimating sleep parameters using accelerometer without sleep diary.” Scientific Reports, 8, 12975. doi:10.1038/s41598-018-31266-z .

title: Sleep Parameters from SPT and Sustained-Inactivity Bouts — sleep.from.spt
code: ts <- seq(as.POSIXct("2024-01-01 12:00", tz = "UTC"), by = 5, length.out = 17280) h <- as.numeric(format(ts, "%H")); night <- h >= 23 | h < 7 set.seed(1) anglez <- ifelse(night, -60, -30) + rnorm(17280, 0, ifelse(night, 0.02, 20)) spt <- rest.spt(anglez, ts, epoch_length = 5) sib <- sib.vanhees(anglez, epoch_length = 5) sleep.from.spt(spt, sib, ts, epoch_length = 5) #>         date               onset              offset      tst waso efficiency #> 1 2024-01-01 2024-01-01 23:00:05 2024-01-02 06:59:55 7.998611    0          1 #>   n_awakenings           mid_sleep #> 1            0 2024-01-02 03:00:00

title: Calculate Sleep Regularity Index (SRI) - Exported Version — sleep.regularity.index
text: Phillips AJK, Clerx WM, O'Brien CS, Sano , Barger LK, Picard RW, Lockley SW, Klerman EB, Czeisler CA (2017). “Irregular sleep/wake patterns associated poorer academic performance delayed circadian sleep/wake timing.” Scientific Reports, 7(1), 3216. doi:10.1038/s41598-017-03171-4 .

code: sleep.sadeh(counts, epoch_length = 60, na_action = c("na", "wake", "zero"))

title: Sadeh Sleep/Wake Scoring — sleep.sadeh
text: counts Numeric vector activity counts (vertical axis). epoch_length Epoch length seconds (default 60). algorithm validated 60-second epochs; lengths raise warning. na_action NA-count epochs appear output: "na" (default) emits NA, non-wear gaps read sleep; "wake" scores wake; "zero" scores zero count.

text: Character vector states, "S" (sleep) "W" (wake), length counts.

title: Sadeh Sleep/Wake Scoring — sleep.sadeh
text: sleep index uses eleven-epoch window (five , current epoch, five ), counts capped 300: $$SI = 7.601 - 0.065 \cdot AVG - 1.08 \cdot NATS - 0.056 \cdot SD - 0.703 \cdot LG$$ \(AVG\) window mean, \(NATS\) number epochs counts [50, 100), \(SD\) standard deviation current five preceding epochs, \(LG = \log(\mathrm{count} + 1)\). epoch scored sleep \(SI > -4\) (threshold used validated ActiGraph implementations).

title: Sadeh Sleep/Wake Scoring — sleep.sadeh
text: Sadeh , Sharkey KM, Carskadon MA (1994). “Activity-based sleep-wake identification: empirical test methodological issues.” Sleep, 17(3), 201–207. doi:10.1093/sleep/17.3.201 .

title: Calculate Social Jet Lag — social.jet.lag
text: Difference sleep midpoint work days free days.

title: Calculate Social Jet Lag — social.jet.lag
code: social.jet.lag(sleep_periods, work_days = NULL)

title: Calculate Social Jet Lag — social.jet.lag
text: sleep_periods Data frame in_bed_time, out_bed_time columns work_days Optional. Logical vector, date vector, NULL (uses Mon-Fri default)

title: Calculate Social Jet Lag — social.jet.lag
text: List social jet lag metrics, including MSW/MSF (mid-sleep work free days), social_jet_lag_hours (MSF - MSW), sleep-debt-corrected chronotype MSFsc corrected social_jet_lag_sc_hours (SJLsc), number work/free nights.

title: Calculate Social Jet Lag — social.jet.lag
text: Social jet lag discrepancy social biological time, computed MSF - MSW (midpoint free days minus work days). MSFsc applies MCTQ sleep-debt correction (Roenneberg et al. 2012): free-day sleep exceeds work-day sleep (sleep catch-), free-day mid-sleep adjusted half excess weekly average sleep duration, SJLsc = MSFsc - MSW. based time bed (supplied -bed/-bed times), sleep onset offset. Positive values (common) indicate later sleep timing free days. Values > 1 hour associated increased health risks.

title: Calculate Social Jet Lag — social.jet.lag
text: Wittmann M, Dinich J, Merrow M, Roenneberg T (2006). “Social jetlag: misalignment biological social time.” Chronobiology International, 23(1-2), 497–509. doi:10.1080/07420520500545979 . Roenneberg T, Allebrandt KV, Merrow M, Vetter C (2012). “Social jetlag obesity.” Current Biology, 22(10), 939–943. doi:10.1016/j.cub.2012.03.038 .

what: Sleep Regularity Index (SRI) - Phillips (2017) Epoch-of-Day x Day Matrix — sri.matrix
title: Sleep Regularity Index (SRI) - Phillips (2017) Epoch-of-Day x Day Matrix — sri.matrix
text: Computes Sleep Regularity Index exactly defined Phillips et al. (2017) using full epoch--day day concordance matrix. published form SRI. Unlike single fixed 24-hour-lag comparison, every epoch binned real clock time (epoch--day) calendar date, binary sleep/wake matrix built, index average agreement clock time CONSECUTIVE calendar days, rescaled interval [-100, 100].

title: Sleep Regularity Index (SRI) - Phillips (2017) Epoch-of-Day x Day Matrix — sri.matrix
code: sri.matrix(sleep_state, timestamps, epoch_length = 60)

title: Sleep Regularity Index (SRI) - Phillips (2017) Epoch-of-Day x Day Matrix — sri.matrix
text: sleep_state Sleep/wake state per epoch. Either character vector "S" (sleep) / "W" (wake), numeric/integer/logical vector 1/TRUE = sleep 0/FALSE = wake. NA marks unscored / non-wear epochs excluded concordance count. Must length timestamps. timestamps POSIXct vector epoch start times, one per element sleep_state. Need start midnight need perfectly contiguous; alignment real clock time. epoch_length Epoch length seconds (default 60). Must divide 86400 evenly; M = 86400 / epoch_length number epochs per day.

title: Sleep Regularity Index (SRI) - Phillips (2017) Epoch-of-Day x Day Matrix — sri.matrix
text: list components: SRI Numeric Sleep Regularity Index [-100, 100] (rounded 2 dp), NA_real_ computed. n_days Integer number distinct calendar days (N) spanned. n_valid_pairs Integer number consecutive-day epoch pairs actually compared (cells non-NA). method Character constant "phillips_matrix".

title: Sleep Regularity Index (SRI) - Phillips (2017) Epoch-of-Day x Day Matrix — sri.matrix
text: Let M = 86400 / epoch_length number epochs day let N number calendar days spanned recording. epoch assigned epoch--day index (1..M) clock time (hour/minute/second) day index j (1..N) calendar date. two indices populate binary matrix s[, j] 1 = sleep 0 = wake. SRI $$SRI = -100 + \frac{200}{M (N - 1)} \sum_{=1}^{M} \sum_{j=1}^{N-1}   \mathbf{1}\{ s_{,j} = s_{,j+1} \}$$ .e. proportion clock-time epochs hold state two consecutive days, mapped linearly perfect regularity scores +100, chance-level (independent) scoring 0, perfect anti-regularity -100. Robustness gaps partial wear: consecutive-day pair (, j) -> (, j + 1) either cell NA (missing epoch, non-wear, calendar day represented data) skipped counted. sum divided actual number valid pairs rather theoretical M * (N - 1), missing data reduce statistical power bias estimate. Days aligned true clock time using .POSIXlt() (hour, minute, second give epoch--day; calendar date gives day index), result correct even recording start midnight. SRI NA_real_ returned (never error) fewer two days, valid consecutive-day pairs, usable input. return structure always list.

title: Sleep Regularity Index (SRI) - Phillips (2017) Epoch-of-Day x Day Matrix — sri.matrix
text: Phillips AJK, Clerx WM, O'Brien CS, Sano , Barger LK, Picard RW, Lockley SW, Klerman EB, Czeisler CA (2017). “Irregular sleep/wake patterns associated poorer academic performance delayed circadian sleep/wake timing.” Scientific Reports, 7(1), 3216. doi:10.1038/s41598-017-03171-4 .

title: Sleep Regularity Index (SRI) - Phillips (2017) Epoch-of-Day x Day Matrix — sri.matrix
code: # A perfectly regular sleeper: same 8h sleep block every day -> SRI near 100 ts <- seq(as.POSIXct("2024-01-01 00:00:00", tz = "UTC"),           by = 60, length.out = 1440 * 4) tod <- as.POSIXlt(ts)$hour state <- ifelse(tod < 8, "S", "W")   # asleep 00:00-08:00 every day sri.matrix(state, ts, epoch_length = 60)$SRI #> [1] 100

what: Rest-Activity State Transition Rates (kRA, kAR) — state.transitions
title: Rest-Activity State Transition Rates (kRA, kAR) — state.transitions
text: Computes rest--activity activity--rest transition rates binarized activity series, following survival-curve method used pyActigraphy (Lim et al. 2011). Thresholds series rest/active epochs, builds per-lag transition probability (hazard) bout type bout-length survival curve, takes single rate LOWESS "sustained" plateau curve.

title: Rest-Activity State Transition Rates (kRA, kAR) — state.transitions
code: state.transitions(counts, threshold = 1, frac = 0.3, iter = 0)

title: Rest-Activity State Transition Rates (kRA, kAR) — state.transitions
text: counts Numeric activity vector; NA dropped. threshold Activity level epoch "active" (default 1, .e. non-zero count). frac LOWESS smoother span sustained-region search (default 0.3). iter LOWESS robustifying iterations (default 0).

title: Rest-Activity State Transition Rates (kRA, kAR) — state.transitions
text: object class actiRhythm_transitions: list kRA/kAR (sustained rest--active / active--rest rates), pRA/pAR (overall per-epoch transition probabilities, 1 / mean bout length), bout counts, two transition curves.

title: Rest-Activity State Transition Rates (kRA, kAR) — state.transitions
text: Lim ASP, Yu L, Costa MD, Leurgans SE, Buchman , Bennett DA, Saper CB (2011). “Quantification fragmentation rest-activity patterns elderly individuals using state transition analysis.” Sleep, 34(11), 1569–1581. doi:10.5665/sleep.1400 .

title: Rest-Activity State Transition Rates (kRA, kAR) — state.transitions
code: set.seed(1) counts <- as.integer(stats::runif(5000) < 0.1) * 100 state.transitions(counts) #> Rest-Activity State Transitions #>  #>   Threshold:  >= 1 counts = active #>   kRA (rest->active): 0.1051   (471 rest bouts) #>   kAR (active->rest): 0.8747   (470 active bouts) #>   pRA / pAR:          0.1055 / 0.8752

title: Rest-Active Transition Probabilities (ASTP / SATP) — transition.probability
text: Closed-form maximum-likelihood Bayesian estimates rest--active active--rest transition probabilities, reciprocal-mean-bout-length fragmentation estimators (Danilevicz et al. 2024). transition count time risk. Unlike survival-curve rates state.transitions, come straight bout counts.

title: Rest-Active Transition Probabilities (ASTP / SATP) — transition.probability
code: transition.probability(counts, threshold = 1, lambda = 1)

title: Rest-Active Transition Probabilities (ASTP / SATP) — transition.probability
text: counts Numeric activity vector. threshold Counts epoch active (default 1). lambda Bayesian prior pseudo-count (default 1).

title: Rest-Active Transition Probabilities (ASTP / SATP) — transition.probability
text: list MLE Bayesian tp_ra (rest active) tp_ar (active rest), active bout count mean active bout length.

title: Rest-Active Transition Probabilities (ASTP / SATP) — transition.probability
text: Danilevicz IM, van Hees VT, van der Heide F, Jacob L, Landre B, Benadjaoud MA, Sabia S (2024). “Measures fragmentation rest activity patterns: mathematical properties interpretability.” BMC Medical Research Methodology, 24, 132. doi:10.1186/s12874-024-02255-w .

title: Rest-Active Transition Probabilities (ASTP / SATP) — transition.probability
code: counts <- c(rep(0, 50), rep(100, 20), rep(0, 40), rep(80, 30), rep(0, 60)) transition.probability(counts) #> $tp_ar_mle #> [1] 0.04 #>  #> $tp_ra_mle #> [1] 0.01333333 #>  #> $tp_ar_bayes #> [1] 0.05882353 #>  #> $tp_ra_bayes #> [1] 0.01986755 #>  #> $n_active_bouts #> [1] 2 #>  #> $mean_active_bout #> [1] 25 #>

title: Ultradian Wavelet Band Power — ultradian.bandpower
text: Partitions activity variance dyadic period bands undecimated (MODWT) Haar wavelet transform, isolating ultradian bands -90-minute, -4-hour, -8-hour rhythms (Percival Walden 2000; Leise 2013). Reports energy, power, fraction total variance band.

title: Ultradian Wavelet Band Power — ultradian.bandpower
code: ultradian.bandpower(   counts,   timestamps,   bands = list(`90min` = c(1, 2), `4h` = c(2, 6), `8h` = c(6, 12)),   epoch_length = 60 )

title: Ultradian Wavelet Band Power — ultradian.bandpower
text: counts Numeric activity vector (minute epochs recommended). timestamps POSIXct timestamps, one per value. bands Named list c(low_hours, high_hours) period bands (default 90 min, 4 h, 8 h). epoch_length Epoch length seconds (default 60).

title: Ultradian Wavelet Band Power — ultradian.bandpower
text: object class actiRhythm_bandpower: per-band table (energy, power, fraction) per-level wavelet variance. Never errors.

title: Ultradian Wavelet Band Power — ultradian.bandpower
text: Percival DB, Walden (2000). Wavelet Methods Time Series Analysis. Cambridge University Press. doi:10.1017/CBO9780511841040 .

title: Ultradian Wavelet Band Power — ultradian.bandpower
code: ts <- seq(as.POSIXct("2024-01-01", tz = "UTC"), by = 60, length.out = 2 * 1440) th <- as.numeric(difftime(ts, ts[1], units = "hours")) ultradian.bandpower(100 + 50 * sin(2 * pi * th / 4), ts)   # a 4-hour rhythm #> Ultradian Wavelet Band Power #>  #>   band low_h high_h  power fraction #>  90min     1      2  248.2    0.199 #>     4h     2      6 1173.8    0.939 #>     8h     6     12  435.8    0.349 #>

title: Wavelet Cone of Influence — wavelet.coi
text: Morlet cone influence (Torrence Compo 1998): largest period, hours, time point wavelet power free edge effects. Power periods curve, near start end recording, unreliable. circadian.wavelet returns curve result; helper computes directly plotting masking.

title: Wavelet Cone of Influence — wavelet.coi
code: wavelet.coi(timestamps, omega0 = 6, epoch_length = 60)

title: Wavelet Cone of Influence — wavelet.coi
text: timestamps POSIXct timestamps, one per epoch. omega0 Morlet central frequency (default 6). epoch_length Epoch length seconds (default 60).

title: Wavelet Cone of Influence — wavelet.coi
text: Numeric vector, one per epoch, maximum reliable period (hours).

title: Wavelet Cone of Influence — wavelet.coi
text: Torrence C, Compo GP (1998). “practical guide wavelet analysis.” Bulletin American Meteorological Society, 79(1), 61–78. doi:10.1175/1520-0477(1998)079<0061:APGTWA>2.0.CO;2 .

title: Wavelet Cone of Influence — wavelet.coi
code: ts <- seq(as.POSIXct("2024-01-01", tz = "UTC"), by = 600, length.out = 6 * 144) range(wavelet.coi(ts, epoch_length = 600)) #> [1] 1.217454e-06 5.247225e+01
